# Thread: Benfotiamine in Pentose Phosphate Pathway
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/benfotiamine-in-pentose-phosphate-pathway.10297/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-03-24T09:37:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/benfotiamine-in-pentose-phosphate-pathway.10297/post-117553>

The form of thiamine known as benfotiamine apparently works in part by stimulating the pentose phosphate pathway:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12882930](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12882930)

I don't see this substance mentioned in Jack's blogs. Normally benfotiamine is used to treat diabetic neuropathy, and one of the possible mechanisms for this is that it shunts potential advanced glycation end products into the PPP.

Curious if Jack or others have any opinion on use for benfotiamine.

I saw Rosedale pushing this as a substitute for dietary D-Ribose, and that just perplexed me.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-24T19:47:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/benfotiamine-in-pentose-phosphate-pathway.10297/post-117638>

Benfotiamine requires concomitant use of high dose B vitamins especially B 1. to get D ribose to work. I don't buy Rosedale's argument. Many studies have shown D Ribose gets into the cytosol and into mitochondria in the cardiac literature on heart failure cases. These cases are when cardiomyocytes are failing and the proximate cause of the AHA grade 3 and 4 heart failure cases. Mushrooms are the best B vitamin supplement out there. In T2D with neuropathy and nephropathy, high-dose thiamine and benfotiamine therapy increased transketolase expression in the kidney. This helped to increased the conversion of triosephosphates to ribose-5-phosphate. This action was shown to strongly inhibited the development of microalbuminuria in nephropathy.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-29T03:19:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/benfotiamine-in-pentose-phosphate-pathway.10297/post-162703>

Mushrooms are a B vitamin story and tied to thiamine story. Those with poor redox potential have very low thiamine and folate levels and this is classically seen in T2D, neurodegeneration, and alcoholics. It can lead to Wernicke's encephalopathy and confabulation at its nth degree. The reason is because NAD+ and NADP+ are chronically low and cells are anaerobic and are catabolic and not able to build proteins. Catabolic and anaerobic issues are synonyms for cell with very high ubiquitination levels. The more this cycle is increased the more of the PPP you need to help restore NADPH which is the major reducing element in all of biochemistry........this is how we increase our redox power. Raising NAD+ is the other key issue.........B vitamins are intermediates in this coupling and that is why mushrooms are key. They provide a lot folate and thiamin which are key in elevated ubiquitin states. The higher ubi goes up the more oxidation in cells happens their is a huge lack of electrons and a net positive charge that develops with inflammation.

lack of reduction = lack of electrons = no DHA in cell membranes and in the SCN.........= too much blue light = too much nnEMF when you think you're mitigating well. # Thread: Mummy scans
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mummy-scans.10298/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-24T13:05:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mummy-scans.10298/post-117564>

> Thanks to a new exhibition at the British Museum, the public can finally gain a glimpse into what lies beneath after eight mummies underwent CAT scans at London hospitals, proving the most detailed picture yet of our Egyptian forbears.
>
> Ancient Lives: New Discoveries, which opens on May 22, reveals that despite the separation of centuries, the Egyptians suffered from the same health issues that plague us today, including high cholesterol, fatty diets and bad toothache. ...

How one can deduce "fatty diets" from a CAT scan is a mystery to me, but never mind. This is the mainstream all over, isn't it?

Later on they tell us:

> Two of the eight mummies tested were found to have a build-up in their legs of plaque - essentially cholesterol, calcium and tissue - suggesting they suffered from heart problems.
>
> This can be caused by a rich diet high in fat, or it can be genetic.

So this is how they say they deduced "fatty diets" from the scans. So fat in the diet causes calcium to be deposited in arteries? How? ...

> Many experienced poor dental health and had multiple abscesses, which if left untreated, may have led to inflammation of the throat, asphyxiation and ultimately death.

I suppose that was caused by "fatty diets", too.

> Analysis of digestive remains suggested the Egyptians enjoyed a wide diet that included fish, a little meat, beer, bread and sugar-rich fruits, such as dates.

So here we actually get some real experimental findings -- as opposed to interpretation posing as findings. When actually looking at the digestive remains, they found among other things: "beer, bread and sugar-rich fruits, such as dates".

No chance the beer and bread and "sugar-rich fruits" could have caused the dental problems -- and the atherosclerosis?

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencet...ians-used-hospital-CAT-scans-closer-look.html](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-2587224/Secrets-ancient-mummies-tattoos-revealed-historians-used-hospital-CAT-scans-closer-look.html)

### nonchalant — 2014-03-24T18:27:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mummy-scans.10298/post-117610>

Fatty diets... a little meat.... Mike, didn't you know that they ate a lot of "vegetable oil"? ;-)

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-24T19:36:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mummy-scans.10298/post-117633>

It turns out there's a slightly better report at the Telegraph -- although much of the wording is very similar.

> Daniel Antoine, the museum’s curator of physical anthropology and curator of the coming exhibition, said: “The scans showed two of them with plaque in the leg.
>
> “That is an indication of cardiovascular disease that can lead to a heart attack or stroke. This might have been caused by lifestyle which means a diet rich in fat or it might be genetic

So the comment is traceable to the museum's curator of physical anthropology.

As I say … I wonder about that calcium. Dr. Rosedale points out:

> If you put endothelial cells, the inner lining of your arteries, in a Petri dish and expose them to leptin, they will build bone.

[http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2013...sedale-keep-the-butter-and-give-up-the-bagel/](http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2013/05/12/sam-should-i-give-up-butter-on-my-bagel-ron-rosedale-keep-the-butter-and-give-up-the-bagel/)

Rosedale's analysis seems plausible to me. It certainly seems possible to me that these Ancient Egyptians may have been leptin resistant. But then I'm not a physical anthropologist. I don't like to criticise people in their own fields, but I just don't see what "fatty diets" would have to do with the deposition of calcium in arteries, while Rosedale's explanation sounds plausible and is based on work published in _Science_ in 2002. Also the physical anthropologist seems to be pulling the "fatty diets" out the air: it's not that the've found evidential trace of the diets being "fatty" in the form of stomach remains, or cooking-pot residues or … well, anything, really.

Another snippet from the _Telegraph_:

> The scientists at the British Museum even studied bread found with the remains of the mummies to see if it had a high content of abrasive sand that might have contributed to tooth decay – it didn’t.

I'm not surprised, and I bet you're not.

You might wear your teeth down a bit with grit, but why should it make them rot? Sugar and starch again.

The Eskimo man in the bottom left has teeth that are worn down almost to the gums by grit that's settled on the fish dried in windrows that his people ate. But he has no tooth decay:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

[http://journeytoforever.org/farm\_library/price/price5.html](http://journeytoforever.org/farm_library/price/price5.html)

It depresses a little me that this stuff keeps coming round.

I found out what "Bro Science" means today. Vinnie Tortorich explained it on his podcast. Apparently, it's the sort of "facts" about human physiology that people down the gym believe, and that don't really have anything behind them but have stuck through constant repetition. I guess the belief that arterial plaque is caused by "a diet rich in fat" might be Bro Science.

# Thread: Need Suggestions - Huge Weight Gain
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2014-03-24T15:33:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117588>

After starting the Leptin RX 2+ years ago, I finally broke through 260 lbs. Mid summer 2012, I started spending a minimum of 2 hours a day outside with just shorts and tennis shoes. I saw some fantastic improvements in weight loss and hormone levels. I got down to 230, but as Fall started my weight started increasing.

Over Fall and Winter of 2012/2013 I gained about 15 lbs, but starting in late Spring it started coming back off. During Spring and Summer of 2013, I spent as much time as possible outside in the full sun with just shorts and shoes. Mid summer I started intermittent fasting (IF), eating breakfast around 10 am and dinner around 4 pm, resulting in about 17-18 hours without eating. I also started walking a minimum 30 miles a week.

The weight started flying off and by early Fall I was down to 205 lbs, the lowest weight in over 30 years. I thought I had things under control.

My doctor thought I might have prostate cancer based on positive dre. Saw a urologist who thought it was cancer. This all happened early to mid summer. The IF and walking was my attempt to starve the cancer of glucose if in fact it was cancer.

I schedule a MRI for early January. I weighed 230 lbs the morning of the MRI. So from early October when I weighed 205, I had gained 25 lbs in less than 3 months.

The MRI was negative for cancer and I allowed myself a couple days of eating whatever I wanted. I couldn't stop. Within a month, I had gained another 25 lbs. So now I have gone from 205 in October to 255 in February, 50 lbs in 4 months.

I can't seem to go more than 3 or 4 days before I have overwhelming cravings. This is the same problem I had before I started the Leptin Rx. When my body is working right, I don't have cravings, so something is definitely not right. I have been drinking spring water for over a year, I eat a ketogenic diet, I don't know of any increase in emf, most of the supplements I take I have been taking for a long period of time, etc.

I am stumped. Every nice day I get out in the sun, but we sure haven't had many nice days so far this year. Maybe all I need is a week of sun. The only other thing I can think to do is increase fats.

I don't understand all this pathway stuff, but it seems the path that uses photons works fine and the other path(s) doesn't. In low light seasons, I seem to need a lot of carbs.

Is the problem that I have such a huge electron deficit that I eat a ton of food during low light seasons to try to get enough electrons from food?

I may have a partial solution. I recently bought a small travel trailer and plan on spending most of the winters (from now on) farther South. It's hard to get far enough South within the US to get great sun in the winter, but hopefully I can at least find some beaches where I can use the water and grounding to compensate.

Help me figure this out. What can I do? And I would appreciate it if you don't just say reread x, y, and z. If I understood x, y, and z, I probably wouldn't have to ask for help.

### cantweight — 2014-03-24T16:08:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117591>

I wish I had the answer. I have struggled with periods of easy losses and rapid gains myself. Can not put my finger on the answers. There is still imbalance in places in my body but it does not seem extreme to me. I do the right things to the best of my ability and myself and one of my children struggles along while the rest of the family are fit as fiddles following the principles we have learned here. I put together all the pieces I can like you and am coming up short. At least we are not alone

> The weight started flying off and by early Fall I was down to 205 lbs, the lowest weight in over 30 years. I thought I had things under control.
>
> My doctor thought I might have prostate cancer based on positive dre. Saw a urologist who thought it was cancer. This all happened early to mid summer. The IF and walking was my attempt to starve the cancer of glucose if in fact it was cancer.
>
> I schedule a MRI for early January. I weighed 230 lbs the morning of the MRI. So from early October when I weighed 205, I had gained 25 lbs in less than 3 months.
>
> The MRI was negative for cancer and I allowed myself a couple days of eating whatever I wanted. I couldn't stop. Within a month, I had gained another 25 lbs. So now I have gone from 205 in October to 255 in February, 50 lbs in 4 months.
>
> I can't seem to go more than 3 or 4 days before I have overwhelming cravings. This is the same problem I had before I started the Leptin Rx. When my body is working right, I don't have cravings, so something is definitely not right. I have been drinking spring water for over a year, I eat a ketogenic diet, I don't know of any increase in emf, most of the supplements I take I have been taking for a long period of time, etc.
>
> I am stumped. Every nice day I get out in the sun, but we sure haven't had many nice days so far this year. Maybe all I need is a week of sun. The only other thing I can think to do is increase fats.
>
> **I don't understand all this pathway stuff, but it seems the path that uses photons works fine and the other path(s) doesn't. In low light seasons, I seem to need a lot of carbs.**
>
> Is the problem that I have such a huge electron deficit that I eat a ton of food during low light seasons to try to get enough electrons from food?
>
> I may have a partial solution. I recently bought a small travel trailer and plan on spending most of the winters (from now on) farther South. It's hard to get far enough South within the US to get great sun in the winter, but hopefully I can at least find some beaches where I can use the water and grounding to compensate.
>
> Help me figure this out. What can I do? And I would appreciate it if you don't just say reread x, y, and z. If I understood x, y, and z, I probably wouldn't have to ask for help.

Huck. I discovered how sensitive I am to sunlight this winter. I didn't have the drive for carbs - yet I gained weight, due to lack of electrons. Sensitivity to light or lack of light directly impacts your vitamin A cycle, as well as thyroid production... and cortisol production. coupled with dehydration - and non-native EMF, your body pretty much has only one choice - gain weight to conserve the limited energy it has. So I've come to the conclusion that Sunlight and Water are key especially in a non-native EMF environment....

I need sun like there's no tomorrow... this also explains why I was my healthiest in Guam 12hrs of sunlight year round at the equator... I was banging with energy even though I was totally out of sync with eating and circadian cycles.... the sun is my key...

>
> maybe I am all wrong.. and rambling stupid stuff.. but this is all because of my own personal experiences I have come to these conclusions.. Might all be a N=1

I don't really understand all this stuff in any detail, but here's what I am wondering.

We need electrons and/or photons. For some of us, our bodies are well adapted to using photons. By getting plenty of sun in high light seasons, our energy levels increase - we lose weight, hormones normalize, brain works better, etc.

But once the sun gets too low in the sky to give us all the photons we need for energy, we have to rely on electrons. And for some of us, the pathway(s) to gather and use electrons for energy is inefficient at best. There is a mismatch between photon processing and electron processing. What I am currently trying to do is to extend the high light season for at least a month in the Fall and start it at least a month earlier in the Spring. That still leaves at least 4 months of low light I have to survive.

For years, I have turned to sugars and starches for energy during the low light season. So now that's what my brain knows - low energy, eat 6 doughnuts. Or snack on a big bag of potato chips all afternoon. But we know glucose is not as good as ketones, especially for brain functioning, so there is an almost constant need for more glucose. For those of us who have very low energy in the winter, the cravings for glucose generating foods are often overwhelming. For example, doing my taxes or writing a college paper usually was accompanied by diet coke and a big bag of potato chips. I knew eventually I would have a big crash, but it was the only way I knew to ramp up my brain.

Just exposing myself to electrons doesn't seem to be enough. I need to know what I need to do to get my body to efficiently absorb and use electrons. While a ketogenic diet, good water, and grounding provide some stability, they don't produce even close to the same energy level as photons do (for me).

So as I see my options:

1.  Move to where I can get plenty of photons year round
2.  Use a light source to generate photons
3.  Improve body function to better absorb and utilize electrons

Option 1 is fairly expensive, but right now is the best option.

Option 2 is a bit of unknown. What is available that works?

I have a Philips light that I use for about 15 minutes first thing in the morning during low light season. I don't know if it helps or not. I know there has been some discussion on this board about lights, but I don't remember anything definitive.

Option 3 is probably the best option, IF I understood what it is that I need to do. This is where I need some suggestions.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-25T14:27:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117847>

I don't know how much this is really about winter.

Huck says:

> For those of us who have very low energy in the winter, the cravings for glucose generating foods are often overwhelming.

So here winter appears as the basic problem, _causing_ "very low energy" -- and sugary or starchy foods appear as the solution to the low energy (although they're also conceived in terms of what the body is demanding).

But those foods are no solution, since they simply don't supply that much energy -- and can't be held on hand to supply it as needed. Accordingly, they have to be eaten and re-eaten -- which Huck also refers to:

> ... almost constant need for more glucose ...

What he _doesn't_ say is that that cycle also helps to push insulin up and down-regulate his fat-burning enzymes. And there's where his real energy stores are: in his fat. It's like money in a safe-deposit box to which he's lost the key.

Is winter the problem, or is that he can't use his fat stores?

Couldn't we suggest ways to help Huck transition to more efficient fat-burning?

For example, how about coconut oil (or MCT oil)? Personally, I dislike the taste of coconut oil (even Nutiva) and don't use it much, but aren't I right in saying that the medium chain triglycerides in it can be burnt quite easily?

How about adding some of that in?

### cantweight — 2014-03-25T14:53:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117848>

Huck do you do bulletproof coffee? I have very little issue with needing carbs yet I am still in a similar situation as you which only lends to the argument that food is a tiny part of the problem.

What is your cortisol like? I know my uncontrollable carb cravings were a problem when my cortisol was way off.
Can you calm those cravings with seasonal carbs? Root veggies, citrus, and the like? The inflammation caused by one donut let alone 6 makes me shudder
If that carb craving is just driving you too hard just give it carbs that are not deadly...the body will get used to it. Surprisingly quickly.

You are absolutely right...we are beings with completely inefficient use of electrons. I have not yet figured out how to battle that beast and I have been at it for some time. And most of the science behind it baffles me.

### Huck — 2014-03-25T15:16:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117850>

> [cantweight said:](/goto/post?id=117848)

> Can you calm those cravings with seasonal carbs? Root veggies, citrus, and the like? The inflammation caused by one donut let alone 6 makes me shudder

I tested hormones for about 2 years and then stopped most testing. In the summer I had fairly good levels and then very low levels in the winter. I decided for now I would just use how I feel as my gauge.

One idea I have is that the cravings may be toxin related. As we lose fat, toxins are released into the blood stream. Is it possible that dumping toxins to quickly for the liver to process results in the body's attempt to protect itself by holding on to or even increasing fat? And the easiest way to increase fat is to eat starches and sugars. There have been several weight points that always seemed to coincide with cravings. For years, I knew that when I got to 270 the cravings would kick in. Then it was 250 and later 230. But I had to hit each weight point many, many times before I could get below it without cravings.

I have several mercury fillings and wonder if this might be related. What's the best way to test for mercury?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-25T15:49:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117856>

> [Huck said:](/goto/post?id=117849)
>
> I just recently started (2 days ago) to make sure I got plenty of fats in my diet - coconut oil, organic butter, olive oil). Last summer, I found that adding fats was really slowing down my weight loss, so I cut way back on fats. When Fall started, I should have started increasing fats, but didn't think of it because I was feeling so good.

It was especially the medium-chain triglycerides that I was thinking might be helpful. I just checked:

> MCTs passively diffuse from the GI tract to the portal system (longer fatty acids are absorbed into the lymphatic system) without requirement for modification like long-chain fatty acids or very-long-chain fatty acids. In addition, MCTs do not require bile salts for digestion. Patients that have malnutrition or malabsorption syndromes are treated with MCTs because they do not require energy for absorption, utilization, or storage.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medium-chain\_triglyceride](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medium-chain_triglyceride)

IOW, it's maybe easier to get energy out of them than from some other fats, so perhaps they'd be a nice option while transitioning. Maybe a way to get a burst of energy without the downside of carbs.

I've used coconut oil a little, but never MCT oil. That may be more effective. Dave Asprey seems to be a great believer in it. Dave sells it, too, although I don't know I'd buy Dave's own, since his stuff is pretty pricey.

The other thing that might be worth a try is ketone-esthers. Try googling that. It's not something I've tried or know much about, but apparently they're becoming available. Apparently, the military have been experimenting with them for some time, for special forces soldiers that need to get into ketosis quickly. But as I say, I've no direct experience there and don't know if there are downsides -- others here may know more.

Some people recommend L-glutamine for sugar-cravings. I recall that Nora Gedgaudas does. But I don't know Dr. Kruse's opinion on that one. It may be an option temporarily.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-25T16:16:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117862>

Huck, in winter when it was a cloudy day (without precipitation) I'd really notice the loss in energy. You said:

> I have a Philips light that I use for about 15 minutes first thing in the morning during low light season. I don't know if it helps or not. I know there has been some discussion on this board about lights, but I don't remember anything definitive.

Did you notice if you skipped the light session that the rest of that day was adversely affected? I doubt anything would be as good as the sun, but I think you should feel the absence of the light session.

Lately I've noticed that a cloudy day doesn't affect me as much. I started to pat myself on the back, but then I realized that the sun is stronger now, and the days aren't as cold. I don't spend much energy staying warm throughout the day. So I'm not becoming a superstar, my environment is. ;-) Still, I think someday I'll have more energy reserves available so that a cloudy day or two won't set me back.

Also, on cloudy days this winter I used a red led light in the nose for a few minutes. No, it didn't seem to help, but red light doesn't have much strength. Plus I know I have poor redox, so the local energy in the nose wouldn't be transferred far.

I can't say I have seen any benefit. But I turn it on in the morning when I am checking email and news, so it is no big deal to turn it on.

> Also, on cloudy days this winter I used a red led light in the nose for a few minutes. No, it didn't seem to help, but red light doesn't have much strength. Plus I know I have poor redox, so the local energy in the nose wouldn't be transferred far.

I am glad you mentioned the red light. I have been waiting for more feedback before I bought one. Think I might pass on it.

### Huck — 2014-03-25T16:47:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117867>

> [BJK77 said:](/goto/post?id=117864)
>
> I could have written your post Huck . . . EXCEPT that I lose weight instead of gain which I have yet to understand. I wonder if it isn't due to my extremely inflamed gut that just cannot absorb any nutrients and I'm basically starving over the winter months despite how much and the quality of the foods that I eat.
>
> I do believe I am much more sensitive to the effects of photons than electrons like you said. Whether this is due to me being unable to "access" the electron pathway or something else, I don't know, but despite all I do with drinking tons of good quality water, eating ketogenic, earthing, etc, etc I find myself getting worse and worse. I made great progress this past summer when I was sure to get plenty of sunlight and of course as I mentioned many times on the forum, I CRASHED and crashed hard as soon as summer ended even though my diet was still very keto and I was doing everything else.
>
> It's getting clearer and clearer to me that I need to make the leap and take option #1 that you mentioned above. We've been actively working toward that for a while now, but then I wonder if I need a deep winter to truly heal, how do I do that somewhere far enough south where I'm getting plenty of sunshine? I know there's always an ice machine, of course, but there's no "natural" cold. I don't know if that makes a difference or not. Would getting the year-round photons just be a band-aid over my problem or will it actualy heal my body? I don't know.

I bet NASA or the Russians or maybe even the Chinese have our answer.

# Thread: New Labs - Please Hack
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs-please-hack.10301/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Paul S — 2014-03-24T17:19:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs-please-hack.10301/post-117601>

Ran new labs for first time since last fall. Been on the Kruse wagon for 2 1/2 yrs. 39 yo male. strict Keto-epi-paleo for 5 months, epi-paleo/paleo for past 2+ years. Sleep very good (using Magnetico for past 10 mo.). I fall asleep easily (9-10pm) and up easy (5:30 - 6:30am, typically). Salivary cortisol assay shows correct pattern through the day, however, within that pattern, I am low/normal across the 4 measurement points. Rev Osmosis water for past 10 mo (1 - 1.5 gal/day). CT 4-5x per week (off and on for past 18 months), most recent 6 weeks has been consistently about 15 min per day at 40 degrees. Body comp is good (6'1"; 175#, 10% body fat) and has been consistently at that level since starting paleo 2 1/2 yrs ago. Workouts: 2-3x per week heavy lifting, 1-2x per week HIIT past few weeks, but little HIIT prior to that. Grounding as much as I can. Home is EMF mitigated. Still, looking for more improvement in several areas, however.

Vit D: 84 (supplementing 5-7k IU daily and using tanning 2-3x per week - helps long time issues w/skin)
BUN/Creatinine: 10x (best it's ever been). Have been 17-20 over past 2 yrs.
DHEA: 200 (see below for more history here)
Estradiol: <20 (has been this level for 15 months, consistently, after being elevated in years past).
Progesterone: 0.2 (range 0.1 - 2.1) First time testing this. Considering cream as support for Free T?
Platelets: < 100 (have been below stated lab range of 150 - 450 for 4 years, pre-paleo and post-paleo). Thoughts? Natural ways to help this?
SHBG: 90 (high). Over past 18 months, this has been between 60-75). Not sure why it jumped recently.
Free T3: 2.7 (pretty consistent result for FT3. I have used BHRT T3 in the past, up to 25mg/day and got same results). Not taking anything for this now. Working keto/CT angle after no results with BHRT.
Total T3: 89 (Range 87-178)
Free T4: 0.98 (Range 0.46 - 1.31)
Rev T3: 24 (range 9-27)
Total Testosterone: 275 (range 175 - 781). Historically, has been between 400-500)
Free Test.: Waiting lab results for this still, but it's always low normal (in past it's been between 10 - 16)
My Liver Enzymes have improved (ALT/ASG) and are well in range. In past they have been elevated slightly.
IGF-1: 96 (range 132 - 333, historically, I have been between 110-150).

I am not currently supplementing with DHEA, having stopped a few weeks ago after getting 23andMe results and linking in the Yasko book (I think) in the section on Decreased G6PDH Activity and the issues surrounding sulfur problems with sulfer donors (DHEA-S!), etc. Each time I have taken DHEA (sublingually or capsules) I have skin issues arise for various amounts of time. It does directly increase my serum labs results of DHEA-S, when tested (I have had DHEA results between 400 - 750 at times in the past 2 years when supplementing 50mg/day) and feel better when on it, however, the downsides are unnerving. I have been limiting/eliminating sulfur foods and supplements for 3-4 weeks now (after 23andMe came back) and may look to add back DHEA in another month or so. Urine Sulfate testing (at home) show levels around 400 - 800 currently. Also, to help skin and gut issues, I avoid all nightshades, dairy, seeds, nuts, etc. Have been strict about this for past 4 months and it has helped. Tried adding 100% cacao late January (after Jan webinar about resistant starch) but that made me regress. Possibly due to seed aspect, but now that I have 23andMe results, may also have been due to sulfur detox pathway issues. Have not had coffee for 5 months either. May retry those down the road after addressing Methylation issues for a few months, which had not been assessed prior to March of this year.

Recent 23andMe results showed: MTHFR C677T +/-; CBS A360 +/- (sulfer/ammonia detox); ACAT1-02 +/-; VDR +/-; COMT -/- (rapid breakdown of dopamine); MTR/MTRR +/-.

Recent 3-4 weeks (before these labs), I started hydroxl- and methly-B12, 5-MTHF, molybdenum, lithium orotate and L-Tyrosine (500mg/day) based on 23andMe. I feel much better, more stable mood, better energy, but continue to need Free T and thyroid support. Other supps I am taking: CoQ10; D-Ribose; Iodoral; Vit K; Vit D; Arginine/Ornithine; Saccharomyces Boulardi and daily, homemade bone broth.

I have next appt with functional medicine doc in April. Am considering progesterone cream, but should I consider testosterone directly instead? if so, in what form/delivery method? More CT and more HIIT? Stinging Nettle Root to address SHBG? Other suggestions alongside these items? Like others, I am working on deciphering recent blogs, but non-science background is making it a slow-go on the redox front and what else to takeaway from an action-items standpoint. On one had, I am pleased that my hydration status has greatly improved, while other things are stagnant or too variable. Thank you for any suggestions.

### Paul S — 2014-03-24T20:37:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs-please-hack.10301/post-117648>

One other question - after skimming the links (not time for a full read yet) to CPC#8, I wondered what impact Keto diets and CRON have on SHBG or other hormone markers? My diet is high fat, moderate protein breakfast, typically no lunch, and a dinner of high fat, moderate protein. Low carbs mixed in to both.

# Thread: Am I ready for the ice?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-ready-for-the-ice.10304/post-117644>

Today was my first day of CT. I ate my BAB, waited an hour and a half, then had 24oz of water from the fridge. I have one of those temperature guns that Jack suggested. I filled up the sink, the water was already 53.7F straight out of the faucet. Right off the bat I was able to hold my face under water for as long as I could hold my breath. I did this 6-8 times right in a row, and my skin temp wouldn't drop below 64F. Does this mean that I do well with the cold and that I'm already good to start with 20-40lbs of ice on my chest?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-24T21:04:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-ready-for-the-ice.10304/post-117668>

Anything larger, though, and I didn't feel as thought it really compressed my skin much. First day of ice on chest tomorrow! Going to be checking the temp of my skin every five minutes and checking color to make sure that it stays cherry red/pink and doesn't turn white.

Step it up, I did! I put 20lbs of ice on my chest (with really tight Under Armour) this morning and I was able to go the whole hour on my first day! Pink/cherry red chest the whole time and my skin temp never dropped below 50F. Should I continue this for a week or so, or do you think I can jump right over to no shirt and ice?

### nicld — 2014-03-24T21:13:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-time-waking-for.10306/post-117671>

peeing.

Ok, I know that is was mentioned that getting up in the middle of the night to use the bathroom meant a sign that something was off but for the life of me I cannot remember what or even what webinar it was in.

The DH is getting up more and more to do this and I am wondering why. I want to give his a good reason why this is happening so maybe he will start to change some of the crap that he is doing.

### Huck — 2014-03-24T21:52:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-time-waking-for.10306/post-117684>

One possible reason is an enlarged prostate. The prostate sits right under the bladder, so when the prostate gets bigger it squeezes the bladder. The urethra also runs through the prostate, so it might be "pinched".

### Lava — 2014-03-24T22:12:17+0200

# Thread: Carbs, how low can you go?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbs-how-low-can-you-go.10310/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Infinusss — 2014-03-25T01:08:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbs-how-low-can-you-go.10310/post-117733>

I was over at the Bulletproof Forum and they were talking about how going extremely low carb can cause all sorts of problems:
**
"So I was listening to this Chris Kresser podcast from last November ( [http://chriskresser....with-jeff-leach](http://chriskresser.com/you-are-what-your-bacteria-eat-the-importance-of-feeding-your-microbiome-with-jeff-leach) ) and they were talking about how if you don't get enough carbohydrates, or starve your micro biome of the fiber that it needs they can start eating the mucus in your gut causing all sorts of problems, including leaky gut.

"This seems to be a similar thing that Dave talks about. He went very low carb and ended up with problems creating mucus for gut lining. I believe it ended up causing a little bit of leaky gut and even caused him to have an egg allergy."**

**"So, I would say at least tentatively now that if anyone is on a ketogenic or very low carb diet, that you might consider some prebiotic fibers or resistant starch just to make sure that you’re feeding your gut bugs what they need to survive."**

**"I have said this before and I will say it again extremely low carb or no carb should be used in only specific medical situations (seizures) and for very short period of time."** [http://perfecthealthdiet.com/category/zero-carb-dangers/](http://perfecthealthdiet.com/category/zero-carb-dangers/)

**"Mucus is a glycoprotein that should require glucose / glycogen and protein to be properly synthesized in the body. Therefore a permanent zero carb diet would prevent if from being developed properly."**

**
(From the Perfect Health Diet Link Above)

"So we might take high LDL on Paleo as a possible sign of two things:
**

-   **A chronic state of glucose deficiency, leading to very low T3 levels and suppressed clearance of LDL particles by lipid transport pathways.**
-   **Absence of infections or oxidative stress which would clear LDL particles by immune pathways.**

**
The solution? Eat more carbs, and address any remaining cause of hypothyroidism, such as iodine or selenium deficiency. T3 levels should then rise and LDL levels return to normal."**

It seems to me that they are saying that we need carbs to feed our gut biome, to make mucus efficiently, to regulate T3 and cortisol and LDL, all sorts of things. Does this only apply to people that are sugar burners? Once I complete the reset, a low (under 30g/day and no "refeeds days") or no starchy carb diet shouldn't matter to me, right?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-25T02:02:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbs-how-low-can-you-go.10310/post-117747>

^^^^total BS begun from Jaminet.

### Inger — 2014-03-25T07:43:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbs-how-low-can-you-go.10310/post-117789>

I know about many people who have eaten 100% zero carb for many years and are healthy as horses.. hmmm.
I am pretty much zero carb most days of the year too for about 4 years, I stick to it because it makes me feel better, not worse lol How could it be possible, we can not eat our natural diet? There are many places on this globe where carbs are very scarce all year round.. and people have eaten pretty much zero carb all their life and have thrived.... I wonder how? I do not believe we would have to eat something that do not grow around where we live to stay healthy. That said... I gorge on wild berries all late summer and fall.. and I eat wild mushrooms and herbs too... maybe it has saved me  (but those has so little carbs you will be probably VLC anyways)
Maybe it is the EMF that destroys the gut instead?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-26T09:15:56+0200

She means 100 g of protein not 100 g of meat/fish, Caroline.

If you reckon on 1g of protein per kg of bodyweight -- a figure used by many low-carb people (including Dr. Tim here from what one poster said) -- then Inger probably wouldn't need that much. If she's s petite lady, she might weigh no more than 55kg.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-26T09:28:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carb-count-for-reset.10311/post-118030>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=117798)
>
> I put my breakfast in a calculator extra for you Audrey. This is what come out;
>
> 850 kcal
>
> 57 g = 60 % fat
> 82 g = 39 % protein
> 1,6 g = 1 % carbs
>
> … I do get I am too high in protein.  I have way over 100 grams every day.  maybe that is why I feel bad when sitting too much in front of the computer? Like my face feel weird? Maybe I would not feel bad about it when eating less protein and more fat? I need to test this out. I do now Jack has said high protein and high EMF = not good. But my solution so far has been to close the darn computer and go outside... not limiting my protein.... and when I do that it feels so good

It is a lot, isn't it?

I know percentages are a bit beside the point, but AFAIK Stefansson in the Belle Vue Hospital experiment was eating around 80% fat and 20% protein (and around 2600Kcals a day). Stefansson had, of course, worked as an anthropologist with the Eskimo and lived, he says, on an "all-meat diet" while with them. I guess he did what they did.

I have seen figures claiming that people in the Far North must have been eating up to 45% protein, but it turns out that these figures are based on assays of carcasses of animals scientists have assumed people ate. There are just too many assumptions imbedded in that (one of the biggest being that that ignores selectivity in what cuts people eat and what abandon or throw to the dogs).

I have to say I find around 80% of fat difficult.

I think it's _easy_ to get up to 65 or 70%. I think one's pretty much there with a traditional Northern European diet -- i.e. tend to prefer fattier cuts of meat, don't cut the fat off, use fat in cooking sometimes, and add butter or olive oil to vegetables and salads at the table.

But I find any more fat than this a bit unappetising, and any less in the way of meat/fish and vegetables unappealing ... and meals start to look not very like meals.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-26T10:48:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carb-count-for-reset.10311/post-118034>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=118032)

> Have you gotten your weight where you want it to be?

It's fine really. I'd guess that another ten pounds or so will probably come off, if I stay fairly low-carb and avoid drinking much. But it just doesn't notice now -- probably it wouldn't even with my shirt off now.

> What do you have for breakfast? do you have lunch?

I usually have eggs (cooked in ghee or whatever) and some meat or fish. With that I have some boiled greens or an avocado, or both. I'll have coffee, too.

I do usually eat lunch -- but usually no more than a bowl of salad with olive oil and a squeeze of lemon and some fish or meat. Not much of the latter, either -- maybe as little as a couple of ounces, though up to probably around 4 ounces, if I feel like it.

I would guess I eat something like 100 to 110 grams of protein a day. I don't really keep any kind of check on it, though.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-26T14:05:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carb-count-for-reset.10311/post-118041>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=118037)
>
> ... I am pretty curvy I think. ...

I'd be inclined to say, "So you should be."

> You all make think to stop gorging like this  i am not even hungry I just have a great appetite.

Yeah, but appetite does self-regulate, doesn't it? I mean it does, if one's regulatory systems aren't screwed -- and I bet yours aren't. This is what the mainstream with its tiresome calories in/calories out picture fails to see. How would people have got on before charts and kitchen scales were invented, if one needed to regulate all this consciously? How would animals manage? Gary Taubes has great fun with this.

I guess even macronutrient choices self-regulate within limits. I know Stefansson said when travelling in the Arctic he'd been in the position both of having a few bags of seal oil but hardly any lean meat and in the position of having plenty of lean meat but having pretty much run out of oil. He said both situations are really nasty and you wouldn't want to be in either. However, the latter is nastier and pretty debilitating. In either you _know_ what you want and you don't require scales and tables to work it out.

I finish breakfast about 7 am and sometimes don't eat again until 2 pm. My hardest issue is eating till nice and full. The eating disorder sometimes rears it's head and tells me to still be a little hungry. If I eat till nice at and full for lunch I might not eat supper and I like to cook supper for the hubby. But, there are times that when I eat my lunch there is no WAY that I am hungry for supper so I don't eat or have very very little and he is ok with that.

> On one occasion, a group of wretched beings was seen on the farther bank of the St. Lawrence, like wild animals driven by famine to the borders of the settler's clearing. The river was full of drifting ice, and there was no crossing without risk of life. The Indians, in their desperation, made the attempt; and midway their canoes were ground to atoms among the tossing masses. Agile as wild-cats, they all leaped upon a huge raft of ice, the squaws carrying their children on their shoulders, a feat at which Champlain marveled when he saw their starved and emaciated condition. Here they began a wail of despair; when happily the pressure of other masses thrust the sheet of ice against the northern shore. They landed and soon made their appearance at the fort, worn to skeletons and horrible to look upon. The French gave them food, which they devoured with a frenzied avidity, and, unappeased, **fell upon a dead dog left on the snow by Champlain for two months past as a bait for foxes. They broke this carrion into fragments, and thawed and devoured it, to the disgust of the spectators**, who tried vainly to prevent them.

[http://www.gutenberg.org/files/3721/3721-h/3721-h.htm](http://www.gutenberg.org/files/3721/3721-h/3721-h.htm)

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-27T15:18:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carb-count-for-reset.10311/post-118264>

Yeah, it would include fibre, as Sammy says. And some low carb people count net carbs rather than carbs -- some of the Atkins people work on that basis.

However, I can't recall Dr. Kruse ever speaking of net carbs.

But I doubt you need to forgo your avocado, Infinimus.

First off, I think Jack's figures are approximate and not meant to be rigid guidelines, since he couldn't be more specific unless he knew all your details and labs.

Secondly, IIRC the figure you'd mentioned was 25 grams. Turning to Jack's book ... page 96 ... I notice the figure he gives there is 50 grams -- and that's for **breakfast** not for the whole day:

> Make sure your breakfast has little to no carbs (less than 50 grams) ...

On the next page he suggests the lower figure:

> ... **if** you are overweight by more than 30 pounds ...

but is presumably still referring to a figure for breakfast not the whole day.

And then he immediately adds that if not you can "titrate up your carbs".

I'd think if you avoid eating the dense carbohydrates that the Paleo people disapprove of (and the Perfect Health Diet people recommend) -- polished rice and potatoes --you'll be OK. If your veg is mostly above-ground stuff, and for the rest there's avocados and maybe a few nuts, you'll likely be under 50 grams on the whole day.

Looks like I'm either going to have to kick avocados for the rest or on days that I have an avocado, I'm pretty much then just limited to one serving of spinach for the whole day, if that.
>
> 1 avocado = 17g
> 6 eggs = 3-4g
> 1/3 bag of spinach = 3g
> \-----------------------------
> Total = 23-24g carbs. Damn, that adds up quickly!

You are missing the big point............[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/insulin-makes-you-fat-or-is-it.10344/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/insulin-makes-you-fat-or-is-it.10344/)

### Infinusss — 2014-03-27T18:55:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carb-count-for-reset.10311/post-118327>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=118314)
>
> You are missing the big point............[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/insulin-makes-you-fat-or-is-it.10344/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/insulin-makes-you-fat-or-is-it.10344/)

I think I'm still missing the point after reading that. I'm going to start over and read through the blogs thoroughly this time. In the mean time, generally speaking, am I ok with the carb content in what I'm eating during the reset?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-27T20:02:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carb-count-for-reset.10311/post-118335>

> [Infinusss said:](/goto/post?id=118327)
>
> I think I'm still missing the point after reading that. ...

First off in that thread we have:

> Think of food not as metabolic fuel but as hormone information.

Well, sure.

Next he starts talking about glucagon. So, AFAICT, glucagon is the point. I guess this may be the (or one) reason why the breakfast is high in protein. Jack is given to leaving clues around but not spelling things out. It's the first I've heard of this.

I tried googling:

"glucagon eggs"

thinking there may have been something relevant to eggs in there (since you said you'd eaten six). I think probably this isn't what Jack's getting at. However, that search does bring up some interesting links. One of the top is at Stefan Guyenet's blog. I'm afraid I'd taken to thinking Stefan didn't know as much as he thought he knew after some knockabout fun Peter at Hyperlpid had made of him. But, at any rate, it turns he does know about this:

> Glucagon is a hormone that plays an important role in blood glucose control. Like insulin, it's secreted by the pancreas, though it's secreted by a different cell population than insulin (alpha vs. beta cells). In some ways, glucagon opposes insulin. ...

[http://wholehealthsource.blogspot.co.uk/2013/04/glucagon-dietary-protein-and-low.html](http://wholehealthsource.blogspot.co.uk/2013/04/glucagon-dietary-protein-and-low.html)

I guess I'll have to read the whole thing … and the link Jack cites.

EDIT:

I forgot to mention _this_ also comes up:

[http://www.lookcut.com/articles/best-proteins-for-weight-loss.html](http://www.lookcut.com/articles/best-proteins-for-weight-loss.html)

I can think of at least one paleo blogger who sells whey-protein off his site. Maybe the guy knows about this, although he mightn't wish to talk about it publicly? There may be others.

# Thread: CT During pregnancy

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 13

### Marie Negrila — 2014-03-25T02:50:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-117759>

I was going through the different threads and I could not find one talking about CT and pregnancy.

Does anyone know if we can continue CT during pregnancy and under which conditions it can be done with no harm to the foetus/baby.

Thank you!

### TribalSpice — 2014-04-17T02:50:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-122035>

I have been wondering about this also.

I have I beautiful video called "Birth As We Know It". There is footage of women birthing in The Black Sea in Russia (in summer of course) where the water temperature wouldn't be particularly warm. There is also some amazing footage of women, children and babies swimming in a hole in the ice, them sitting semi-naked breastfeeding in the snow.

### Marie — 2014-04-17T06:06:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-122058>

[http://www.orgasmicbirth.com/](http://www.orgasmicbirth.com/)
and
[http://documentarylovers.com/film/the-business-of-being-born/](http://documentarylovers.com/film/the-business-of-being-born/)

A must view for anyone with a child on the way.

Marie

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-18T15:43:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-122363>

IIRC Jack has said that CT during pregnancy can be beneficial however, if you're over weight you need to be careful b/c it can cause you to release toxins in your fat stores. So if you're over weight or obese while pregnant - you want to wait until after you deliver the baby and finish your nursing relationship.

That said - I did CT while I was nursing Havoc w/o any ill effect upon him. If you're concerned talk with your MW or OB about getting into Cold water regularly while pregnant. I will say that when I was pregnant with both Chaos and Havoc - I did take more cold showers than hot - due to be constantly hot!

### Marie — 2014-04-18T19:22:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-122397>

Ummm... I'm not selling anything. I posted those links for people who read this thread, not just the OP.
And the Business of Being Born video is free on that link.

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-18T19:30:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-122402>

Business of Being Born is an amazing video! really awesome stuff!

### Marie Negrila — 2014-04-20T14:35:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-122666>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=122363)
>
> IIRC Jack has said that CT during pregnancy can be beneficial however, if you're over weight you need to be careful b/c it can cause you to release toxins in your fat stores. So if you're over weight or obese while pregnant - you want to wait until after you deliver the baby and finish your nursing relationship.
>
> That said - I did CT while I was nursing Havoc w/o any ill effect upon him. If you're concerned talk with your MW or OB about getting into Cold water regularly while pregnant. I will say that when I was pregnant with both Chaos and Havoc - I did take more cold showers than hot - due to be constantly hot!

Thank you so much for your straight forward answer. I truly appreciate it. I will be posting my results over time.

### sooperb — 2014-04-27T10:58:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-123558>

Gretchen I would be cautious about asking your midwife or obstetrician such questions! I can't imagine there are any who would give you informed advice, they would probably throw their hands up in horror and think you are nuts. It's almost certainly outside of their experience/knowledge base and certainly not taught.

When my daughter was expecting her first, she decided quite late on that she wanted a home birth in a birthing pool. She told her midwife who was aghast, told her she couldn't possibly, it wasn't safe. My daughter insisted, she was then visited every few days by midwives who tried to dissuade her, culminating in the head of nursing who also told her she couldn't. Behind the scenes they were checking on her mental health! When they saw she wasn't for budging, they told her that the midwives would only be there if she gave birth 2 weeks either side of her due date. When she said well, I will just have to manage on my own they told her that it was an offence to give birth unaided and that she would be charged and probably jailed if she did.

In the event she gave birth within the "window" and they turned out and were fine but they had given her many weeks of unnecessary anxiety at what was already a very difficult time as her boyfriend had walked away.

### yarralea — 2014-04-29T12:54:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-123849>

So glad this thread was here. I have been trying to get pregnant for a few years, and have given up. The issue is with my partner -who had previously had a vasectomy and reversal. We have one daughter 8 post reversal, and tests indicate pregnancy is unlikely.
But i was wondering whether if a miracle happened whether CT would be damaging or cause any issues. I'm looking pretty fit and have had a pretty clean diet, as as much of a clean environment as I can, but I would not be concerned of a toxic overload in fat stores as an issue.

If there is a star in the east I will let you all know. lol

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-29T18:01:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-123895>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=123558)
>
> Gretchen I would be cautious about asking your midwife or obstetrician such questions! I can't imagine there are any who would give you informed advice, they would probably throw their hands up in horror and think you are nuts. It's almost certainly outside of their experience/knowledge base and certainly not taught.
>
> When my daughter was expecting her first, she decided quite late on that she wanted a home birth in a birthing pool. She told her midwife who was aghast, told her she couldn't possibly, it wasn't safe. My daughter insisted, she was then visited every few days by midwives who tried to dissuade her, culminating in the head of nursing who also told her she couldn't. Behind the scenes they were checking on her mental health! When they saw she wasn't for budging, they told her that the midwives would only be there if she gave birth 2 weeks either side of her due date. When she said well, I will just have to manage on my own they told her that it was an offence to give birth unaided and that she would be charged and probably jailed if she did.
>
> In the event she gave birth within the "window" and they turned out and were fine but they had given her many weeks of unnecessary anxiety at what was already a very difficult time as her boyfriend had walked away.

Sue

A lot depends regarding OBs/MW if they're in a stand alone practice or part of a bigger consortium/ACO/ or HMO here in the US. In Virginia - you can work with MWs who are NPs or CNPs who do not have hospital privileges and only provide birthing support at home! Mine encouraged me to do things that the TRADITIONAL BIRTH guidelines set by ACOG believes is RISKY! Many of these MWs deliver breech babies vaginally - when ACOG REQUIRES OBs to deliver by C-Section. Then when you look a the track record of places like the FARM & What Ina May Gaskin has done embracing natural birth... you can see this is a better way to deliver and is safer for both mama and baby for the majority of the population. Granted there are small percentages that this type of birth and support won't work.

But in General most MW in the US are very cognizant of Eating Real Foods, limiting carbs during pregnancy etc. Some even advocate a glass of wine/week (Gasp!), and eating raw fish.

My International Board Certified Lactation Consultant, is currently finishing up her MW training. She advocated Paleo/epi-Paleo template for her clients, as well as following circadian cycles.... what's interesting is the people in medicine pushing change are the outcasts that the AMA and other Organizations can't control - I think we know someone like that....

### sooperb — 2014-04-29T18:13:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-123900>

It's gratifying that there are such enlightened professionals around, my daughter could sure have used someone on her side. What you do get here are some who will agree in principle that natural is best but if they work for the NHS (and that's the vast majority) they won't risk assisting in anything that isn't an agreed procedure. That's pretty much across the board for health professionals in most disciplines here. I can see their point but being submissive never enabled any changes and I'm afraid nurses are amongst the worst for sticking to the rules, in the UK anyway.

### Marie Negrila — 2014-05-02T22:58:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-124721>

Yarralea, I believe I heard Dr. K say CT was excellent for fertility issues in the sense that it enhances the chances. That being said, in this case, I believe your partner would be the one to ideally be diving into CT to reach your common birth goal. Of course if you'd be doing CT as well it might be a great suynergetic effect. Just a thought.

# Thread: Measuring Serum Leptin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-03-25T07:24:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117788>

I noticed that Jack's optimizing labs do not list any test to measure serum leptin. Is that because this value cycles in some highly variable fashion and capturing a single fasting value would not give much information? Or maybe it is hidden there included as part of some panel?

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-25T09:22:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117803>

I think it's because it's not serum leptin that is important, but the ability of the leptin receptors to sense it (leptin sensitivity). You can think of it in a similar fashion as insulin, you can have higher or lower insulin levels but what determines that your metabolism is working well is your insulin sensitivity. The more resistant you are, the more of these two substances you have constantly in your blood. Higher leptin might seem useful in order to communicate to the hipothalamus that your energy stores are full and cut back your hunger, yet his has only showed to work in some research in mice and humans that had a genetic defect with the leptin receptors. If you give it to someone that is leptin resistant due to a messed configuration of his epigenetic switches it won't do a thing. That alone is difficult to use in isolation in order to assess how affects you. There are other biomarkers that can be used as a proxy to sense how leptin sensitive you are, like the DHEA levels, reverse T3, the 4x cortisol and melatonin panel, Vitamin D and HDL levels.

### persistence — 2014-03-25T11:07:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117819>

> [Albert83BCN said:](/goto/post?id=117803)
>
> I think it's because it's not serum leptin that is important, but the ability of the leptin receptors to sense it (leptin sensitivity). You can think of it in a similar fashion as insulin, you can have higher or lower insulin levels but what determines that your metabolism is working well is your insulin sensitivity. The more resistant you are, the more of these two substances you have constantly in your blood. Higher leptin might seem useful in order to communicate to the hipothalamus that your energy stores are full and cut back your hunger, yet his has only showed to work in some research in mice and humans that had a genetic defect with the leptin receptors. If you give it to someone that is leptin resistant due to a messed configuration of his epigenetic switches it won't do a thing. That alone is difficult to use in isolation in order to assess how affects you. There are other biomarkers that can be used as a proxy to sense how leptin sensitive you are, like the DHEA levels, reverse T3, the 4x cortisol and melatonin panel, Vitamin D and HDL levels.

So I was not suggesting any therapy with Leptin. I was just wondering if the person with Leptin resistance might show that as higher than normal fasting levels of Leptin in serum / blood.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-25T11:38:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117829>

Yeah I understood it that way also, I just brought up the example of the trials as a way to point out that the serum leptin levels that you might find _may_ not be an indicative of anything or at least not one easy to interpret. I'm not an expert at all but my understanding is that, to say, a leptin sensitive person that is well fed might have similar leptin serum levels at one time compared to a leptin resistant individual that is fasted yet their levels are high due to the increased fat mass. Two identical static frames of each context that are part of a totally different film. Maybe Jack might chime in to clarify it.

### nicld — 2014-03-25T15:51:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117857>

I had it done once and he said that it was pretty much a waste of money. My test was in the morning and the leptin levels showed up as low. Don't think that it would hurt to have it done and see what they read but it might not really tell you anything.

### persistence — 2014-03-25T20:53:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117892>

> [Albert83BCN said:](/goto/post?id=117829)
>
> Yeah I understood it that way also, I just brought up the example of the trials as a way to point out that the serum leptin levels that you might find _may_ not be an indicative of anything or at least not one easy to interpret. I'm not an expert at all but my understanding is that, to say, a leptin sensitive person that is well fed might have similar leptin serum levels at one time compared to a leptin resistant individual that is fasted yet their levels are high due to the increased fat mass. Two identical static frames of each context that are part of a totally different film. Maybe Jack might chime in to clarify it.

There is a similar issue with fasting insulin. It turns out that the endogenous pattern of insulin production looks a little like a sine wave. It constantly flows up and down and if you measure a single static reading, you have no idea where on that curve you actually sit.

To me it is mind boggling that they don't - as a standard practice for all prediabetics - sit you down for two hours and do a time series on insulin, just to establish what your endogenous pattern is. \*IF\* they could do that same time series for glucagon, leptin, and other key metabolic chemicals, then you would have a complete picture of the patient's actual condition.

As I said in another thread here, don't they want to distinguish the patient who overproduces glucagon and has normal insulin levels from the one who has normal glucagon and has massively high insulin (and probably insulin resistance)? Apparently, the medical profession really doesn't care what disease you actually have.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-25T21:59:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117897>

True, why would they put so much effort and time (aka money) in creating such a detailed profile when they can just tell you to exercise more, eat less and take some pills? duh

### persistence — 2014-03-26T03:45:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117988>

> [Albert83BCN said:](/goto/post?id=117897)
>
> True, why would they put so much effort and time (aka money) in creating such a detailed profile when they can just tell you to exercise more, eat less and take some pills? duh

No amount of exercise is going to reset what is broken there if the insulin resistance is not related to being overweight.

Fixing insulin resistance for a thin person might be addressed by things that make you more insulin sensitive.

Fixing glucagon overproduction might require you to use drugs that suppress glucose production in liver.

I don't think one cure fits all diseases here. I think they should treat the actual disease, which requires for a start defining what the actual disease is.

### Inger — 2014-03-26T08:15:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-118021>

Pone, maybe your actual disease is.. wreaking havoc on your natural circadian rhythm for years (?)......... and other kind of terror for the body... (like way too much EMF...)
What is disease? Isn't it when we cut ourselves off from mother nature? That might be when the suffering begins.

### persistence — 2014-03-26T21:36:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-118124>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=118021)
>
> Pone, maybe your actual disease is.. wreaking havoc on your natural circadian rhythm for years (?)......... and other kind of terror for the body... (like way too much EMF...)
> What is disease? Isn't it when we cut ourselves off from mother nature? That might be when the suffering begins.

I agree that something has been building up here for years and reached a critical threshold.

I treat what I can measure, and I just keep iterating through the possible causes and hoping one of those will bring more success.

I've tried grounding myself outside more often each day. I do find it brings some peace of mind.

I continue to adjust my circadian rhythm.

# Thread: Vascular Surgeon: Why I've ditched statins for good
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vascular-surgeon-why-ive-ditched-statins-for-good.10317/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-25T11:14:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vascular-surgeon-why-ive-ditched-statins-for-good.10317/post-117821>

> When I had a routine health check-up eight years ago, my cholesterol was so high that the laboratory thought there had been a mistake. I had 9.3 millimoles of cholesterol in every litre of blood — almost twice the recommended maximum.
>
> It was quite a shock. The GP instantly prescribed statins, the cholesterol-lowering drugs that are supposed to prevent heart disease and strokes. For eight years, I faithfully popped my 20mg atorvastatin pills, without side effects. Then, one day last May, I stopped. It wasn’t a snap decision; after looking more closely at the research, I’d concluded that statins were not going to save me from a heart attack and that my cholesterol levels were all but irrelevant. ...

< .... >

> ... The GP simply suggested I try another brand of statin. The sooner the better, he said, given that I’d already been off my prescription for three months. “Hang on,” I said. “Could you give me a blood test first?” When the results came back, he was amazed that my total blood cholesterol was lower than when I’d been on statins. After three months without the pills, it was 5.4mmol/l (5.4 millimoles per litre of blood) compared with 5.7 mmol/l a year earlier.
>
> The only major changes I’d made to my lifestyle since coming off statins were eliminating sugar (including alcohol and starchy foods such as bread) and eating more animal fat. Many experts now believe that sugar is emerging as a true villain in the heart-disease story; while after decades of demonisation, saturated fat has been acquitted of causing heart disease by a recent “meta” analysis of 70 studies by Cambridge University.
>
> Typically, I was eating red meat three or four times a week and enjoying butter, full-fat milk and plenty of eggs. ...

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/health/10717431/Why-Ive-ditched-statins-for-good.html](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/health/10717431/Why-Ive-ditched-statins-for-good.html)

The background: there's a big push on now in the U.K. to help the the pharmaceutical companies get their hands on NHS (National Health Service) money via changed criteria on the prescription of statins:

> Until 2005, statins were prescribed only to those with at least a 30 per cent or greater risk of having a heart attack within 10 years. This was then reduced to a 20 per cent risk. Now, draft NHS guidelines would have them dished out to those with just a 10 per cent risk — in other words, most men over the age of 50 and most women over the age of 60.

### Lahelada — 2014-03-25T11:24:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vascular-surgeon-why-ive-ditched-statins-for-good.10317/post-117826>

Terrible. I used to do a wellknown diet but when the "inventor" a doc started pushing statins I stopped listening.
