# Thread: Interview with Dr. Thomas Cowan
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-25T15:21:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-117852>

There are so many good podcast interviews out there. I was quite pleased to find this one, because I always liked Dr. Cowan's stuff. This is quite a wide-ranging talk. Much of it is about a book he wrote with Sally Fallon on childcare. So, for example, he says:

> ... it’s just wonderful to be with a child and let a child find their own way – it’s the opposite of the ADD culture which is put a child in a walker before they can walk and teach them languages before they know anything and teach them to read when they’re 3 and this whole pre-school. You know the more we teach people, the stupider and less educated they get.

He's also good on the issue of desertifaction, saying much the same as Joel Salatin:

> ... we have a problem of desertifying the earth ... This has happened over the history of agriculture to the point where there’s already – like Iraq, which used to be the fertile crescent. And now it’s happening in Kansas and all kinds of places. And the only thing that reverses that is large amount of herbivorous animals grazing on the land. So the other reason why you don’t find this in traditional cultures is that in order to have a truly sustainable land base, you have to have herbivorous animals grazing the land. There is simply no other choice. And so just for ecological purposes, the irony of this of course is all these people saying that because of global warming and because of environmental destruction and we should all be vegetarian. It’s of course exactly the opposite, and for people who don’t know about that, **just think for example of the great plains in the United States which were basically from Minnesota to Gulf of Mexico from the Mississippi River to the Rocky Mountains – this expanse of grassland – when the early settlers got there, they all said the top soil was 10 feet deep and there was literally millions and millions of buffalo and other grazing animals.** So then hundred years of converting this grassland into corn and soy beans and wheat which is exactly what the vegetarians tell us to eat; grains and beans, then it became a dust \[bowl\].

How about a nice picture of a buffalo hunt?

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Actually, not much soil in that picture, but it's still an attractive reminder of a lost ecosystem.

> ... here’s the sort of big lesson – humans are part of that system, the native people knew they were made of buffalo, the buffalo were made of grass, and this whole cycle continues. We are made of herbivorous animals – their fat and their proteins, that’s what builds our bodies.

He's also very interesting on vaccines. I always used to assume that the "conventional" view on vaccination was right. I think that was partly because you only get given about four over here -- and three of those come together. And you kind of assume they work. So why not? However, the explosion in the number of vaccinations being offered in the U.S. seems quite alarming. Apparently, there's a legal reason for this:

> By the way, it’s also interesting that while vaccines have driven such a growth in the number of diseases which we can now vaccinate, particularly in the last 10 years. **It’s been an explosion and there’s proposed to be at least 10 or 30 more diseases which we can vaccinate against**. And you know part of the reason for that is the part of the US Patriot Act ... part of \[the\] Patriot Act was exempting vaccine manufacturers from any damages in perpetuity, meaning forever from injury caused by vaccine. ...

Therefore, it makes economic sense for companies to develop vaccines -- because it's all profit and no risk of prosecution even if your vaccines kills or cripples people. What a crazy situation!

Lots more interesting stuff here:

[http://www.liveto110.com/nourishing-traditions-of-baby-and-child-care-with-dr-thomas-cowan/](http://www.liveto110.com/nourishing-traditions-of-baby-and-child-care-with-dr-thomas-cowan/)

### nonchalant — 2014-03-25T16:52:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-117868>

I hadn't heard that about the Patriot Act and vaccines. Thanks, Mike.

### sooperb — 2014-03-25T22:54:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-117905>

I think I have his book, is he Thomas Cowan MD?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-26T09:06:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-118028>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=117905)
>
> I think I have his book, is he Thomas Cowan MD?

That could well be him. He is a medical doctor.

Is the book _The Fourfold Path to Healing_? That's the one he co-wrote with Sally Fallon of the WAPF and a movement therapist called Jaimen McMillan. In fact, his website is called that:

[http://fourfoldhealing.com](http://fourfoldhealing.com)

### sooperb — 2014-03-26T10:02:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-118031>

Yep, that's the one. I read some of his book, well quite a lot, as I thought he was on the same wavelength. He is to a point but if I remember rightly, our paths diverged somewhere. I might get the book out and have another read. I used to email updates once in a while but it's been a long time since the last one.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-26T10:29:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-118033>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=118031)
>
> Yep, that's the one. I read some of his book, well quite a lot, as I thought he was on the same wavelength. He is to a point but if I remember rightly, our paths diverged somewhere. I might get the book out and have another read. I used to email updates once in a while but it's been a long time since the last one.

Yes, I get his email updates, but they seem to be pretty rare.

I bought that book after reading _Nourishing Traditions_. I like the broad approach the book has -- in theory. So IIRC he tries to come at health from the point of view of nutrition, medical healing, movement, and exercises in reflecting in certain ways intended to change one's thinking. All very interesting, but I don't actually know I've ever used anything in it.

But I had just the same thought as you: I was just thinking I might re-read it.

His thinking seems to be strongly influenced by Rudolf Steiner's. Maybe Sally Fallon's is, too.

### Gracelind — 2014-03-26T11:28:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-118036>

My son and I attended the Fourfold Path to Healing Conference several years ago. It's more intimate than the Wise Traditions WAPF with closer access to Sally Fallon, Dr Tom Cowan and Jaimen McMillan.

About the movement sessions--I had really glossed over that part of the _Fourfold_ book but in person Jaiman is engaging, welcoming, and very healing in his manner. I talked to him about my balance issues (due to quads atrophied by autoimmune myositis) and he gave me some insightful suggestions. His knowledge of the mechanics of how the body moves is masterful and his presentations draw his audience in. For mid-60s he moves with smooth, flexible, lithe and athletic grace--for many years he was a master fencer.

Most of the movements are in the book but there's nothing seeing them presented well in person.

None of these three, of course, work at the electron/proton/photon level. When Jaimen speaks of energy he's clearly talking about a different level--even a difference definition of energy--more of the spirit.

Dr Cowan puts many of his patients on the GAPS diet and also uses low dose LDN. My son's former roommates fed their baby the Cowan/GAPS/Nourishing Traditions menu and he's quite a healthy little guy with beautiful teeth.

My son and I also attended the Wise Traditions conference in Dallas several years ago. The babies there were all beautifully healthy with happy dispositions. I only heard one baby cry in three days and that when Daddy leaned over too far and the baby rolled out of the Snugli. Tom and Sally must be doing something right for such a bright, happy, curious crop of kids to grow up fed and raised by their protocols.

### sooperb — 2014-03-26T12:47:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-118038>

Sounds like an all round good experience. The States has so many enlightened practitioners, the UK doesn't seem to have any, not that I've seen. All the books I've read on health, bar one, have been written by Americans. I have a few Patrick Holford books, one is interesting "Food is better medicine than Drugs", it is quite hard to get past his reputation as a charlatan though. Personally I've found his stuff quite interesting, especially his work on homocysteine.

I will be getting the book out again, you can't beat personal recommendation

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-26T21:02:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-118107>

> [Gracelind said:](/goto/post?id=118036)
>
> My son and I attended the Fourfold Path to Healing Conference several years ago. It's more intimate than the Wise Traditions WAPF with closer access to Sally Fallon, Dr Tom Cowan and Jaimen McMillan.

I've just started re-reading the book, Gracelind. I just put it down and logged on, because I just came across something interesting almost immediately ...

> None of these three, of course, work at the electron/proton/photon level. When Jaimen speaks of energy he's clearly talking about a different level--even a difference definition of energy--more of the spirit.

Or another way of putting it would be that they don't talk in those terms, although doubtless many of the things they do would get a nod of approval from Jack, who does.

But anyway -- here's the passage that struck my eye. It's on page 3 of the book _Fourfold Healing_:

> … there actually is some scientific evidence for the existence of non-physical-life-force bodies in all living organisms … Harold Saxton-Burr, a professor of anatomy at Yale University, spent many years researching what he called "fields of life" … He proposed that all living things were molded and controlled by "electrodynamic fields" … analogous to magnetic fields ...

Now I've no idea how closely what someone like Becker found relates to what this guy Harold Saxton-Burr was saying. And further than that just how closely that would map onto Rudolf Steiner's notion of the "Life-Force Body" is a big question.

It's really easy for people to take theories that have some vague similarity hold them up together and say "There! You see the connection?" (as Umberto Eco puts it) when there really is none. People do it all the time. But it's not a substitute for an argument showing the connection, as Eco says.

That said, Dr. Cowan does seem to be feeling his way towards the electromagnetic effect.

# Thread: crashing kid/labs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crashing-kid-labs.10319/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 7

### cantweight — 2014-03-25T18:55:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crashing-kid-labs.10319/post-117877>

You all have a way of pointing in me in a direction I can't see on my own....

My youngest son (age 9)and I are very similar we have the poorest redox in the family, we put on weight easily, everything seems to effect us.

We have moved mountains with this kid....cleared him of multiple tics, lots of neuro stuff....speaking, walking, bet wetting. He has a magnetico and had a massive detox on it and has seemed to show improvement.

Summer into fall he has been amazing. We've pushed to keep him afloat this winter and were doing ok but have come to a screeching halt. For about the last 8 weeks everything has started to unravel.

His t3 is low, 4 is fine so not converting.
D is low
Mag is low
HHV6 very high
B12 high
BUN/Creatinine ratio 25.5

He is complaining of constant stomach aches. Stuttering again which brings anxiety about school and possibly the root of stomach aches. He has put on about 15lbs. He is throwing screaming tantrums....polar opposite of his usual sweet composed self. Its like he is out of his mind when this is happening, he is just ballistic, like his brain is on fire. Highly agitated and irritable. In the blink of an eye he has become this monster of a child and I am floundering with how to help.

We are still waiting on C4a and mold panel....

4.3 mmol/L range 3.3-5.3

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crashing-kid-labs.10319/post-117880>

Im thinking anti-viral to bring down the hhv6, but that is a knee jerk reaction I am trying to change.

Sitting here with thyroid rx for him but dont want to give it.

I know there is a better answer Im just not coming up with it. If the damn sun would just come out....maybe that would help!?!?

### Josh — 2014-03-25T19:16:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crashing-kid-labs.10319/post-117881>

Parasites....they take everything down fast in a sensitive system....everything is a band-aid if there is an overwhelming inflammatory driver like an infection inflaming the guts...

Other acute infections as well, I saw my dad go down with lyme's in PA at x-mas in 2012

# Thread: Seasonal Effects

**Subforum:** Redox Rx
**Posts:** 30
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-03-25T19:22:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-effects.10320/post-117883>

Change of season=more IR/increased photoelectric effect and higher temperatures=a lot of changes to redox

I am becoming more aware of the effect of seasons on things. I noticed a distinct shift around the 21st. It is in Oriental Medicine about seasonal affects on physiology. In terms of our 3 legged stool, we are radically increasing photoelectric eeffect with more IR spectrum light to increase the potential for EZ formation and increased electron flow with upregulation of metabolic/mitochondrial function all over the place. In OM, the "liver" takes the hit and loses its ability to regulate the "smooth" flow of energy. The result is the "yang rises". I interpret this as, all of the increased metabolism brings a double load on the liver organ to detoxify more metabolic waste/byproducts and to support both more anabolism and catabolism. Since we did not sleep for the last 3-4 months in the dark and cold to reset all of our LR and IR and other resistance issues while burning off our toxin-laden fat; the liver is not typically ready for this, especially in those of us predisposed to having limits upon these "methylation" activities. Thus we lose our accustomed neurotransmitter cycling, we see different and increased needs for things that support metabolism such as magnesium and perhaps trace elements and B vitamins and we may actually get more inflamed pushing us into LR and IR due to the effects of the brain and the Leptin receptors. Of course, in the world around us, others are experiencing similar effects and may begin to behave in a manner that challenges us a little more to the point where we feel enough stress to create SCN issues that reduce or eliminate autophagy and well we are "off to the races" so to speak....

On the other end of the spectrum, those who may not have "methylation" limitations and who are blessed with robust metabolic and liver biotransformation function may experience an overall sense of heightened well-being, creativity and productivity-the basic 'heaven or hell" based on one's redox potential.

[http://straightbamboo.com/how-to-stay-healthy-in-spring/](http://straightbamboo.com/how-to-stay-healthy-in-spring/)

So as I sit "sleepless in Sedona", I ponder these "truths" and look back at the few thousand years where men and women have noticed this phenomena and wonder how I may use my own apparent limitations as an opportunity to learn and grow...sometimes I really hate this part and would prefer to just sleep through it...Unfortunately, I do not seem to have that choice. I will go back to Peter Lane to see if I can get a little deeper into my own mitochondria, their limitations and their possibilities to fuel something more than up late at night on the computer pondering being up late at night-now actually early in the morning on the computer......on we go.....

Perhaps I burned a little too much "midnight oil" digging into epigenetics, cancer and such over the last couple weeks in response to my rising "yang" and now I have to pay a little for it.....I only learn by breaking things.....It was worth it...As I have heard said "every breakthrough is preceded by a breakdown....". Caution though, "every breakdown is not followed by a breakthrough..." we need to chose our "breakdowns" carefully.

### Josh — 2014-03-25T19:36:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-effects.10320/post-117885>

I feel this is important here as well from the

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/seasonal-decline-in-health.8582/#post-99241](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/seasonal-decline-in-health.8582/#post-99241)

> This is gonna take a while........Google triathalon deaths or cardiac death in winter. You will see that triathalon deaths occur mostly during the swim or immediately after it. Why? It is the same reason you face your seasonal demise but how it happens in you is a bit different. These athletes are all carb adapted because of their beliefs tied to performance. This changes the protein confirmations on most of the proteins used in the mitochondria. The cytochromes being the most important where quantum tunneling occurs. Once electrons are tunneled they head toward oxygen to reduce it. The inner mitochondrial membrane also water micelle's around it they have polarized water around it that contain's protons that are transfered to the ATPase to make ATP. When they are in a warm environment their proteins have a certain confirmation because they are dehydrated from the task but also because they are living carbs 90% of the time. In the warm environment it is not a problem for them because they are recycling ATP via the fast pathways you learned about in EMF 4. Remember what I taught you in the EE series.
>
> As temperature goes up entropy increases. When entropy increases, water density is less. When water density is less, the water contains less oxygen. When oxygen is lowered less ATP is made. This means that in a warm environment you are running more hypoxic and you perceive a need for carbs to offset this set of circumstances. As long as all those condition stay the same the body and system adapts. When they hit the water or cold temperature they immediately leave a warmer environment during the run or the bike ride........This radically changes the situation to the living system. Remember what I taught you from the CT series. In water heat transfers occur 24 times more quickly than they do in air. The sensory systems in the PNS are all designed from an evolutionary standpoint to respond to **environmental transient changes and not the status quo.** This is the essence of sensory receptor adaptation.
> This is where the shit begins to hit the fan for the athlete or person in cold when they are carb adapted. The cold receptors sense the transient change and send it to the brain and the mitochondria in coherent water. The water surrounds the mitochondrial membrane. This temperature change changes the molecular arrangements of proteins and fats in the outer and inner mitochondrial membrane. It also changes the molecular structure bonding angles, and the rotations of the bonds in these molecules. This implies there is electronic, molecular, and rotational changes in these proteins to alter its function in a quantized fashion.
>
> So the body, specifically the mitochondria, begin rapidly to sense this "change in matter" and the proteins and water around the mitochondria react in quantum fashion to this change. But what can not happen in these people? They need ATP to unfold their new protein confirmation to bind more water inside the cell to meet the new environmental demands. They can not do it for two reasons. They are dehydrated and they do not have the ability to make enough ATP to match the protein and water changes inside the cell to the radical temperature change, so what happens? They make less ATP and transfer less energy via the water micelles and this all accelerates the process of hypoxia. Another big thing happens. In temps below 62 degrees insulin no longer works in a mammalian cell. If you have no insulin you have no way to work the Krebs cycle to bring metabolic intermediates to the mitochondria. Since all they eat is carbs and protein they have no substrate ready to make ATP. So the cold alone, runs them dry of energy substrates to make ATP very quickly because of their mismatched fuel choices. The cold hastens this demise. This is why ketosis is linked with cold temps in nature all the times. As it steepens their muscles begin to hurt but eventually it alters their ability to think because they just think this is a temporary thing they experience from the grueling race. Soon their heart become starved for O2 while they continue the swim. The environmental signals are massive because of the way heat is transferred in water so rapidly. When they emerge from the water the environment changes just as drastically back to the warm........from cold. They have an immediate spike in Oxygen reduction in the face of a cytosol and mitochondria now adapted to severe hypoxia. This is the ultimate environmental mismatch that their body is not prepared for on such short time scales. When this occurs, oxygen becomes toxic as the hydroxyl free radical.......I taught you about this in the Sept webinar. When this happens it immediately changes the ability to quantum tunnel electrons in the cytochrome proteins because they are loaded with transition metals. This immediate causes the ability to make no ATP. When ATP is not made in the mitochondria it causes a cell to lose its meta stability (read the Q & A October webinar thread here to get the full impact) and the cell begins to reach true equilibrium.
>
> When this happens the heart and skeletal muscles all release water and potassium and become rigid. They can no longer contract and the heart stops in diastolic failure. We call this failing in autophagy. This is precisely how one dies of an AI disease or from sleep apnea. The only difference is the time scales. That last sentence should have caught your attention by design Jason..........you and I both know your deal. Im not going to rehash it here. Suffice to say, I think now you can see why their plight and yours are playing ball on the same field of action. Hope this helps.
> Dr. Kruse

### Josh — 2014-03-31T04:44:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-effects.10320/post-118906>

Seasonal Research Free Article:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

> Seasonal cycles of adiposity and body weight reflecting changes in both food intake and energy expenditure are the norm in mammals that have evolved in temperate and polar habitats. Innate circannual rhythmicity and direct responses to the annual change in photoperiod combine to ensure that behavior and energy metabolism are regulated in _anticipation_ of altered energetic demands such as the energetically costly processes of hibernation, migration, and lactation. In the last decade, major progress has been made into identifying the central mechanisms that underlie these profound long-term changes in behavior and physiology. Surprisingly they are distinct from the peptidergic and aminergic systems in the hypothalamus that have been identified in studies of the laboratory mouse and rat and implicated in timing meal intervals and in short-term responses to caloric restriction. Comparative studies across rodents, ungulates and birds reveal that tanycytes embedded in the ependymal layer of the third ventricle play a critical role in seasonal changes because they regulate the local availability of thyroid hormone. Understanding how this altered hormonal environment might regulate neurogenesis and plasticity in the hypothalamus should provide new insight into development of strategies to manage appetite and body weight.

[http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0018506X14000506](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0018506X14000506)

### Josh — 2014-03-31T05:01:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-effects.10320/post-118913>

So here is my "seasonal" hypothesis for me this year...As noted above, I perceived a change in function right around the equinox and since then, there have been a steady set of "Liver-Yang Rising" s/sx's. I translate these as the detoxification and metabolic demands on my liver exceed the capacity of my likely to have methylation issues liver to handle all of the demands. Once upon a time, I would have slept through the Winter in the cold, dissolved all of my toxic fat and detoxed most everything away before I awoke. In my post eutherian world, I do not get the blessings of the long nap and Spring arrives to find my body a bit toxic and not especially well rested. In spite of liberal amounts of CT and deep CT with a primarily ketogenic diet, I am no where near as lean and mean as a grizzly emerging from the den. So my poor liver has to deal with more electrons from the strong AZ sun at altitude (4300 feet) and warmer temperatures upregulating function and entropy simultaneously. So for me with my "predispositions", I get to experience stuff as the system struggles to find and regain metastability. I do actually find that I need less food because I probably am getting more from the sun walking around in shorts without a shirt most of the time. I am further guessing that this phenomena is the origin of the tradition of "cleansing" or "detoxing" in Spring to rid the body of the accumulated toxic burden that used to be relieved by hibernation. This would support the liver to handle the increased metabolic function from more available photoelectric energy without being compromised by accumulated toxins from the long winter of hypofunction and potential metabolic backlog.

In concert with this, I notice that I need to be very diligent with the key methylation bypass supplements: 5 MTHF, Molybdenum, Manganese and Lithium. Most "dysfunctiuon" seems to resolve quickly with a dose of one or more of these. I take molybdenum and manganese at bedtime to insure that when my liver revs up at 3AM (liver time in Chinese Medicine) there is some around to make the enzymes to do the work...seems to make a difference in whether I wake up. My N=1 for now.....

Sooner or later to honor my profession, I will need to go back and cross reference the methylation issues with the syndromes in Oriental Medicine and the herbal formulas prescribed for these syndromes.

Worked this weekend... The first question, have you been abroad? No i have spring in my life, my answer... The second one, damn you worked with four clients at the same time, how do you manage? Easy, when adding up two or more i get them to resonate. People open up to each other and fill in their own blanks, i show them their shadows, put a little candle there so they get a new perspective.

For so many years now i lived in sync with seasons, i have invested all my time in this, making them a part of me, or rather me a part of them. It is quite easy, if you accept them.

Spring - Waking up, dropping the dust of winter from myself... Slowly my skin gets bronzed, charging the surface so it activates my inner. Seeing, hearing, smelling spring is more important than eating... Sensing the sun that together with the cold creates huge difference in me.

Summer - All my life is about elements, to the fullest... I do my runs, my hiking, my diving, my climbing AND my living, all in. Soon i can get extra bonus from berries but it will have to wait, end of august... All days are easy, free energy is everywhere.

Fall - The load up, extra stored sun is everywhere... Water is charged thru summer, berries are the suns batteries. The air is super crisp, i fill myself with all this air that is cleaned from my trees. Air in summer is filled with so much life but the air in fall feels like a wake up call, winters is coming... I scent the first snowfall.

Winter - Magic winter land, black and white only... Nothing is screaming for attention, it is almost dead. The cold makes my summer meaningful, all the fun must now reverse... The memories clear up, mind becomes relaxed, cold makes me almost walk thru life like a dream... I get a reset of my body and mind, the big exhale.

# Thread: First human epigenetic linkage of neonatal glucose to mom's IS
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-human-epigenetic-linkage-of-neonatal-glucose-to-moms-is.10323/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-26T01:19:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-human-epigenetic-linkage-of-neonatal-glucose-to-moms-is.10323/post-117944>

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2014-03-response-unborn-children-glucose-mother.html](http://medicalxpress.com/news/2014-03-response-unborn-children-glucose-mother.html)

A study recently published in Diabetologia (the journal of the European Association for the Study of Diabetes) is the first to provide direct evidence that fetal brain response to a dose of sugar given orally to its mother is associated with the mother's insulin sensitivity. This may indicate that the risk of subsequent obesity and diabetes may be pre-programmed in the womb. The study is by Dr Hubert Preissl and Dr Andreas Fritsche, University of Tübingen, Germany and German Center for Diabetes Research (DZD), Neuherberg, Germany, and colleagues.

This is the first human study to provide direct evidence that response of unborn children to glucose is associated with mother's insulin sensitivity

Diabetes or obesity in the mother are already known to influence fetal development and subsequent development after birth. Children of obese or diabetic mothers have an increased risk for type 2 diabetes and obesity in adulthood, independent of their genetic background. Further to this, the prevalence of obesity and type 2 diabetes mellitus is rising worldwide and the percentage of young people affected is increasing. The reasons for these changes are unclear, although environmental and epigenetic mechanisms (where environmental factors affect genetics) are likely to be involved. A major epigenetic mechanism is the so-called fetal programming system, whereby the mother's exposure to environmental factors can affect programming of the genes in her unborn child.

In this new study, Preissl and colleagues aimed to show that the metabolism of a pregnant woman, following a meal, influences fetal brain activity. A total of 13 healthy pregnant women underwent an oral glucose tolerance test (75 g of glucose delivered orally, a standard method for determining insulin sensitivity). Insulin sensitivity was determined by glucose and insulin measurements at 0, 60 and 120 min. At each time point, the response of the fetus was examined by recording fetal brain responses elicited by sounds with a magnetoencephalographic device.

The researchers found that after 60 minutes, women who were more insulin resistant had fetuses that reacted more slowly to the sound test. When divided into two groups based on insulin sensitivity, the insulin-resistant mothers had fetuses that reacted to the sound at an average of 283 milliseconds, compared with 178 ms for the insulin-sensitive group.

They suggest that the findings support a hypothesis first made almost 50 years ago (1967) by scientist Jørgen Pedersen. The authors say: "It is possible that insulin-resistant mothers have higher glucose levels accompanied by increased insulin levels after a meal. As glucose passes the placenta, these increased glucose levels induce excess insulin (hyperinsulinaemeia) in the fetus. Therefore, high insulin levels in the mother may correspond to high insulin levels in the fetus."

They add: "It is possible that high insulin levels are a prerequisite for appropriate brain maturation. However, chronic hyperinsulinaemia, which is present in insulin-resistant mothers and corresponds to high insulin levels in the fetus, might induce insulin resistance in the fetal brain."

The authors also discuss other possible reasons for the difference in audio response times, including that the insulin resistance of the mother may be associated with limited insulin transport into the fetal brain; alternatively, the insulin resistance of the mother may be associated with a variety of other hormonal and metabolic fuel effects that control the response of the fetal brain.

The consequences for the unborn child are also spelt out, with the authors saying: "The insulin resistance of the fetal brain may be interpreted as metabolic imprinting of insulin resistance with important consequences for later life. The consequent effect of hyperinsulinaemia on fetal development has already been shown. Compared with newborns of non-diabetic women, children of diabetic mothers with poorly controlled glycaemia show neurophysiological impairment and have a higher risk for metabolic syndrome, obesity and type 2 diabetes mellitus in later life."

The authors conclude: "Lower maternal insulin sensitivity is associated with slower fetal brain responses. These findings provide the first evidence of a direct effect of maternal metabolism on fetal brain activity and suggest that central insulin resistance may be programmed during fetal development."

### Andrea — 2014-03-26T04:18:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-today.10324/post-118006>

> [Aggie said:](/goto/post?id=117989)
>
> I was hoping to speak directly with Ben - he was talking about SuperSlow which is the only exercise to do - crossfit and HITT are more dangerous than helpful. rare to find anyone familar with SuperSlow.  ugh

I didn't get any email either...

Anyway, Super Slow is a training protocol described in Doug McGuff's and John Little's book ["Body by Science"](http://www.bodybyscience.net/). The purpose, basically, is to lift as heavy as possible as slowly as possible for as long as possible. That way you engage both slow, intermediate and fast twitch muscles, until exhaustion.
Here's a video where Doug McGuff demonstrates their "Big 5" protocol:

### tellmisty — 2014-03-26T15:25:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-today.10324/post-118047>

Hi Patrick,

I am not sure what the deliverability issues is, but you are right: emails were being delivered fine and now there seems to be an issue. All emails show sent, but when we dig deeper, we see that some people are not getting their emails delivered. We are escalating that problem right now with the service we use and hopefully we'll get that figured out soon.

All Gold Klub members can upgrade right now to Optimal Reset PREMIUM and get all of the webinars at your fingertips. Click on Member Login, above, login to your account, and from your dashboard, follow the prompts to sign up for Optimal Reset Premium. Message me privately if you have any trouble and I'll make sure you get in.

We are checking on the email issue and hopefully can have that resolved quickly.

Aggie, I will personally share your question with Ben and report back if I can get a reply from him.  We are announcing all Optimal Reset webinars in the Announcement bar at the top of the JackKruse.com website, and we also announce all webinars on the [Optimal Reset Facebook page](https://www.facebook.com/groups/469730323136938/) to get the word out about what's coming up. You can keep tabs here and hopefully we can figure out what's happening with the email issue ASAP.

### tellmisty — 2014-03-26T16:55:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-today.10324/post-118058>

Patrick, click the "Forgot Password" link and you can reset your password.

I am sorry you are frustrated ... we have not changed anything recently. For the Gold Klub, we deliver all webinars and recordings to your email so you do not have to ever login to your dashboard. The Optimal Reset is a FREE program, let's not forget. And your upgrade into Optimal Reset Premium as a Gold Klub member is also free.

You do not have to be a member of the facebook groups or anything, really, if you do not want to hassle with those channels. If you DO want the info from those channels, however, you have to learn to work with the technology to get it. Everyone in this community is different as far as how they want to interact and communicate. Some hate "email only" because they don't keep track of their emails well. They love the dashboards where they can just login. Some love having Facebook because they don't come to the forums. There is no way for me or anyone to please every person ... you all have very different needs and ways you want to receive the info. So we are doing our best to support all the shades of people who are part of this community.

If you cannot get in to your dashboard ([you can login here](http://www.jackkruse.com/members/wp-login.php)), follow the Forgot Password link and it will help you reset it. Email me directly and I'll try to help you further: [misty@jackkruse.com](mailto:misty@jackkruse.com).

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-26T18:27:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-today.10324/post-118076>

Same here, like last week with Dr Patel's podcast. I bet the staff will solve it quickly like the last time

To be more constructive, I agree that the current method for the delivering of the contents is a little bit counter intuitive. Have you considered setting up an iTunes Podcast Channel like the one Jimmy Moore and many other's have? It's not that I like iTunes a lot but I must admit that in this area is quite a nice solution. I guess it will offer the option to allow only people who is enrolled in a premium plan if needed (like the gold club here), but I know it's more convenient to have your own plataform set up if able to, but this requieres it to make it work optimally (pun intended hehe). BG's blog solution for his podcasts is quite nice also, I bet you already considered it since these sites are quite related.

Just only one more thing (a little bit offtopic). The webinar files weight a lot. 300+ MB for a voice recording mp3 is a killer IMHO. If you know how, it'd be interesting to make the conversion to mp3 with an algorithm set to low quality and low bitrate, audio conversations need no more than this, it will make no audible difference quality-wise, but the same file could be just 20MB or so for an 1h audio, it's quite a difference. With nowadays' bandwith its not much of a problem, but if you plan to let them sit on your mp3 player and is a cheap one, it consumes a lot of space. I like to keep them instead of cycling them, you never know when you'll want to re-hear one of the webinars again

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-26T20:56:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-today.10324/post-118104>

I went back to all my emails - and show that the last email I received was on 3/14. I have seen the posts on Facebook for webinars. The challenge is not everyone is on Facebook so not every one will see the information.

I have multiple emails (which I hate) but I need to to separate junk from stuff I care about. I've had to go back through and tag certain things so I know where to find them... like sign-ins etc. I will say that is a PITA.

Members should have the ability to sign-in their account - that takes them to their dashboard. That linkage should be able to interface from the forum, as well as the main page (incase you're reading a blog and Jack mentions a specific webinar & you want to access it from where you're reading)

Emails - fall into multiple categories for me: READ ASAP. READ at leisure, File to read later (Keep marked as un-read), or delete. I know I've kept things to read later that I never get to... nor do I have time to go back and find the info.... I'd bet there are lots of folks here who are in the same boat....

### tellmisty — 2014-03-27T21:48:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-today.10324/post-118357>

Alright.

1) **There is a link to the Optimal Reset Login in the header.** You can login there to see upcoming webinars and listen to webinars that are in the replay window. If you don't know your password, click the "Lost my password" link and you'll receive an email to reset your password.

2) **Silver / Gold / Platinum Klub Login.** If you are a Silver Klub, Gold Klub or Platinum Klub member, you can login to your dashboard by clicking the "Member Login" link in the header. If you don't know your password, click the "Lost my password" link and you'll receive an email to reset your password. Here, you will be able to access all your webinars that are included with your membership.

3) **Gold / Platinum Klub & Optimal Reset Premium.** If you are a Gold Klub or Platinum Klub member, login to your dashboard and scroll below your webinars to a section for the Optimal Reset. There is a button you can click to upgrade to the full premium version of the Optimal Reset.

4) **If you are a Silver Klub or Gold Klub member, _you do not automatically get the Optimal Reset emails._** If you want those emails, you will need to sign up for the Optimal Reset: [www.90dayoptimalreset.com](http://www.90dayoptimalreset.com).

5) **The email issue has been tracked down and fixed.** We are going to do a replay weekend for you guys to give everyone the opportunity to catch the webinars affected by the email issue THIS WEEKEND. An email will go out about this, but you do not need an email to participate. Simply login to your Optimal Reset dashboard as described above during the replay window (which will be tomorrow, Friday March 28 from 10 am CST until Monday, March 31 at 10 am CST) and you'll be able to listen to the replays. The webinars that will be included in this replay weekend include:

Ben Greenfield
Dr. Bhavesh Patel
Michael Neuert

6) I believe we have also fixed the issue Aggie was dealing with related to being able to login to the Silver Klub dashboard and the weird link. Aggie, if you still have any issues, please message me privately and I will personally help you out.

We are working hard to get through the tickets that have been submitted. This email issue resulted in an unusually high volume of tickets so we're doing the best we can! Hopefully, these instructions will help many of you get what you need so you can access the content you're looking for.

Thanks for your patience, everyone.

Misty

I ask this because there are so many supplements being sold for telomere lengthening. But I am guessing you would say improve redox potential would be a more potent way to reverse telomeres.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-26T04:19:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeric-stress-causes-wide-spread-dna-damage.10327/post-118007>

I liked a comment you made in a webinar or podcast in which you connected disease reversal to "quantum time". I can't remember the exact comment. # Thread: Iron levels
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-levels.10330/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ken23 — 2014-03-26T19:34:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-levels.10330/post-118090>

In EE4 Dr. K writes about iron being critical for thyroid and temp. My question is what is an optimal ferritin level on a blood test?

I took a look at my blood work and showed ferritin at 84 (30-400 ng/ml). Not sure if this may have anything to do with my cold hands/ feet, hairloss, dry skin.

### JanSz — 2014-03-26T20:32:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-levels.10330/post-118101>

> [Ken23 said:](/goto/post?id=118090)
>
> In EE4 Dr. K writes about iron being critical for thyroid and temp. My question is what is an optimal ferritin level on a blood test?
>
> I took a look at my blood work and showed ferritin at 84 (30-400 ng/ml). Not sure if this may have anything to do with my cold hands/ feet, hairloss, dry skin.

[http://www.ironoverload.org/information/diagnosis-pt1.html](http://www.ironoverload.org/information/diagnosis-pt1.html)

Iron workup:
• Iron and Iron Binding Capacity
• Iron, Total
• Ferritin
• Transferrin
• Folate, RBC & Hematocrit
• Hemoglobin A1c (496X)
• Hemoglobin, Plasma (514X)

Thyroid workup
T3, Total
T4, Total (Thyroxine)
T3, Free
T4,Free
T3, Reverse
Ultrasensitive TSH
Thyroid Peroxidase and Thyroglobulin Antibodies

\--------------------------------------------
cold hands/ feet try

Lugol's solution
Selenium

....

.

### Ken23 — 2014-03-26T23:25:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-levels.10330/post-118140>

Thanks Jansz. Tried iodine.... Hands and feet got colder

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-27T00:22:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vegetarian-diet.10333/post-118146>

A new Austrian study compares a few diets and determines that a vegetarian diet is the least healthy:
"Our study has shown that Austrian adults who consume a vegetarian diet are less healthy (in terms of cancer, allergies, and mental health disorders), have a lower quality of life, and also require more medical treatment."

[http://www.plosone.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pone.0088278#abstract0](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0088278#abstract0)

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: March 2014 Webinar promo
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-2014-webinar-promo.10335/>
**Subforum:** Redox Rx
**Posts:** 85
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-27T01:56:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-2014-webinar-promo.10335/post-118175>

Rarely............do I let the cat out of the bag............But the March 2014 webinar is one I wanted to do after we cleared the hurdles of what cysteine and water mean to the redox potential of human cells.

Many of you come on here and do not like when I hound you on details of wellness. When I tell you how the redox potential is critical to gaining it.

In March, I introduce you to someone who can never have an optimal redox potential because he does not the have the ability to have what most of you have. Instead, he sees himself where he is now. He deals with his present moment because that is all he and his parents were promised by my profession.

Jeremy Thomley has a fatal disease. That is what they say.....................

I say, Jeremy has the proof that anyone can overcome a poor redox potential and still succeed. Jeremy is the optimal I seek in my own life. he has figured out how to use the environment to maximize energy flows to and from his mitochondria with behaviors and instinct no one had to teach him. Today, when he errs I remind him that this is the case.

In fact, I think Jeremy shows what one can do when they believe in themselves. Jeremy sees the world through his fingers and body. He connects with the Earth more than anyone I know. He has no clue why he initially did it or continues to do it. I think I do. We talk about this linkage in this webinar. This is probably one of the best webinars I will do for you because it shows you just what is possible when you believe you are a broken human. Jeremy has always been told he was and could never escape that fate: and it has never stopped him. He does more with less, than most do with their best. Jeremy takes the hand he was dealt and plays it.

He can do more than I can because he made his limitations stretch his canvas to open him to a new world. A world where your disease, your illness, your failings, create strength. He shows us it's not the load that breaks you down, it's the way you carry it that does. He uses touch to overcome his CTFR defect. He can not handle iodine across his basements like you can no matter what he does. It does not break him, it makes him better.

He maximizes touch to overcome it. He knew implicitly as a young child how to overcome his issue. He climbed to be connected with Earth most of his life. When one touches, you gain access to the Earth's current. That current electrifies collagen. Collagen touches every single molecule of water in our body. Water is the most abundant molecule in each of us. Jeremy stumbled into his Rx by climbing and collecting electrons and photons. Jeremy found how to out work his fate. He uses Nature's laws to outwit my profession. They told his mom he would be dead at 6 years old. I met her and heard the story for myself. Today he is 31 years old. In fact, today he is climbing mountains in Utah. He will come home Sunday and soon talk to members about what he found and how he overcomes his redox potential. He accepts no excuses. He only seeks answers. He is someone who has my ultimate respect and admiration. I am pleased to call him my friend.

With touch, the collagen architecture in our body is deformed. When collagen is deformed it releases an electric signal. We call this piezoelectric ability. The pressure, the change in electric and magnetic fields are propagated in collagen to every single crevice of our body. This information is then transferred to all parts of our body including our brain, via water in CSF. On the surface of our brain lies our neocortex. Time after time data has shown no matter how broken our brain is, (think AD or PD) exercise and movement increase the function of our brain. It increases our performance. Stop for a moment and watch Jeremy perform in the video below. Think about the "fasted training thread" post on this very form. Jeremy does all this with hypoxia constantly, not chronically as someone with cancer deals with. I told you in CPC 8 the effect of chronic hypoxia. I want you to examine Jeremy's video below. Do you think he overcomes his genetic defect with awesome epigenetic direction? I think you know what I think about that question.

Touch is one way we can overcome a poor redox potential. Recovering your redox potential is not an environmental story, or a supplement only, or diet only story. I want you to meet my bud. He teaches me every time we gather at my place or at his Christmas tree farm, just what constitutes health. Jeremy has cystic fibrosis. He has accomplished more than most people will ever accomplish, who have a normal genome and are illness free.
CFTR, the gene associated with cystic fibrosis, encodes the protein cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator. The gene was identified in 1989 and is found at 7q31.2, the long arm (q) of chromosome 7 at position 31.2. CFTR regulates the movement of chloride and sodium ions across epithelial membranes, such as the alveolar epithelia located in the [lungs](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lungs). Most people without CF have two working copies of the CFTR gene, and both copies must be missing for CF to develop, due to the disorder's recessive nature. CF develops when neither copy works normally (as a result of mutation) and therefore has [autosomal](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autosome) [recessive](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recessive_gene) inheritance. The protein spans this membrane and acts as a [channel](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ion_channel) connecting the inner part of the cell ([cytoplasm](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cytoplasm)) to the [surrounding fluid](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extracellular_fluid). This channel is primarily responsible for controlling the movement of halogens from inside to outside of the cell; however, in the sweat ducts it facilitates the movement of chloride from the sweat into the cytoplasm. When the CFTR protein does not work, chloride and thiocyanate are trapped inside the cells in the airway and outside in the skin. Most of you following this blog may realize how Jeremy genome hampers him with respect to water chemistry and halogen chemistry. Have a close look at the video below. Is it an impediment? Or can we all learn something from him?

When someone tells Jeremy "no," it doesn't mean he can't do it, it simply means he can't do it with them.
**Good results are difficult when indifference predominates. You must seek your answer. When we are seeking our answer, we cannot be indifferent about the questions we ask, because we want the answer that brings transformation. So, the first questions that lead to the right answers we must ask of ourselves: Am I making a positive deposit into my future? What will I reap from this decision? What is motivating me to take this action? When we ask ourselves the right questions we will lead ourselves to the right answers. The problem is not to find the answer, it's to face the answer. Begin to live with the answer than die with the question.**
Here is my friend Jeremy. Here is the beginning of the March webinar for members in 2014. [http://www.mohawksteelco.com/shop/philosophy-of-touch-promo/](http://www.mohawksteelco.com/shop/philosophy-of-touch-promo/)

### Inger — 2014-03-27T08:36:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-2014-webinar-promo.10335/post-118225>

I cannot wait too...!!! Wow... this is so touching.......

I KNOW it is true what Jack is saying above - like yesterday I was a bit stressed and lost a bit that deep well being feeling because of the work (I have to work on the computer too).. and then I went outside with the sheep in the garden and let them graze under the apple trees me watching. I took off my shoes and socks and went barefoot as I did some garden work (I picked up all the twigs from the lawn, my brother had cut the apple trees last weekend) . OMG how nice the soft grass and moss felt under my feet.. and in that very moment the well being returned - to just make me full of joy. And today I woke up after amazing sleep to one even more amazing dream
It is like... we are meant to touch the earth....!

I am 100% sure healing is very simple... and that alone - makes me jump from joy ..    it is there for everyone to take and enjoy! That is the thing I love the most... it would be so unfair if you could heal only if you had lots of money... I would hate that! Healing... should always be for free  and it is

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-27T13:44:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-2014-webinar-promo.10335/post-118255>

The pressure of adversity is a powerful sustainer of accountability. Our burdens are our where our purpose lie. When you are at the precipus of failure, you recoil from slacking. Fears hold you back. A challenge only becomes an obstacle when you bow to it. The beautiful thing about adversity is they introduce us to our strengths.

### Josh — 2014-03-30T02:15:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-2014-webinar-promo.10335/post-118731>

A physicist, Moishe Feldenkreis, was faced with a knee problem from playing rugby and decided to instead of having surgery to teach himself to move all over again as a baby does. This is now the Feldenkreis system. It evolves with each individual that explores the work.....I have experienced and practiced this, so I have some perspective. Alexander started a related system. Pilates took a different approach...all are based on "sensing yourself".

I studied with one of Mia's students. My teacher is now in Providence, RI: Sumati Eberstadt..

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-01T11:23:37+0300

> What a great idea - thanks. I have wanted to do something with kids. I haven't been able to find my place.

Children seem to like me. I don't know why, but they do.

I'm not one of those people that animals have a great affinity for, though. Some people just seem to be. There seem to be people that cats and dogs will just make a beeline for. They want to approach and be stroked by John or Mary -- and if he or she doesn't much like dogs or is allergic to cats, as sometimes happens, too bad.

One of my sisters when her eldest son was only a few months old went to stay with an aunt and uncle in Essex. It was a big old house that had once been given by Charles II to a mistress. The dogs raced upstairs in the morning to visit Jacob. My aunt was staggered. She said, "They've never done that before \[come upstairs\]. They know they're not allowed to, and they don't."

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-01T12:12:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-2014-webinar-promo.10335/post-119195>

You should publish a paleo cookbook based around foods that are easily obtainable in Australia, Audrey.

I think there's probably a fair-sized market for paleo cookbooks. I heard of another recently:

Peter Servold's _Paleo by Season_

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Paleo-Season-Peter-Servold/dp/1628600063/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/Paleo-Season-Peter-Servold/dp/1628600063/)

I quite like the sound of that one, because I don't think paleo people in general are aware of seasonality. For example, I can't ever recall Cordain mentioning it anywhere: so long as you cut out the "grains, legumes and dairy" and chomp down on "lean \[ugh\] meats, fruit and vegetables" you're doing the right thing really does seem to be his message (tempered with maybe a little fat is OK, these days).

But I don't know of anything written specifically for the Australian audience, although paleo seems to be very popular Down Under.

But maybe there already is, and I just don't know.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-04-01T15:53:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-2014-webinar-promo.10335/post-119210>

Listening to last night's Q&A I sort of got this feeling like when Bob Dylan went 'electric'. A change. Some people loved it but he left some behind too. For me I get a good feeling about it to me Dr Kruse is a 'philosopher' as well as a biologist and his thinking is taking him places maybe he didn't even expect or a lot of other people did not imagine. Biology per se IMO can be very 'reductionist' and literally tried to reduce everything to compounds and chemicals. Dr Kruse has already taken us beyond that and I think it is noticeable even in the 'alternative' health community there is still a heavy reliance on what I consider 'reductionist' thinking. Even most of the guest speakers on the Optimal Reset while I liked them I found them also strangely limited. Or at least nothing so much out of the ordinary or nothing that would really inspire. Dr Kruse 'inspires' but what is so cool is he ALSO is very grounded in 'solid' science.....................it is that combination I find so attractive. And so much of the time I feel we get one or the other but when it is well combined that is magical.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-01T16:09:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-2014-webinar-promo.10335/post-119216>

The problem is many dont see what I see. They want to craft the way the message will unfold or be marketed. This information can not be crafted and it can not be marketed. It is the core of our very nature. At times it will upset you and revolt you but ultimate I will show you why it is this way using the science. A quiet mind is ready to listen..........sometimes our minds are not quiet. I know the way I think and do things is not everyone's cup of tea. I make no apologies. I have my way of doing things and I have reasons for it.............when you get the sense someone is blind to your real goals............its time to shift gears and move on.

SACRIFICE IS PUTTING EVERYONE you know and may love ON A BACK BURNER IN ORDER TO ACCOMPLISH WHAT YOU TRULY BELIEVE IN, WITH OR WITHOUT SUPPORT................support is not necessary. You have to create the correct environment to get the ingredients of the recipe to come out as you see it.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-01T16:17:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-2014-webinar-promo.10335/post-119217>

> \[EMAIL said:
>
> [patgrif@hotmail.com](mailto:patgrif@hotmail.com), post: 119210, member: 604\[/EMAIL\]\]... I think it is noticeable even in the 'alternative' health community there is still a heavy reliance on what I consider 'reductionist' thinking.

IMO, many people in paleo are very much way. I think that tends to be far more so than of people in more established alternative health, like chiropractic. Of course, _they_ often tend towards vegetarianism.

Someone else who does is Dr. Art Ayers. I didn't really see it at first, but it's there. I like him. He posts some interesting stuff, and if you like he's "alternative" inasmuch as he's not the mainstream. But he is reductionist.

It seems to me that this is why he is far happier about GMOs than his readers and also blasé about pesticides. From that point of view, people's worries on these scores are just obscurantism -- or ignorance because they don't know very much about microbiology. A plant's just like a meccano model, isn't it? You can disassemble it and swap parts out with a different meccano model, can't you? Where's the problem? I think Rob Wolf sometimes seems to be coming from a similar place.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-01T17:43:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-2014-webinar-promo.10335/post-119248>

After the webinar last night................Jeremy went to his truck and pulled out a new sculpture he made. It was a blown glass piece blown up by his own valuable breath. It was made to approximate his lung. It was surrounded by copper metal to simulate his lungs. We spent 45 minutes mounting it on a lamp to illuminate the gorgeous colors of blue, gold and green...................and then I smiled. He asked why I was smiling. I put on the April webinar and showed him what played a prominent role in the April webinar story........................copper.

This my friends is quantum biology at work.

It is precisely what the Q & A was hinting at..........................

We all know..............but we are consciously unaware of of the truth. Collecting information and energy is key to unlocking the data your brain collects.

When I could not walk anymore, he refused to leave me and go out with anyone else, and just went in our backyard instead. He was my best friend during that time... and I loved how he tried to help me by licking the painful spots all over me. Miss him so very much....

They do that thing of licking sores and wounds, don't they? In some Ancient Greek temples of Asclepius devoted to healing they used to keep dogs to do that.

I googled for a reference and found one:

> … temples dedicated to Asclepius, their god of medicine and healing, often contained dogs trained to lick wounds. ...
>
> The simple mechanical action of a dog's tongue can be helpful in dealing with a wound. The saliva of a dog's tongues acts to loosen any debris that may be on the surface of the wound. Any dirt or other debris will also become attached to the moisture of the saliva, thus at the very least, the area of the wound will be cleansed.
>
> However the focus of much scientific research has been on the various antibiotic and helpful compounds which are found in a dog's saliva. The healing powers of saliva have long been suspected, because lesions inside the mouth mend more quickly and scar less than wounds on the skin. ...

[http://www.psychologytoday.com/blog/canine-corner/201106/can-dogs-help-humans-heal](http://www.psychologytoday.com/blog/canine-corner/201106/can-dogs-help-humans-heal)

BTW, Kind of you to say what you said, Tanya, but I think it's more how you saw the posts than what I'm like.

### Gracelind — 2014-04-01T21:12:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-2014-webinar-promo.10335/post-119286>

Spent some time with Jeremy's thoughts and videos online last night. Such a profound, interesting, captivating realm in which he lives. I can see the source of the spark he lit in Jack.

His climbing feats would be awesome by any standards but knowing his physical challenges--he is extraordinary. We have been following Jack as he leads us down intricate pathways in the function of "normal" healthy bodies. We here have a range of different ailments that we hope to halt if not reverse. We are here to preserve the health we have and then to reach the optimal that each of us can achieve with our own n=1.

Due to his genetics Jeremy's body cannot use those "normal" pathways. Jack's observation that every day Jeremy lives on Mt Everest is apt in terms of his lung function. He has tapped into different pathways (that we all have but perhaps use less often) to produce the energy his body requires not merely to function, but to magnificently thrive. He has achieved the same level of physical fitness as his climbing buddies. To look at their long limbs, their muscles flexing and straining, their powerful yet graceful movements, you'd never know that his body chemistry functions on a different plane than theirs do. What he adds to the physical is the mental/emotional. Key word: _Intention_.

We're still talking about the same phenomena, at a different "power of ten" than we have been. The collagen and water, the EZ, the magnetism and photons, the glands and hormones, the DNA/RNA, the epigenetics and all the rest--come to life in this powerful example of Jeremy.

Powers of Ten

I think many have problems with those pictures... First they wake something inside of us, a memory form. Then they start to feel that a so called wild animal and human should not interact. And then we get those who are thinking that this is bad for animals, i have friends that could burn those pictures because they thing it is WRONG to do this. I think that you must have felt that connection with wild animals to get what these pictures can say...

For me this is a blend once again, taking that would be normal back then and blending with modern human.

The easiest way to connect with wild animals would not to go into the woods, simply go to the sea. Join a simple class of diving and start explore, animals in the sea is more open to connection, is it because we have a place together with them?

Photo: Hanli Prinsloo

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-15T00:00:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-2014-webinar-promo.10335/post-121506>

And look what shows up today............LOL Tensegrity 1 get a huge boost.

A big piece of a long-standing puzzle—how cells maintain osmotic pressure via volume-regulated anion channels (VRACs)—appears to have been solved, as researchers reported in [_Cell_](http://www.cell.com/cell/abstract/S0092-8674\(14\)00358-4) today (April 10) having identified a key VRAC protein. [Ardem Patapoutian](http://www.scripps.edu/research/faculty/patapoutian) of The Scripps Research Institute in La Jolla, California, and his colleagues found that this protein, which they’ve dubbed “SWELL1,” is a critical component of the osmotic channels that help keep cells from swelling until they explode.

“So far, nobody has been able to identify the proteins involved in mediating this widely-expressed current,” said [Jorg Grandl](http://icru.duhs.duke.edu/grandl/) of Duke University in Durham, North Carolina, who was not involved in this work. “To have a first report about one molecule that’s likely forming an ion channel that is mediating this current is a major step forward.”

Study coauthor [Zhaozhu Qiu](http://www.scripps.edu/patapoutian/people.html), a postdoctoral researcher in Patapoutian’s lab, first encountered VRACs while attending a conference talk that described how, for three decades, researchers have struggled to identify the proteins involved in the channels. VRACs keep the osmotic pressure of cells in check, using anions like chloride or iodide to generate an electrophysiological pulse when they’re open. These channels are ubiquitously expressed, making them difficult to isolate for study. Scientists have suggested several proteins as possible VRAC components, but one after another, each candidate dropped away as it became clear they were not actually involved.

So Qiu and his colleagues devised an assay to identify the proteins involved. They transfected cells with small interfering RNAs (siRNAs) and adapted a yellow fluorescent protein (YFP) to see which genes knocked were down by the siRNAs. Among the 51 genes the researchers homed in on, “there was only one gene that completely abolished the VRAC activity,” said Qiu. “Previously, very little was known of this gene.”

The researchers named the gene _SWELL1_. The same gene had been discovered a decade ago, and linked to B cell development. But little research had been done _SWELL1_ (once known as _LRRC8A_) since then. In 2012, it was found to be related to a class of proteins pannexins.

Using cells in which _SWELL1_ had been knocked down, Qiu and his colleagues found the SWELL1 is required for volume regulation. They also found that the protein was embedded in the cellular membrane in an unexpected way. “People have been thinking that the leucine-rich repeats were on the outside,” said Qiu, “However, the topology we found \[indicated\] they were on the inside.”

SWELL1 appears to form a pore-like structure, although it’s unlikely that the protein is responsible for channel function on its own. And overexpressing _SWELL1_ didn’t increase VRAC activity in the cell, the researchers found.

The identification of _SWELL1_ could impact biomedical research, said [Geoffrey Abbott](http://www.faculty.uci.edu/profile.cfm?faculty_id=5869) of the University of California, Irvine, who was not involved in the work. “Now that the gene is known, it opens the floodgate for geneticists and people interested in certain diseases to say ‘OK, let us start searching for human mutations in this gene that might be associated with disease states,’” he said.

Given the history of VRAC protein research, some ion channel researchers are cautious. “While very exciting, this excitement is tempered by the knowledge that several other protein candidates have been identified over the last 25 years,” [Peter Brown](http://www.ls.manchester.ac.uk/people/profile/?personid=305) of Manchester University in the U.K. told _The Scientist_ in an e-mail. However, he added, “this study seems more robust than those supporting the claims of the previous pretenders.”

And questions remain as to the molecular mechanisms at play. “It’s not clear how this channel is able to sense osmotic stimuli and then react to them,” said Grandl. “This is very interesting, very novel, but it will still require a lot of work” to fully characterize SWELL1’s role, he added.

Qiu, Patapoutian, and their colleagues now plan to purify the protein and insert it into a lipid bilayer membrane to assess its activities on its own, and with the help of other proteins and activator molecules.

**Z. Qiu et al., “SWELL1, a plasma membrane protein, is an essential component of volume-regulated anion channel,” _Cell,_ 157_:_447–58, 2014.**
