### seanb4 — 2014-04-30T09:32:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-124002>

I'm finding it hard to get in a lot of fat when I have been eating like this. I have some raw prawns (entirely protein), then raw salmon (double protein to fat) in a bit of olive oil and maybe some butter of which I do not like eating on it's own however can force down 100g but even that's only 800 kcal of fat. Do I dislike the taste of butter because of excessive vit A?

I think I do well on less protein and more fat. If I overeat on the protein my symptoms are worse and I am far more tired. I think this is something to do with dehydration.

I ended up eating cheese and yogurt yesterday because I was still hungry after the above food and I have shit will power. Any high fat suggestions?

Also I asked at every place in my town market for salami and there was none...weird. I might try a big supermarket tomorrow and see if they do it. Could be a good source of fat...

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-30T10:43:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-124008>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=124002)
>
> Do I dislike the taste of butter because of excessive vit A?

It's unlikely to be that, Sean.

A tablespoon of butter has about 350 IU of retinoic acid. For comparison, 4 oz. of lamb's liver has about 28,000 IU.

> ... salami ... Could be a good source of fat...

Sure. Chris Bonnington used to take it for its calorific value compared to its weight. And ... well, have a look at some:

### seanb4 — 2014-04-30T19:07:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-124082>

So for a kg of the packaged crap you'd pay about £25. I guess if you bought a kg of the real stuff it would be more like £15-20. That's still pretty damn expensive...

Just had my tea now, 150g Raw King Prawns, 200g Kippers (half raw half cooked), 150g Butter, 20g Cocoa Butter, 40g Powdered Egg yolks, 200ml hot water.

I started eating the kippers raw but because they were the store bought, boil in a bag ones I was suspicious of how they'd been handled and parasites, plus it tasted like crap, so I cooked it. The yolk and butter mixture wasn't tasty but wasn't bad either, probably be better if I actually added the spices the guide suggested instead of just ignoring them.

My stomach doesn't feel that bad so far. I think the fact that I've been having almost daily BM's has helped...

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-01T15:26:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-124273>

It'll dry out if left open like that. People sometimes do that briefly as a way of drying poultry skin, so that it will crisp nicely.

Much game is traditionally hung till high. Venison would be an example. Just googled to find something for you. As he says:

> Meat is made up of long muscle cells connected by a fairly tough substance called collagen (the same stuff plastic surgeons inject into the lips of supermodels to make those lips full and "pouty"). Collagen causes most meat toughness. Young animals have little of it between their muscle cells, but as an animal gets older, more develops. Natural enzymes break down this intercellular collagen as meat is aged, so the longer it hangs, the more tender it becomes.

[http://www.fieldandstream.com/articles/other/recipes/2006/01/deer-hang-time](http://www.fieldandstream.com/articles/other/recipes/2006/01/deer-hang-time)

Of course, if your aim in eating the raw meat was to consume collagen that wasn't much broken down then perhaps well-aged vension, though you can buy it easily enough, wouldn't be the way to go. But perhaps you want tenderer meat or prefer the taste.

Bacteria are actually involved here -- similar to the ones in blue cheese, I believe.

At home, if you don't know what you're doing, you could come unstuck. Look up something on Icelandic specialities: you can see there are a lot of processes involved:

[http://www.guidetoiceland.is/about-iceland/article/the-worlds-most-disgusting-icelandic-food](http://www.guidetoiceland.is/about-iceland/article/the-worlds-most-disgusting-icelandic-food)

Sometimes whey is used to help the ferment, or there's smoking, or salting. Introducing bacteria, with the whey, would be a way of making sure the _right_ ones predominate. Again, salt inhibits gram-negative bacteria. The shark is buried in gravel ... but then they have to. It's a way of leaching out what would otherwise harm you.

I'd be wary if you don't understand the processes well and how to control them.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-01T20:20:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-124384>

Best way to colon health is eat things with DHA. As far as our knowledge goes, DHA has been the dominant fatty acid in the membrane phosphoglycerides of the photore- ceptors, neurons in the head and the gut and synapses for all 600 million years of animal evolution. Even today, the composition of the photoreceptor and brain varies little between species despite large scale species variation in the lipid composition of the diet, liver and muscle. Remember that your gut has more nerves in it that the cerebral cortex. When you are human you need DHA to collect electrons in every tissue. In fact, DHA is the evolutionary conductor for DNA since the beginning of all animal evolution. Proteins are selected to function with the constancy of DHA and not the other way around for 600 million years. This is extraordinary natural selection. As humans moved away from the shore and began civilization and moved inland they slowly began to consume mostly agricultural-based processed foods, and we got what we should have expected........ a global evolutionary crises beginning in the last two evolutionary systems, namely neurogenesis and autoimmunity. The further you get away from seafood the worse we will de evolve in all respects.

### seanb4 — 2014-05-02T18:11:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-124621>

Just to clear, it's not just about money, it's about what makes my symptoms worse. For example, the couple of months last year I was doing gaps intro diet stage 2, which is just broth and meat/fish with something like carrots. I was getting a lot of calories as well, like 3000+. These few months were really bad for me, palpitations, bloating / stomach pain, etc where all at there worst and so was tiredness, I was sleeping after every meal. I suspect this is because the meal volume was high. Contrast that to when I was eating egg yolk, multi vitamin, whey isolate, orange juice, and coconut meat. This was lower volume and my symptoms where much less servere. I was more able to get about and do something. Now it's obvious which one is the better diet, GAPS, but for how my body was reacting the other one was far better. It's not a case of, if I eat perfect epi-paleo I will be fine, at all.
Example today as I type this I feel very sick/full in my stomach and all I have drunk is water. I think I took too much at once or something but it happens all the time.

Anyways getting back on track. I have found an asian stall at the market that sells raw oysters and beef for relatively cheap. Which is good.

I have noticed that when I try to use magnesium to increase motility, it doesn't quite work right. Like I have to take real high doses, 4g x2 mag malate, and then I still won't have a BM for like 2 days but when I do it will be very loose. It's almost as if the magnesium is waiting around in my stomach/small intestines for far longer than it should...

@ caroline What were your gut and bowel like before you started swallowing the shellfish whole? Is this the only change youve made since then?

BTW, I would love to move to Norway...

Ok that sounds rather sexual but who cares. But it is one of the most sublime pleasures of life - wine that is.

From the movie: A good year

[Uncle Henry Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0001215/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): Max, have I told you why I enjoy making wine so much?

[Young Max](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0383603/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): You don't make the wine, Uncle Henry - that guy Duflot does.

[Uncle Henry Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0001215/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): \[Reproachfully\] In France it's always the landowner who makes the wine, even though he does nothing more than supervise with binoculars from the comfort of his study. No, I enjoy making wine, because this sublime nectar is quite simply incapable of lying. Picked too early, picked too late, it matters not - the wine will always whisper into your mouth with complete, unabashed honesty every time you take a sip.

This movie, like my own lifestory... Give it a try with a mind wide open and then you stop making excuses...

[Fanny Chenal](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0182839/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): \[Fanny approaches a customer who is behind a raised newspaper\] Bonjour. Vous avez choisi?

[Max Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0000128/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): \[lowers newspaper\] I think so.

[Fanny Chenal](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0182839/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): \[recognizing him\] You sure you don't need more time?

[Max Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0000128/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): No, I know what I want.

[Fanny Chenal](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0182839/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): You're sure?

[Max Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0000128/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): Absolutely.

[Fanny Chenal](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0182839/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): So, what is it to be?

[Max Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0000128/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): How's the soup?

[Fanny Chenal](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0182839/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): The soup is finished.

[Max Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0000128/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): Like my job... The fish?

[Fanny Chenal](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0182839/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): We've run out.

[Max Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0000128/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): That's like me with excuses.

[Fanny Chenal](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0182839/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): Don't waste my time. Choose something we have.

[Max Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0000128/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): I would like a lifetime spent with an irrational and suspicious goddess, some short-tempered jealousy on the side, and a bottle of wine that tastes like you, a glass that's never empty.

\[they kiss\]

This happens from time to time with water and it usually goes after a few hours. Anyway I forced down some some raw scallops, raw mackerel, and salmon. ### seanb4 — 2014-05-17T09:40:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-127313>

I've gone through the last two, whilst there's some cool stuff in there, dha capturing raised electrons, I couldn't see anything to answer my butyrate question.

Did some more snooping about and found more mixed opinions:

> Delivered orally in butter, one wonders how much actually reaches the colon (large intestine) vs. the butyrate generated by bacterial fermentation of fibers "on location". Think what you will of the pharmaceutical industry, you can learn a lot from delivery methods employed. Indeed [sodium butyrate](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science?_ob=ArticleURL&_udi=B8JJX-4V3V4RP-9&_user=10&_coverDate=09/30/2007&_rdoc=1&_fmt=high&_orig=search&_origin=search&_sort=d&_docanchor=&view=c&_searchStrId=1655947791&_rerunOrigin=google&_acct=C000050221&_version=1&_urlVersion=0&_userid=10&md5=ab66a2a2ee0a261bd8885aa91d3fc9a8&searchtype=a) is used in the treatment of Crohn's disease, and [supplements are coated](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17373743) to ensure release in the ileo-caecal region and colon. This would lead me to believe that the majority of butyrate in butter is absorbed well before it gets to the end of the small intestine and large intestine in the fully developed digestive system. May well be why it's found only in the infant food of mammals that have yet to establish bacterial populations in their guts. It seems to me that soluble fibers are a much better source of butyrate than butter.

> "Bovine milk fat contains from 7.5 to 13.0 mol/100 mol butyric acid. Because dibutyrylacylglycerols are present in trace amounts only, this means that about one third of milk fat triacylglycerols contain one molecule of butyrate. On ingestion, lipase-mediated hydrolysis of butyrate commences in the stomach and will be complete on reaching the proximal small intestine. Liberated butyrate is absorbed from the intestinal lumen to the enterocytes; it then passes directly to the portal circulation for transport to the liver where most is metabolized." (Parodi, 1997)

> The short and medium chains of butter allow it to be directly absorbed from the intestines for energy

> Does butyrate in what you eat even make a difference? If you surf around regarding oral butyrate (either via food or via supplements), you’ll see concern about how butyrate does not make it to the colon. It seems to be important to have butyrate actually physically in the colon. (8,9,10) Many human studies on oral butyrate use an enteric coated formulation so it can make it all the way to the colon (11). Two things cross my small mind here:
>
>
> 1.  Although most butyrate seems to be absorbed by the small intestine, the absorption of butyrate is “saturable,” meaning at some point the transport of butyrate will become overwhelmed and butyrate can scoot on by to make it unabsorbed to the colon. (12,13)
> 2.  It appears that the butyrate that **is** absorbed makes it to the blood for beneficial effects, even on the colon, and this seems beneficial to the body, too. From a study looking at short chain fatty acids (SCFAs) and butyrate on mice with induced colitis: _“It is now clear that the trophic \[positive growth\] effect of SCFA is due not only to the simple provision of energy to the host but also to the combination of local action **and** systemic metabolism of SCFA…We have demonstrated that this protection can be obtained by **oral** doses of SCFA.”_ (emphasis mine)(14)
>
> Point one makes me think that if enough butyrate is taken through foods, there is a point at which absorption is overcome and so some butyrate does actually make it to the colon. Point two makes me think that even if it does get all absorbed, even that which is absorbed makes a difference both to the entire body **and** to the colon.

TLDR: Either the butyrate from butter gets absorbed and metabolized before reaching the small intestines, providing no real benefit to the colon. OR the butyrate gets absorbed before the large intestines but travels there, via the blood stream, and provides beneficial effects. OR The pathways for absorbing butyrate can only do so much at a time so some butyrate sneaks through to the lower intestines.

Can you or anyone weight in on this?

### yewwei.tan — 2014-04-08T06:34:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-protein.10472/post-120338>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=120283)
>
> make sure you add grisel and tendon

I suspect that these forms of "resistant proteins" are more crucial for gut health than eating your meat raw.

Turns out that I was already naturally doing this. For example, I'd buy beef calf tendons (pretty cheap -- $7/kg), and then just boil them for about 30 mins, then proceed to slice up the meat portion with a sharp knife into thin slices, and return the extended tendon to a pot with more beef bones to make beef broth. This short cooking time is not enough to break down much of the connective tissue at all, resulting really chewy slices of half-meat-half-gristle beef which I almost just give a token amount of chewing before swallowing mostly whole.

(Sidenote: those tendons look like this -- [http://img.about-chinesefood.com/h7t/h7t8w2t9w2n0f8x3.jpg](http://img.about-chinesefood.com/h7t/h7t8w2t9w2n0f8x3.jpg) , but the ones I get have less meat. Beef cheek works great as well, as does what here in Australia is called "gravy beef", which is also part of the beef shank)

Originally, I was doing it just because I liked the texture, but looks like there's a side benefit which I wasn't aware of until last week (when Jack posted in this thread -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...ntial-through-hydride-ions.10343/#post-118768](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/do-raw-foods-improve-redox-potential-through-hydride-ions.10343/#post-118768))

Even a cut like the pork shoulder or neck contains a surprising amount of gristle and connective tissue, which most people want to break down through long cooking times, but which I think I now prefer just briefly cooked, and then sliced thin to either be eaten as-is, or incorporated into other dishes (salads, mixed as a filling in a pie, etc ...). Same with lamb shanks -- cook them quickly in the oven with no marination to get all the meat portion cooked through, but not enough to cause substantial conversion of collagen to gelatin.

If carbs aren't too big a deal, some briefly-cooked pork neck that is sliced while chewy makes a great portable meal in the form of a vietnamese rice paper roll -- [http://fatboo.com/2010/10/rice-paper-rolls.html](http://fatboo.com/2010/10/rice-paper-rolls.html) (loaded with lots more meat of course)

\----

I've also been picking away at these little raw dried anchovies that is a pretty traditional snack in South-East Asia. You can often find them piled up in a heap at some Asian Grocer, or nowadays, sealed up in packages (last batch I bought was $5.50 for a 400g pack). They look like this -- [http://4.bp.blogspot.com/-Nvf0xoAzh1E/UTIlRSgFjsI/AAAAAAAAE3A/AeLDK0ZnSNA/s1600/ikan bilis.jpg](http://4.bp.blogspot.com/-Nvf0xoAzh1E/UTIlRSgFjsI/AAAAAAAAE3A/AeLDK0ZnSNA/s1600/ikan+bilis.jpg)

Goes great with nuts! (another great food for gut health)

\----

Those aren't recipes per se, but I'm sure that anyone can use those tactics to innovate in their own kitchen.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-04-08T09:03:29+0300

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=120339)

To be honest, I haven't made these for myself in quite awhile  , mainly because of the carb content, but maybe some time in the future. I always liked the traditional sauce of choice, Nuoc Cham, which is basically a fish sauce (example recipe: [http://www.girlcooksworld.com/2012/06/nuoc-cham-everyday-vietnamese-dipping-sauce.html](http://www.girlcooksworld.com/2012/06/nuoc-cham-everyday-vietnamese-dipping-sauce.html)). I also liked any form of chilli sauce, be it store-bought Rooster sauce (Sriracha), or a homemade sambal olek.

That said, anything can be wrapped in rice paper. I once had chicharrones, pork shoulder, and some guacamole rolled in rice paper with lettuce as a gluten-free substitute for taco shells. I'd assume some shredded beef ribs with a little BBQ sauce wouldn't be bad either. And of course, peking duck as a rice paper roll is awesome!

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-07T23:34:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/april-2014-webinar-promo.10474/post-120277>

In 2013 you learned about water's fourth phase. In February 2014 you learned about how collagen and water combine with the magnetic moment to create a fourth phase of matter. And March 2014 you saw how these concepts came together to explain a very complex illness using energy mass equivalence.

April 2014 builds on them all. How do you build tensegrity? How does collagen do this? What disturbs this balance? What are the factors involved. How does copper play its role as a transition metal?

What is vitamin C real role and why was it replaced?

There is be a lot of random thoughts presented to get you to think deeper........because we are headed where few people go. To get there you need to have your perspective shook.

Why are some proteins excluded from collagen.........and how many collagens are there?

How does Becker, Szent Georygi, Ling, Pollack work all mesh..........

Why do we have a Vitamin D pandemic.

Why are you missing this stuff? Do you begin with a beginner's mind? Think back when you were a curious teenager. Do you approach life the same way you did back then with passion and drive or just do you just live life on cruise control?

Teenagers learn best by doing things, they learn best in teams and they learn best by doing things for real — all the opposite of what mainstream schooling actually does.

Education needs to work by pull, not push.

Education, life life should be a self-organizing system, where learning is an "emergent phenomenon".

The teachers union does not want us to know that. Neither does medicine. This is why I do what I do here. This is the school of hard knocks where you can learn based upon what you put in. If you want to be force fed you wont have it here or in life.

Education is great, to a degree … but it's really my creativity that's taught me that I can be much more than what my education told me I am.

Creativity now is as important in education as literacy, and we should treat it with the same status. We don't because of the system of education in this country. Very many people go through their whole lives having no real sense of what their talents may be, or if they have any to speak of. We are educating people out of their creative capacities.

If you write down what you have learned here, it’s helps you gathering up all of the "bits" of knowledge and experience you’ve collected in life to help you explore the things you don’t know and where never aware of.

It is like seeing life constantly as a child does. It is a pretty amazing way to live anew.
**Genius is childhood recaptured at will.** The ability to attain greatness and make forward progress with positive transformative change dies on the vine the moment we try to emulate someone else. Be you, the world needs your insight. In April we pick up the story we began and we add a ton of key parts about collagen and all its quantum magic. I am expecting the Q & A to be very lively because many of the wires I have given you are now beginning to make a lot more sense to the levels and scale many of you are on..........we need to hit this area because where go next depends upon it.

### NeilBB — 2014-04-08T16:20:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/april-2014-webinar-promo.10474/post-120382>

OK Jack, I'll take the bait and start the ball rolling. Here's my take on Vitamin C...

The popular view of vitamin C as an antioxidant free radical scavenger is very short sighted. In redox biochemistry, vitamin C (ascorbic acid) is an important electron donor, especially to transition metals. It's particular redox potential causes it to donate most of its electrons to iron or copper, due to the specific reaction thermodynamics.

In particular, repeated reduction of iron is required for the hydroxylation of the amino acid proline, which is a necessary step in the formation of collagen. It is also needed for the ubiquination of HIF (hypoxia inducing factors) in the presence of oxygen, which is vital for the process of adapting to shifting oxygen concentrations.

Primates, guinea pigs and fruit bats lost the ability to synthesize ascorbic acid from glucose, clearly for some adaptive reason. It may have been because the synthesis of ascorbate itself can produce oxidative damage. Maybe it became easier to regulate through ingestion in the gut?

Also, ascorbate doesn't have to be ingested constantly even in humans provided it can be efficiently recycled. Recycling of ascorbate after it has donated it's electrons requires an efficient glutathione system. So it may be that physiological Vitamin C deficiency in humans has more to do with dysfunction of ascorbate recycling and poor glutathione function than it has to do with any simple dietary deficiency of citrus fruit?

### NeilBB — 2014-04-08T19:44:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/april-2014-webinar-promo.10474/post-120401>

Ascorbic acid's net effect can be pro-oxidant as well as antioxidant. Therefore, in some circumstances, such as in hemochromatosis, this means excess Vit C may contribute to oxidative stress. The basis of intravenous ascorbate usage as a cancer treatment relies on its ability to actually _exacerbate_ the effects of free radicals by doing tissue damage to the tumor.

So, like everything else it seems, vitamin C in the presence of oxygen is a double edged sword. I can speculate that in a human in an altered field who accumulates transition metals in places where he shouldn't (neurodegenerative diseases for example), this mechanism could account for some of the accelerated levels of free radical mediated tissue damage that are seen.

> How do you build tensegrity?

The Forum is a learning environment where we are "pulled" along by our improving redox potentials supported by our improving physical "tensegrity" structures that stabilize our supreconducting water and mitigate stress through supporting the "3-legged stool" where the work of Becker, Ling, Szent Georgy and Pollack meet and manifest in the "ten thousand things" as the Chinese have called once upon a time. It is a "self-organizing system" and it does require some writing to see the mirror of our thoughts/mind and the mirror of other's thoughts/minds....The idea of a commune is to put people physically in this situation and to see what transformation is possible as things begin to "pop"...I have experienced this under better and worse circumstances and there is definitely an alchemy to it...as long as there is a dynamic "emergent phenomena" supported by a conceptual "tensegrity" things thrive...therein lies the art and "creativity" shall we say

.....copper and types of collagen???
Copper and transition metals increase the surface charge of collagen to improve water structuring and EZ zone formation around the collagen. This enhances tensegrity. I am guessing that Copper and other transition metals are crosslinkers for collagen fibrils...funny that I can see this because I have a background in some polymer chemistry related to crosslinking from agriculture and polyacrylamide gels that are crosslinked to give them unique 3D properties...interestingly these are used in soft contact lenses and some other biological applications from lab to direct implantation which was not such a great idea in the long run......perhaps copper is slightly more efficient for the following reason...

Maybe something related to this:

> In this study, **_we attempt to overcome this limitation by employing the paramagnetic relaxation properties of copper(II) ions to shorten the (1)H intrinsic spin-lattice_** (T(1)) relaxation times measured in natural-abundance (13)C cross-polarization (CP) magic-angle-spinning (MAS) NMR experiments on bone tissues for the purpose of accelerating the data acquisition time in SSNMR. To this end, high-resolution solid-state (13)C CPMAS experiments were conducted on type I collagen (bovine tendon), bovine cortical bone, and demineralized bovine cortical bone, each in powdered form, to measure the (1)H T(1) values in the absence and in the presence of 30 mM Cu(II)(NH(4))(2)EDTA. Our results show that the (1)H T(1) values were successfully reduced by a factor of 2.2, 2.9, and 3.2 for bovine cortical bone, type I collagen, and demineralized bone, respectively, without reducing the spectral resolution and thus enabling faster data acquisition. In addition, paramagnetic quenching of particular (13)C NMR resonances on exposure to Cu(2+) ions in the absence of mineral was also observed, potentially suggesting the relative proximity of three main amino acids in the protein backbone (glycine, proline, and alanine) to the bone mineral surface.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22953757](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22953757)

### Josh — 2014-04-08T22:26:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/april-2014-webinar-promo.10474/post-120435>

> Why are some proteins excluded from collagen.........and how many collagens are there?

1.  16 types of collagen
2.  Differ based upon:

    > The various collagens are distinguished by the ability of their helical and nonhelical regions to associate into fibrils, to form sheets, or to cross-link different [collagen](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/n/mcb/A7315/def-item/A7417/) types.

3.  Mostly types I, II, III
4.  Very very very dependent upon tertiary and quaternary protein folding=problems with EMF and sulfur metabolism, all tertiary and much quaternary protein structure is disulfide bond dependent

> The disulfide bonds are strong, with a typical [bond dissociation energy](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bond_dissociation_energy) of 60 kcal/mole (251 kJ mol−1). **_However, being about 40% weaker than [C–C](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C%E2%80%93C_bond) and [C–H bonds](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C%E2%80%93H_bond), the disulfide bond is often the "weak link" in many molecules._** Furthermore, reflecting the [polarizability](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polarizability) of divalent sulfur, the S–S bond is susceptible to scission by polar reagents, both [electrophiles](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrophile) and especially [nucleophiles](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nucleophile):[\[1\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disulfide_bond#cite_note-1)
>
> RS–SR + Nu− → RS–Nu + RS−
> The disulfide bond is about 2.05 Å in length, about 0.5 Å longer than a C–C bond. Rotation about the S–S axis is subject to a low barrier. **_Disulfides show a distinct preference for [dihedral angles](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dihedral_angle) approaching 90°. When the angle approaches 0° or 180°, then the disulfide is a significantly better oxidant._**
>
> Disulfides where the two R groups are the same are called symmetric, examples being [diphenyl disulfide](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diphenyl_disulfide) and [dimethyl disulfide](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dimethyl_disulfide). When the two R groups are not identical, the compound is said to be an unsymmetric or mixed disulfide.[\[2\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disulfide_bond#cite_note-Sevier-2)
>
> [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disulfide\_bond](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disulfide_bond)

Collagen is about strength and folding so proteins that will not support this are excluded based on functional design I would imagine....

Folding is crucial and therefore collagen formation and the hoped for "tensegrity" are very susceptible to anything that distorts this such as methylation issues, non-native EMF, low redox (of course these are often together)...anything that "denatures" proteins and destroys their structure.

When osteopathy shifted toward mainstream allopathy, he stayed true to Still and they took his license for "curing" someone with manipulation. ### Josh — 2014-04-09T00:43:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/april-2014-webinar-promo.10474/post-120460>

Back to Vitamin C....to use Oxygen for cellular respiration, the size of the organism limits things unless one can carry it around and deliver it....Copper and Iron require Vitamin C to make Hemoglobin and Cerruloplasm that carry oxygen and deliver it to cells. Any evolution or COE that requires alteration of oxygen utilization would require something to regulate oxygen carrying proteins. Perhaps Vitamin C functions this way in its role as a cofactor in Iron and Copper metabolism? Regulating the metabolism in the event of hypoxia would be a vital function to preserve the organism. It appears that cellular levels of ascorbate might be part of the signalling mechanism for this as the body would compensate for hypoxia by making more oxygen carriers to try to maximize available oxygen. Neolithic conditions mimicking hypoxia such as heavy metal toxicity and non-native EMF appear to deplete ascorbate and produce similar epigenetic signals that in the absence of true hypoxia create a great deal of free radical damage and dysregulation/disease. What does cancer look like? Adaptation to hypoxia....could ascorbate levels be a trigger for cells to adapt to hypoxia which favors anaerobic metabolism? Might this have been an epigenetic switch to turn off evolution and energy-intensive system development such as nervous and immune system in times of hypoxia?

I derived this from a brief read of this epic article that Neil posted above, it really hurts the head and is a good review of almost everything we have learned to date...:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3118659/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3118659/)

### Josh — 2014-04-10T21:58:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/april-2014-webinar-promo.10474/post-120849>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=120447)
>
> Oxygen is a powerful medicine because of what it does to electrons. Life before O2 was a collagen story. Oxygen use by life began the initial penetration of solving complexity.
> Complexity is enabled by electrons and is responsible for producing more electrons in a system. This is why life exploded after the Cambrian event. No other reason. We need more electrons to get more oxygen so we can generate more electrons.

Now into this as a review....

1.  Oxygen is a strong donor and attractor of electrons
2.  If low O2 environment was a "collagen story" then it reinforces the concept that good collagen creates the tensegrity necessary to thermodynamically optimize cell volumes and organism size for the utilization of limited O2 supplies.
3.  Vitamin C would have been very important with Copper low O2 environment.
4.  Higher O2 concentrations opened the door to larger and more complex organisms=mass equivalence stuff
5.  As we head back the other way, you are pointing out that this stuff is buried in there and we need to learn how it works to survive...
6.  The amphibians do not seem to have very sophisticated mechanisms to use this for optimization in their system, so they are dying like flies...along with the insects. Size for them is directly tied to O2 levels because they do not have lungs and sophisticated ways to concentrate and optimize O2 usage the way higher organisms do. The EMF issue compounds that O2 issue by destroying the redox potnetial with increased energy and entropy in the system....too many disordered electrons...cannot make and optimize collagen to optimize cell volume to satsfy the mass equivalence relationships....

So here are my labs.... taken on 3/21/14

So looking at these E2 went CRAZY! as did SHGB (there's a surprise) Pregnenolone tanked, as did Progesterone

Taking 100mg Pregnenolone and 2ml of 20% compounded topical Pg & .4ml of 5% sublingual Pg.
50mg DHEA & 25mg 7KetoDHEA
saw palmetto
200mg Selenium
500mg calcium d-glucarate and myomin
750mg Acetyl-lCaratine
1500mg CoQ10
40g D-ribose
4000mg Magnesium malate
100mg zinc
30mg Armour
1500mg Metformin
4 x .8mg cycloset
**I AM NOT TAKING ANY ESTROGEN!!!**

Positive. Bun:Creatine dropped from 22 to 16!!! WHOOT! Collegen baby! & my liver panels are back in range (thank you Now liver detoxifer)

Thyroid seems to be working better - this was on 60mg Armour split btwn am/pm. (currently backed down to 30mg Armour and will retest in about 4wks.

waiting to see what Dzugan's going to do. I haven't had a hormonal migraine since October.

Blood drawn for labs on Menstrual Cycle Day 21

Estradiol 590pg/mL, high should be ~150, consider aromatase inhibitors (Arimidex as first, start with 1mg/E3D then titrate biweekly), keep calcium D glucarate, lots of cruciferous

There is a good chance that supplementing with pregnenolone will give you that or more.
Anyhow at CD21 you are still low on progesterone, it should be around 15.
I suggest to give pregnenolone priority, but if you still low then add progesterone as need.
I am guessing that if successful, you would use pregnenolone-MLM same daily dose every day, and additionally progesterone, likely on CD(16-24).

Pregnenolone, progesterone, DHEAs (couple others) are neuro-steroids, likely high correlation with migraines.

# Thread: Touch .... Jeremy style
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/touch-jeremy-style.10476/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2014-04-08T02:42:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/touch-jeremy-style.10476/post-120315>

There is so much about Jeremy and Jack's connection .....
I have been thinking about all of this so much .... but one thing baffles me - well many things - lets not go there yet!

Jack talked about Jeremy working on his shoulder ....and how he has to learn to tough like that!

This mystifies me .... Jack is a Doctor and a neurosurgeon ...if need be - he could take a scalpel, or axe, or drill, or saw, or jack hammer, or how ever he does it to crack my coconut. I would think that he would have exquisite and an extremely sensitive touch in his hand.

I would think if he needed to examine my head or neck or back - he would have acute sensitivity in his hands - looking for whatever.

Soooo - why does Jeremy have something more in his touch? I suppose it is the collagen?

So - what about a Doc who is in poor health/low redox himself?

If Dr. k. needs to learn how to touch like Jeremy ........ if we do as well .... the potential to increase how we connect to others is off the charts......

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-08T03:45:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/touch-jeremy-style.10476/post-120326>

The collagen in my glial cells is where I have focused my tensegrity network and Jeremy's is within his periphery and in his body..............We are the Ying and Yang of tensegrity and that is why when we meet 1 + 1 = 4

### sjoshua — 2014-04-10T02:04:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/touch-jeremy-style.10476/post-120694>

Brain meets brawn in the quantum realm. Wish I coulda been a fly on the wall at the coffee shop!

### SeaHorse — 2014-04-10T02:20:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/touch-jeremy-style.10476/post-120700>

It's kind of like the breathing through skin…you can't do it if your brain is too engaged in a thinking process…touch is the same I believe, touch with a purpose or intent that is focussed (diagnostics, surgery etc) will not be the same touch as dancing, planting flowers, slapping, on and on. They are all different and contain different information. I'm love how certain practitioners who use touch are able to access different parts of the nervous system and it's not simply variations in pressure.

Horses (sorry!) respond to intent when touched….there is communication that flows with the actual physical sensation. The meaning changes….it's not cued responses. And in fact "touch" exists almost in another dimension as you can "touch" a horse from 20 feet away and he will register it in his body…..that's the practical world of non-local communication. They are masters of it.

# Thread: Ben Greenfield's Book review: Beyond Training
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ben-greenfields-book-review-beyond-training.10477/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 30
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-08T03:20:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ben-greenfields-book-review-beyond-training.10477/post-120321>

Ben sent me a copy of his book several weeks ago and I read it in a day.......and put it out in my waiting room and I asked people to browse it and give me some comments when they came in to see me.

Here is my book review:

Ben Greenfield’s book asks the reader to question themselves where they are right now. This is not an information book at all when you read between the lines. It is book that asks you how many skills do you have in your own “thermodynamic toolbox” to go beyond your current boundaries. Ben calls this a superhuman life. When he gives you information, he is really asking you to assess where you are now. To understand what you are capable of, you have to look at your life as a thermodynamic equation. The goals in your life should be the ‘given’ on left side of the equation. The the variables in your life right now should be on the right side of it. Ben asks you in Chapter 20 to create a bucket filled list with where you find yourself now. When you work from your current knowns and you already know where you want to go from the left side of the equation, all that is left is to figure out a blueprint of how to create a blueprint that can bridge this gap. This is a game plan that can transcend training. It also maybe the best way to live your life. Ben is not pretentious in this book, by coming out to tell you how you should live; he was smart enough to write about enough variables in this treatise to get the reader to think about their own current position in life. In a world where your training, your goals, and your failings can open both the doors to heaven or hell, he shows you how to use it to create yourself. He tries to show us it’s not the load that breaks you down, it’s the way we carry the load that does. You begin to see why you fail and win, and you begin to see how those failures may become your greatest strengths if you use it in your plan to recreate your life beyond training.

I particular liked the back half of the book where Ben focused on lifestyle issues and on looking under the stones that most people forget. We need to be reminded about our blind spots. Chapter 18 asks you to consider hidden killers in your environment. The middle part of the book focused on nutrition and I thought it could have been a lot more concise and to the point. The genesis of the book in Part 1is Ben’s sweet spot. I think if you are fitness afficionado this will be a highlight of the book.

I left the book in my patient’s waiting room after I had read it and I asked several of them what they liked about the book. Almost all of them focused on Part 2, on the recovery from exercise. The sections on far infrared, PEMF, and cold lasers were excellent. I had wish they were expanded upon, truthfully. The use of touch for recovery was also a nice add, but I wanted more of Ben’s perspective. The chapter on sleep was a great overview for the lay public on why sleep is critical for us all, not just an endurance athlete.

My favorite thing about Ben’s book was his quick hitting, honest, rapid fire summaries with references at the end of each chapter. I have had several people ask to photocopy them in the office. The tables throughout the book packed in good information. I have grown tired of many of the books touting an ancestral lifestyle and training ideas, and Ben shows a deft balance in this book that tells the reader to run away from average. Aspiring for average is not something to aspire to, it is something to run from. Ben admits he does not follow an ancestral template and he explains why without having to say it. Ben’s goals for the book were summarized on page 467. He wants to fill “your toolbox” with many ideas that cross disciplines and your own ideas of optimal. Ben’s ideas run away from the “paleo pyramid” which creates books that are interested in promoting mediocre ideas that promote safe and boring life visions of eating and training; they produce retread recipe cookbooks, because then one never needs to fear for his position in this community, which, in case of serious controversy, they'd be forced to defend. Therefore, for those on the top of the ancestral pyramid, ‘controversy’ is structurally undesirable. For them, innovative thinking becomes inevitably controversial. They attack the person and not the ideas they possess. Ben goes after his target audience of endurance athletes in the preface. Ben engages the reader early on by listing all the endurance athletes who have died too early or succumbed to diseases that lead them to average. I respect him for that honesty, and I think it makes the book have a ‘gravitational force’ to draw the reader in to want to know more, so they can become better.

Ben’s argument for writing this book is in the preface and it is clear. Endurance exercise is fraught with risks and you need a treatise of information to consider before you give your life to it to become superhuman. His goal is to make you think about these things, while also giving you resources to to develop a plan that fits your personal thermodynamic problems to training. I believe if you read this book with that in mind you will be able to seriously help yourself reach your goals from the position you are now safely. What good is any record in any race if you die early or acquire a neolithic disease in the process? Ben asks you to consider these things, because he is thinking it with you as he writes. In my view, this makes the book a very valuable resource to anyone who is looking to make a transformational change within their life. He is thoughtful and he is honest and that is what made this book for me, a good read. The most profound property of our life time, is nothing stays the same. Ben shows and explains you are built to be dynamically receptive. He questions you to consider stop living with the results of other people’s thinking. He points to things that may reveal your purpose; your purpose should dictate your time, creating the person you want to be.

> I've followed Ben Greenfield for the past couple years, being a recovering endurance athlete I love his approach and bio-hacking ways. Since I follow him I was lucky enough to get to read/skim over most of the book before it was published, and he has a refreshing point of view and is also quite funny listening to him and his cohort, Brock when I am travelling. ### Teri Pitman — 2014-04-11T17:13:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ben-greenfields-book-review-beyond-training.10477/post-120994>

> [Hemming said:](/goto/post?id=120982)
>
> Recovering endurance athlete, can you please elaborate? I have a feeling that you have been through the same as me.

Since about 19 yo to about 30 yo I competed in marathons, ultramarathons (up to 50 miles), mountain biking-ultra distance, and up to Ironman triathlon overtraining and eating poorly (although I thought it was healthy) all along the way. I was pretty much go big or go home. I am still active but definitely more moderate, I enjoy the harder shorter workouts as opposed to spending all day on my bike. I am trying to heal a thyroid, probably from all the low fat wheat products I was indulging in to give me energy as well as some leaky gut issues probably from all the inflammation and gluten poison. It's hard to see friends still out there doing it because it was sure fun but now I have time and energy to work on our house, volunteer a bit and feel more balanced in life.

# Thread: What Is the Right Way to Maintain Electrolytes?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-04-08T14:31:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/post-120367>

It looks like I have really been hurting myself. I have low sodium. When dehydrated, I drink water, which when taken alone lowers sodium. I have episodes where heavy exercise leads to dehydration all night long. I end up taking a lot of water, which my body urinates quickly (maybe in part because I have low sodium and it doesn't want to sodium concentration to go lower?), which makes me drink more, and I end up in a vicious cycle. This got me to thinking how much electrolytes should we put into normal drinking water in order to maintain healthy levels of electrolytes like sodium?

There are sports drinks, but these have \*excess\* electrolytes. They are intended to be used when you are sweating heavily. I will certainly start taking electrolytes after exercise, but I don't think those are intended to be used as a replacement for normal drinking water. I want to make sure I have enough electrolytes in my normal drinking water so I don't contribute further to any low sodium condition.

Is there a formula out there for how we could make our own drinking water by adding in just the right amount of electrolyte to keep the body well balanced in electrolytes? The idea is to let the body regulate the amount of water it wants to keeps naturally, and not accidentally get into a cycle like the one I was where you have simultaneous low sodium and low hydration that no amount of pure water by itself can fix.

### Dali Dula — 2014-04-08T14:48:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/post-120371>

Spring water or well water?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/post-120373>

I don't see any point in raising calcium for Vitamin D after exercise? Excluding those two, how could I build the above into an electrolyte replacement drink? How much salt to add? How much potassium chloride or other source of potassium? How to add the magnesium?

### persistence — 2014-04-16T01:22:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/post-121743>

I read in the book "The Art and Science of Low Carbohydrate Living" that low carb diets require increased sodium intake. I have only gotten a few chapters into the book, but it looks like they do not give specific recommendations on amount of sodium to include in the diet.

A single teaspoon of salt should contain about 2 grams of sodium and should weigh about 5 grams. For a person on a high or normal level of carbs, this would be considered a typical sodium intake. Has anyone seen specific guidelines on amounts of sodium to take on a low carb diet?

I am taking about 1/4 teaspoon mixed in 10 ounces of water, about three times a day. That has made a remarkable difference in some of my fatigue symptoms. My guess would be that overall I am getting about 1.5 teaspoons per day. I don't have a good basis for adjusting dose.

### persistence — 2014-04-23T21:14:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/post-123226>

> [persistence said:](/goto/post?id=121743)
>
> I read in the book "The Art and Science of Low Carbohydrate Living" that low carb diets require increased sodium intake. I have only gotten a few chapters into the book, but it looks like they do not give specific recommendations on amount of sodium to include in the diet.
>
> A single teaspoon of salt should contain about 2 grams of sodium and should weigh about 5 grams. For a person on a high or normal level of carbs, this would be considered a typical sodium intake. Has anyone seen specific guidelines on amounts of sodium to take on a low carb diet?
>
> I am taking about 1/4 teaspoon mixed in 10 ounces of water, about three times a day. That has made a remarkable difference in some of my fatigue symptoms. My guess would be that overall I am getting about 1.5 teaspoons per day. I don't have a good basis for adjusting dose.

So reading more of "The Art and Science of Low Carbohydrate Living" they are recommending that people on a low carb diet supplement about five grams of sodium per day. That works out to around two and one half teaspoons of salt per day, a lot of salt!!

I am doing this for a few days and it makes a remarkable difference in how I feel. It's too early to see if this lets me start to recover my exercise levels and strength.

In the category of "Twilight Zone" I have noticed that for about three hours after I take each sodium dose (about 1/2 teaspoon with 12 oz of water), my blood glucose totally corrects to normal. I can eat 50 grams of starchy carbs and peak at 115 mg/dL, whereas without the sodium I was peaking over 150 mg/dL. Three hours after the starchy meal while continuing to take sodium I can see readings under 80 mg/dL, and my steady state numbers stay under 90 mg/dL. Without the sodium my steady-state glucose numbers during the day are 105 to 115 mg/dL. At this point I am not understanding is this all a chemistry trick, where the higher sodium levels somehow fool the glucometer into giving false readings.

### cantweight — 2014-04-08T16:58:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-an-ancient-disorder.10479/post-120386>

**"It is likely that, as for other susceptibility alleles for modern metabolic diseases ([14](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3164771/#R14), [15](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3164771/#R15)), the origins of PCOS began in Paleolithic hunter-gatherer communities, in which environmental stressors favored the survival of those males, females and offspring with the greatest capacity for energy storage necessary to endure prolonged episodes of privation, the so-called “thrifty genotype” ([16](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3164771/#R16)\-[18](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3164771/#R18)). If so, such a thrifty genotype may have enhanced survival during times of food deprivation, with reduced postprandial thermogenesis from insulin resistance diminishing energy expenditure as an additional evolutionary advantage ([19](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3164771/#R19))."**

Awesome article...Thanks Annie!

they are saying that the insuliin resistant state (to conserve energy) results in a decrease in the loss of heat after eating. so they are diminishing energy expenditure as free heat, to retain energy in the system as an evolutionary advantage.
the "thrifty genes" were amazing for that particular era, however in modern environments and in the face of modern substrates they are disasterous. APOE4 is a similar thrifty gene, or ancestral allele, when carriers live an ancestral lifestyle they are unstoppable.
i like to see these alleles as a free pass to rockstardom, if you align your environment and diets well!

# Thread: From athletes to couch potatoes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-athletes-to-couch-potatoes.10480/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-08T20:44:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-athletes-to-couch-potatoes.10480/post-120419>

> Human bones are remarkably plastic and respond surprisingly quickly to change. Put under stress through physical exertion – such as long-distance walking or running – they gain in strength as the fibres are added or redistributed according to where strains are highest. The ability of bone to adapt to loading is shown by analysis of the skeletons of modern athletes, whose bones show remarkably rapid adaptation to both the intensity and direction of strains.
>
> Because the structure of human bones can inform us about the lifestyles of the individuals they belong to, they can provide valuable clues for biological anthropologists looking at past cultures. Research by Alison Macintosh, a PhD candidate in Cambridge University's Department of Archaeology and Anthropology, shows that after the emergence of agriculture in Central Europe from around 5300 BC, the bones of those living in the fertile soils of the Danube river valley became progressively less strong, pointing to a decline in mobility and loading. ...

[http://www.eurekalert.org/pub\_releases/2014-04/uoc-fat040314.php](http://www.eurekalert.org/pub_releases/2014-04/uoc-fat040314.php)

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-09T08:24:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-athletes-to-couch-potatoes.10480/post-120527>

Yes, it's quite fun, isn't it, Audrey?

It seems to turn Marshall Sahlins on his head.

But I guess the title the editors have given the piece, as so often, is more eye-catching than accurate. I don't think anyone who had any idea of the amount of physical labour put in by people in agricultural societies before mechanisation would blithely put up "couch potatoes".

In fact, what we find is:

> Macintosh laser-scanned skeletons found in cemeteries across Central Europe, concentrating in particular on an analysis of engineering-based cross-sectional geometric properties as measures of the loading imposed on the lower limb bones during life.

IOW, there's a fairly narrow focus. This is a measure of "long-distance walking or running". I suppose that even where game is abundant, the hunters must sometimes have spent much of the day walking, and running, in search of the herds or after game.

### nonchalant — 2014-04-09T16:38:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-athletes-to-couch-potatoes.10480/post-120596>

I'm not sure the decline in strength was entirely from a decrease of mobility. I've seen DH go on a wheat spree, and his joints start popping and cracking in just a few days. What affects the joints must affect bone as well.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-09T20:20:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-athletes-to-couch-potatoes.10480/post-120649>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=120596)
>
> I'm not sure the decline in strength was entirely from a decrease of mobility. I've seen DH go on a wheat spree, and his joints start popping and cracking in just a few days. What affects the joints must affect bone as well.

Well, here you have got a relatively smaller change in bone thickness and strength in the lower leg bones of _women_ over time. Now _their_ diets will have changed, too, so why relatively less change in these women's bones after time? The answer would seem to be, because they weren't walking and running as far as the men would have been before the advent of farming, so that there was relatively less change in their patterns of use. Plants, after all, don't run away. (I should think that running, sometimes for long distances, probably played a bigger part in the lives of hunters than some paleo celebrities would like to believe. I'm not pushing the "Born to Run" myth -- just saying what I think is likely to be true.)

So I think it, indeed, is use that's the relevant factor here. The main finding seems to be: people whose lives involved much running and walking had bone changes in the bones of the lower leg. Lots of fanfare. But I wonder should we be surprised at that?

But, sure, I take your point that in general loading isn't the only factor. AFAICT, physical anthropologists (and others) seem almost always to reach for notions of use and impact to explain bone thickness.

Something similar holds for teeth -- Vilhjalmur Stefansson made fun of people for saying that strong teeth were down to plenty of chewing:

> There is for separated districts a wide difference in the amount of Eskimo chewing, but no one has reported that health of the teeth is better among heavy chewers. How could it be when as yet no caries has been found either among the lightest or heaviest masticators?
>
> It is used as a second line of defense by the mastication advocates that even if Eskimos perhaps don’t chew their food so very much they do chew skins a great deal. Their chewing of leather is far less than you might believe from what has been said by a particular kind of writer and pictured in certain movies. In any case, skin chewing is mainly by the women, and **it is not easy to bring under the conditions of modern scientific thought the idea that the wife’s chewing preserves her husband’s teeth.**

Here's something else that has struck me. Weston Price has a photograph of skull fragments from the U.S.:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

He captions that:

> FIG. 27. Left: Example of greater thickness of pre-Columbian Indian skulls in Florida than modern skulls. ...

[http://journeytoforever.org/farm\_library/price/price6.html](http://journeytoforever.org/farm_library/price/price6.html)

Now I know that loading can cause bone to thicken. That kind of thing happens more on one side than the other in archers, for example:

> Bone densities too differ across the shoulder blades and back, as well as from the bow arm to the drawing arm.The same was found among those lost at sea in the Mary Rose.

[http://www.longbow-archers.com/heavybows.html](http://www.longbow-archers.com/heavybows.html)

But the bones of the skull ???

It's not as if people were banging their heads on the ground in pre-Colombian America.

I think in general people underestimate the physical effects of diet. Doctors certainly do. There was a joke going round paleo circles on the internet the other day. It ran thus:

> "I took my dog to the vet and the first thing he said was, 'What are you feeding it?' I went to the doctor and the first thing he said was, 'What meds are you taking?' Next time I'm going to the vet's myself."

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-11T04:34:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adhesive-capsulitis-frozen-shoulder.10482/post-120927>

all directly tied to loss of electrons. Collagen is a n type semiconductor. What does this mean? Through various techniques like [doping](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Doping_\(semiconductor\)), the semiconductor can be modified to have an excess of electrons. This is what a N-type semiconductor is. When you have a frozen shoulder you have a problem in doping gating or dehydration and tearing and what happen? You lose electrons and charge........and the collagen ionizes. When doped semiconductors are [joined to metals](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metal-semiconductor_junction) (transition), [to different semiconductors](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heterojunction), and [to the same semiconductor with different doping](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/P-n_junction) mechanisms, the "resulting junction" often strips the electron excess or deficiency out from the semiconductor near the junction.

I fixed my screwed up rotator cuff by half-ass doing yoga head-stands against the wall - it didn't even take a week to go from holding my elbow to steam-cleaning carpets - then I graduated to the hammer-chisel... a medical Dr. Lorean Fishman - who was into yoga - tried it for the hell of it and cured herself - she has done it for 700 people thus far with great success:
[http://www.huffingtonpost.com/loren-fishman-md/yoga-for-rotator-cuff-injury\_b\_916728.html](http://www.huffingtonpost.com/loren-fishman-md/yoga-for-rotator-cuff-injury_b_916728.html)

Of course, then I totally screwed up the other one with an angle grinder followed by crushing it - but tape \*really\* helped it...

I sleep with 2 ice packs on my shoulders and one on my back - it really helps - also, turmeric and astaxanthin (synthesized from krill oil) really help with inflammation... in fact, some people use astaxanthin to increase the time they can spend in the sun - amazing stuff...

You also might want to research the wonders of fulvic acid for hydration - that Dr. Jerry Tennant talked about it - some kind of highly bioavailable minerals?

The trends in these injuries probably follow the trend in cataracts - which are now being found in people in their 50's as hydration is the best thing you can do for your eyes...

> [Da-mo said:](/goto/post?id=141734)
>
> I've wondered about the TENS treatment in relation to EMF. My physio is mentor for my daughter (who is in 3rd year of training) and I remember her saying to my daughter that until the inflammation is gone treatment is not going to be effective - so I hope this extends to her use of TENS. I've never noticed any inflammation after TENS so hopefully she has it sorted.

**Lou Gehrig's disease is an iatrogenic disease of athletes caused by use of electrotherapy devices**
1/3/2010 by Samuel Milham
Abstract: Dr. Milham examines evidence of the relationship between trans-cutaneous electric stimulation devices used in sports medicine and amytrophic lateral sclerosis (Lou Gehrig's disease). He hypothesizes that sporadic ALS is caused by external electrical currents induced in or applied to the body. Since most cases of ALS have no obvious connection to electrical shock or electrical therapy, most non-familial cases must have had currents induced in their bodies by working or living in environments with strong EMFs.

[http://www.stetzerizer-us.com/Lou-G...d-by-use-of-electrotherapy-devices\_df\_80.html](http://www.stetzerizer-us.com/Lou-Gehrigs-disease-is-an-iatrogenic-disease-of-athletes-caused-by-use-of-electrotherapy-devices_df_80.html)

### fitness@home — 2014-10-09T20:53:28+0300

**@[Josh](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/8791/) (s)**
I read this article this morning, Tinnitus of Myofascial Origin, and much of it fit me to a T:

[http://www.tinnitusjournal.com/detalhe\_artigo.asp?id=504](http://www.tinnitusjournal.com/detalhe_artigo.asp?id=504)

Precipitating factors which apply to me:
· Posture (scoliosis)
· Whiplash at 20 years old
· Dental problems - have bite issues and bruxism
· Myofascial dysfunction - whole body thermography scan showed areas
· Trigger points in the trapezius muscle

The treatment described is something I would like to try. If it didn’t work, I’d at least know it wasn’t of myofascial origin and/or related to bruxism. The tinnitus showed up a year ago in August and I did see an ENT for a hearing test - have some loss in each ear.

**My question is – what type of physician would I see to try this?** Would it be ENT, a dental specialty, physical therapist, pain management, or other type?? There are a plethora of doctors in the Houston area…just need to know where to start looking.

I also understand that the underlying issues for the above would still need to be addressed.

Thanks

### JMO — 2018-02-28T03:50:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adhesive-capsulitis-frozen-shoulder.10482/post-235911>

Finally had a MRI of my shoulder and was diagnosed with frozen shoulder. I have had intense shoulder pain since Nov. The pain is better now and I can finally sleep. Movement is still pretty restricted and my shoulder is totally f'd up. The doc told me it can take up to a year to get better. He also said that physical therapy is not going to help. Not a lot of help. Been having massage and some chiro on it. Cold and red light. Just wondering if anyone on this thread was able to regain movement totally and what you did...how long did it take??

### Valerie — 2018-02-28T04:38:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adhesive-capsulitis-frozen-shoulder.10482/post-235912>

Yes. Both shoulders (about a year apart). First one worse because didn't know what it was. All this before Jack Kruse web site existed. Went through with cortisone shot, but still came back. It was physical therapy that finally loosened my shoulder and it was like having natural childbirth. Just keep stretching it out. My shoulders are now back to normal. Took about 6 -8 months. Knowing what I know now, I would dose up on herbal anti-inflammatory...like boswellia/black pepper extract. It does take a few weeks to kick in. I would suspect cold and redlight are good as well.

> To show you how important meal timing to the light cycles are to us humans, consider these facts regarding exercise and meal timing. If you are able to yoke your workout to your evening protein dinner meal (within 30-45 minutes) you actually “triple the amount of protein synthesis” that occurs compared to those who do not. This is how a hunter gatherer attained their optimal body comp without having to do huge amounts of exercises. Moreover, if this is also yoked to the light cycle in winter when the temperatures are below 40 degrees, you can increase protein synthesis to 400% while inducing uncoupling proteins to burn fat at a tripled rate to baseline. This allows you to increase fat burning to shred body fat further faster while you are increasing lean muscle mass. NASA uses this technology for the astronauts that space walk. They found this data out ironically from the Sherpa’s who help foreigners scale the last 2000 feet of Mount Everest summit. This is also precisely why I am leanest around December 21st,. At this time, the light cycle its lowest and the temperature is usually low so exercising in this window is the easiest way I know to shred body fat and gain muscle mass quickly.

### cantweight — 2014-04-09T17:22:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dave-asprey.10486/post-120618>

I always joke with my hubby that Dave is my brother from another mother. With the lyme, hashis, mold, methylation, obesity....we have a lot in common and I love that he has all those crappy things and is "Bulletproof". The situation he described with a too low carb diet is exactly what I have been experiencing. The amonia smell in my urine, I have developed an allergy to eggs, and I feel so crappy. Over the past few months I have had pho with rice noddles a couple times and when I did I felt amazing! I have been telling myself I just need some carbs but thought it was the fat lady inside just trying to rationalize a way to get something I wouldnt give her...lol

### kovita — 2014-04-09T17:38:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dave-asprey.10486/post-120623>

Christi, I have an impression MOST women are not made for long term keto. I am not offering any sort of scientifical explanation, but this is experience I madea round me and myself. I do very well as long as. i do something verys i ilar to what Dave does, eat some carbs once or twice weekly and the same day limit proteins, essentially break ketosis. I do so well doing this that I do not havea ny sort of schedule, but do what my body asks for on that particular day. I am coming from very lean, almost underweight, have history of short anorexia while 17, followed by bulimia and binge eating for few years. i said in my journal, that im the last aprox 2 months i gained about 4-5 kg of lean muscles doing what i just described and my body fat percentage (the classic conductivity measurements since i have no access toa nything better went from 20 to 11%. I use mainly vegetables, some little honey, some pumpkin, squash or sweet potato, coconut and rarely berries, but as the season is coming i will eat more berries now. Just offt he topic after i went yesteday to test my new muscles and came home shocked. I did quite rough hiit running interval training, rather on the edge of lengt. This I did. fter 5 months refeeding, having done mostly nothing and last 3 months some yoga and max 10-15 min daily some hiit exercises (not running). I did almost as good yesterday as during my running career, too good to believe it, really. I felt like a superwoman and quite a few runners and the boys in the outdoor gym station i passed by were waving and gesturing i am in form. funny, no, after 5 months of doing nothing! well i own this to Jack and his wise blog!

### SeaHorse — 2014-04-09T17:59:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dave-asprey.10486/post-120629>

> [cantweight said:](/goto/post?id=120618)
>
> I always joke with my hubby that Dave is my brother from another mother. With the lyme, hashis, mold, methylation, obesity....we have a lot in common and I love that he has all those crappy things and is "Bulletproof". Has always been a huge source of inspiration to me knowing this crap wont stand in my way.
>
> The situation he described with a too low carb diet is exactly what I have been experiencing. The amonia smell in my urine, I have developed an allergy to eggs, and I feel so crappy. Over the past few months I have had pho with rice noddles a couple times and when I did I felt amazing! I have been telling myself I just need some carbs but thought it was the fat lady inside just trying to rationalize a way to get something I wouldnt give her...lol
>
> So between Jeremy and now Dave I am waking up to fact I may just be in the need a little more carbs group.

It would be interesting to see if any of us who have had the increased sensitivity to foods and emf along with ammonia issues when we went no/low carb are living in optimal environments. After two weeks in Arizona in the sun I was eating all kinds of things that would have put me in bed with headaches, inflammation etc. etc. (Also a big fan of molybdenum now after taking if for the past month or so) I think we have to keep adjusting and adjusting throughout the year depending on where we live. Kind of like your son and the magnetico….a great idea for part of the year, but for him things needed to change when the sun was less available.

This makes a lot of the advise so conditional….I know coffee is still a bad idea for me, but after last nights webinar I WANT it to be a good idea. Perhaps this summer I'll try again.

And the whole process of convincing yourself that a nice bit of chocolate is so full of antioxidants when you know it's the slippery slope….well don't get me started. I'm truly over that delusion. (Just saying for myself, wouldn't be able to say for anyone else)

Really enjoyable webinar mostly because Dave just seems like a happy passionate guy….that's a great success story in itself.

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-09T20:47:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dave-asprey.10486/post-120652>

> [CTforlife said:](/goto/post?id=120645)
>
> I was under the impression that cold thermogensis and keto diet was the key to reversing every disease and folding error, but I guess I could be incorrect, unless I just read my bible, CT 6...
> I will stand up for no carbs and the cold.

I think it is CT. However for some of us we might need more carbs to function based upon our biology. This winter I was full Keto (CT was extremely difficult due to weather and exploding estrogen dominance) everything was tight. but I still gained 30lbs and my thyroid was crazy with hyper labs and hypo symptoms but no positive antibodies. I had cravings for sweet potatoes. I have asthma and OSA - both of which drive hypoxia. As Jeremy pointed out in the Q&A w/Jack - he can't eat like Jack. he needs carbs - and it got me thinking... I k now it got several others thinking too.

Given I've been Keto for over a year - my glucose is good but my postparindal runs on average close to 170 after meals and I'm starting to see beta cell stress - my PCP who's a huge advocate of keto wants me to add in carbs. Now we're not talking 300g of carbs. she wants to see how I do if I eat close to 50g and see if that will impact my labs...

I've got other things going on as well.. but we're working on it... Crazy is working.... Right now I just need a little help. a 90 day biohack of adding in carbs while their inseason makes sense to me...
