# Thread: So, you think Obama care is OK?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-obama-care-is-ok.10547/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 42
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-15T05:50:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-obama-care-is-ok.10547/post-121570>

[http://youngcons.com/amazing-open-l...ma-mr-president-im-leaving-the-medical-field/](http://youngcons.com/amazing-open-letter-from-a-young-doctor-to-obama-mr-president-im-leaving-the-medical-field/)

### cmegan — 2014-04-15T10:33:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-obama-care-is-ok.10547/post-121588>

Having been in healthcare since the early 80's nothing has saddened my heart more than watching this monstrosity unfold. And nothing has been more frustrating than trying to explain to laypeople the complexities of why this has the potential to destroy one of the greatest healthcare systems in the world. The letter above is just one example, losing some of our brightest young minds. Human nature naturally gravitates towards a free market...it is instinctive.

Obamacare is not about providing good care to many people, it's just a cog in the socialist wheel. We didn't need to lower the common denominator for the majority to figure out how to provide coverage for the minority of the people that can't afford coverage. One of my cousins said to me recently "Thanks to Obamacare now poor children can have health care!" Well, actually, they always did have health coverage! It's called medicaid, and most medicaid patients get better and more timely care than patients in socialized systems. I know first hand, I've worked in the public health system.

Anyway, I could go on and on and on about this...the letter you posted really struck a cord with me. The first thing that came to mind during the initial stirrings of obamacare was the potential loss of smart, dedicated young minds entering into the field of medicine.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-04-15T11:32:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-obama-care-is-ok.10547/post-121602>

I know this is not a 'political blog' but I have a whole different take on this. The 'free market' is a disaster for medicine and Obama's big mistake was to try to incorporate the 'free market'. I come from Ireland and spend time in England....................I would from a 'customer' point of view much much prefer their 'socialized' system. Making vast amounts of money from drugs, specialized procedures etc is just plain crazy. The US is gradually and will have to much more realize the limitations of the so called 'free market'

### Gracelind — 2014-04-15T12:08:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-obama-care-is-ok.10547/post-121605>

American healthcare has not been subject to anything like a "free market" for a very long time. What we have experienced for most of our lives has little resemblance to a free market. Govt meddling prompted by undue influence of big pharma, insurance industry, and large medical associations, to name a few, has terribly distorted the ways in which medical care is researched, offered, and paid for.

Pat, did you see Gretchen's cite on how the RUC sets many of the limits on payments for Medicare and by extension for other insurance companies? Eye-opening.

For my own case, yes, I have benefited by the availability of insurance formerly denied due to pre-existing AI condition. This could have been accomplished without dismantling so much of the recent healthcare system. I was able to replace an employer-sponsored PPO that proposed a premium increase to $29,000/year for my husband and me with a plan that costs $12,000 (for just me) with no deductible. A lot of money--but a bargain compared to the coverage we've had. I chose this plan in December after assuring that it covered the university med school/teaching hospital where I get most of my care and do most of the labs for my AI condition. Stupid me for not checking again before Mar 31. The university has been dropped and is no longer a contract provider. Thanks to the ACA even though my plan was purchased privately and not through the state exchange, I can't change insurance companies until the next open enrollment period in November. Well, if I divorced my husband or became widowed (major life change) I could possibly make a switch. The $600 office visit charge is now $550 out of pocket. Can't even imagine what the lab charges would be.

The ACA was a poorly written bill not even read nor understood by most of the legislators who voted for it. But it clearly has the purpose of moving the country toward a single-payer system.

This is why its important to understand how to leverage the insurance you've got, and make it work for you...

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-obama-care-is-ok.10547/post-121664>

Yeah but................I KNOW I know nothing about Obamacare. Right now I don't even HAVE insurance or WANT insurance, I would prefer to spend money being healthy than 'insurance' that's why I am glad to spend my $99.00 with Dr Jack instead and pay the fine for not having ins.

So in the details I am sure you guys know what you are talking about but I still feel in the big picture you may be missing a lot. Not your fault you had the misfortune ONLY to know the American Way believe me there are lots of better ways. Obamacare is a mess for sure because it tried to build on a broken system a system based at least on a so called 'free market'. I have noticed this kind of objection before in say the financial sector people say oh the system broke down because it was not deregulated ENEOUGH, there was still too much Govt interference..................with all due respects I feel this is totally off base

This is a Right Wing trope and sad for me to see people fall for it. Same here in California when they deregulated electricity and every thing went haywire some people (Right Wingers) said 'oh the problem was there was STILL too much government' That is crazy the 'free market' is nonsense and people will slowly and painfully realize that.............after everything crashes..............It is correct there is no real 'free market' there is always SOME Government involvement but to conclude from that that the problem is the 'Government' part and not the 'free market' part is to me a major mistake and just a Right Wing trope that you guys are swallowing. This is a big problem now in the US especially in politics..........people have NEVER known good government, it has always been shot true with money making (free market so called) so they literally don't know any better, it is a bit like telling people about real food having never seen or eaten any..................

If you think 'philosophers' like Ayn Rand have any anwers you are in for a very bumpy ride and a bad end IMHO..............

### joangtaylor@gmail.com — 2014-04-15T20:41:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-obama-care-is-ok.10547/post-121701>

I do agree that the cost of medical education is a huge problem, herding medical students into higher paying specialty practices and thinning the ranks of primary care. Nevertheless, with all due respect, and knowing that I won't change anyone here's mind, I just want to agree with what Patrick has said. There can be no truly "free market" in medicine because a free market depends upon all parties to a transaction having all relevant information. Not the case in healthcare. Moreover, we spend more money than any other country on medical care but our healthcare system ranks surprisingly low (37th in in the world). Obamacare is flawed, but it is a step in the right direction, getting more citizens covered and prohibiting discrimination based on pre-existing conditions.

[http://www.forbes.com/sites/rickungar/2012/02/12/proof-of-the-failure-of-free-markets-in-medicine/](http://www.forbes.com/sites/rickungar/2012/02/12/proof-of-the-failure-of-free-markets-in-medicine/)
[http://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMp0910064](http://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMp0910064)
[http://krugman.blogs.nytimes.com/20...t-cure-healthcare/?\_php=true&\_type=blogs&\_r=0](http://krugman.blogs.nytimes.com/2009/07/25/why-markets-cant-cure-healthcare/?_php=true&_type=blogs&_r=0)

This is why I wrote this [blog](http://bit.ly/TakingtheTaxpayersforaRide). People need to understand the Healthcare system in the US (both before and after Obamacare) isn't a free market. We brought this upon ourselves b/c we don't treat health care as consumers should. I have friends who don't have insurance and they're very particular what docs they're going to see, and pay $$$ to. This is a big problem now in the US especially in politics..........people have NEVER known good government, it has always been shot true with money making (free market so called) so they literally don't know any better, it is a bit like telling people about real food having never seen or eaten any..................

Pat - your choice highlights that while you tout big gov't or gov't intervention, you're choosing not to buy the product it's selling. And yes our gov't is bought by large corporations. Things changed when the 17th Amendment changed how Senators were selected. moving from selection by the State legislatures to the popular vote. There was a reason the original constitution was drafted to ensure State Rights, and limit federal gov't stepping out of bounds and limit influences from business.

That said there are very few entrepreneurs, small business owners, or individuals who would willingly give up 60% or more of their annual salary to fully support a socialist medical system like Sweden. Mr. Buffett can freely write an additional check to the IRS every April 15th, yet he doesn't. I don't know about you but I'd rather have my money to spend on the medical care I want, verses being told what doctors I can see and which one's I can't b/c I should be the norm, nor do I want to go back to waiting 6-8 weeks to get in to see my primary care doc or 6-18 months to see a specialist (these are the current wait times the VA has)

However I do believe that we can change how we get services. We need to start asking for the information upfront.

> [Rylee said:](/goto/post?id=121713)
>
> What country do you live in? Docs in the US aren't federal workers (unless they work specifically for the VA or something).

Rylee I believe cmegan was referring to VA, Military, & US Public Health Service Docs. US Public Health Service is one of the largest employers for doctors. USPHS is the medical arm of Health and Human Services. USPHS docs fill positions in Indian Health Services, CDC, and provide medical support for Embassies overseas. USPHS also run Medical Operations on the USS Comfort & Mercy (US Navy owns the ships and drives them but USPHS mans the medical staff). I've worked with VA, Military, and USPHS docs as part of my day job. there are great ones, good ones, mediocre ones, and those who are wasting oxygen waiting for their federal retirement to kick in. In addition to those docs, the Federal Gov't also contracts doctors as federal employees with federal retirements, the difference is contracted docs can be fired for poor performance, where as the Docs working in the VA/Military/USPHS don't get fired they get promoted to keep them from practicing medicine. Which is what happens in big hospital companies too...

### Marie — 2014-04-16T01:19:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-obama-care-is-ok.10547/post-121742>

Personally I LOVE "Obamacare". (And I have to laugh at the nickname as it was a 100 year bipartisan process to get the ACA passed. Look at the history of US health care policy, if you don't believe me.)

**The ACA is _more about National economics_ and (hopefully) preventing a National bankruptcy _than about truly providing better health care_.** Currently large organizations hold employees hostage by providing health care coverage. Big Biz holds the most productive, innovative groups hostage, just as they are starting families and buying mortgages. They also prevent smaller/new organizations from competing with them fairly. These are the same years true entrepreneurs bloom - yet the fear of having their family unprotected from sky-rocketing health care costs (+$7500/pp in the US and climbing) allows Big Biz to artificially keep salaries low, through these institutional handcuffs, to boost their profit margins.

This decreases the amount of entrepreneurship and lowers the "quits rate", both of which we need to increase in order to make our economy healthy. Increasing both of these economic markers raises the tax base and allows the government to pay down our debt.

We are a Nation of Creatives, born of people who (mostly) fled bad circumstances because they believed they could create something better. It is this creativity and drive that has produced so much innovation and economic strength for the past 250 years. We are a nation of Dopamine addicts, which I believe has been a process of 4 massive culls beginning in Africa and ending on the West Coast (so far).

What happens when Dopamine addicts are handcuffed by Cortisol? It isn't pretty and you can see it every day.

Anyway, back to the topic of our broken health care system and economics.

Just as the body needs an annual seasonal cycle, societies need economics cycles. This is why we get a depression every 70-80 years. Hmmm... a four generational cycle... interesting... ringing some bells? How does that work? It works just as our seasons work. We are currently in the Winter season where Ketogenics are a must. Everything that is unnecessary must be stripped to the core. One must become lean and fit for the Spring, so those systems that are not working must be burned to the ground.

Look back at the 1930s. What was happening then? ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New\_Deal)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Deal) What was happening to Big Biz? to health care policy? Look at generational behavior. Do you see the similarities in your children between the ages of 15-25. What are their concerns? What do they value? These values reflect neurological function, right? Based on these values, what can you predict?

These economic cycles are all just natures way of providing change/evolution. However, they reflect a deep neurological basis as those in their 40s experienced a vastly different economic cycle in their childhoods to the current one. Think about how today's leadership experienced the late 60s-mid 80s during their childhood. That childhood provided the perfect grounding to allow for tough decision-making. (They were alone a lot as the first generation of latchkey kids with dual income households, plus a bunch of other stuff that happened. Including things that happened to their grandparents in the epigenetic make up, this cohort is filled with tough little bastards.) How many of you in the 35-55 y/o cohort have a difficult time making decisions? Not many. This generation is good at ground breaking and creating momentum, not so good at social connections and platitudes.

Back to economics.

Why would the government need to break down the medical system? Why are you here? The current system doesn't work. It is just as bloated as the banks and financial institutions were. Look around you - are WE healthy? Are WE getting value for our $500K tax dollars that subsidizes the training of new MDs? As consumers, we're being screwed.

The problem is that, as you saw from the mortgage crisis, goods and services must have value. When they don't, a bubble is created as the cost outstrips the value. The escalation in health costs mirrors the mortgage crisis and will bankrupt our country if not fixed. (Will it be the next bubble, in 70 years, I don't think so. But if it is, it won't be as "easy" to fix as the mortgage crisis.)

Do I mind that MDs are quitting? Nope. I've had to fire too many of them, so obviously the supply chain isn't working if 80% of the MDs I've consulted can't answer simple biochemical questions, or worse, they've proposed treatments I know are unnecessary, but will dramatically increase their reimbursement rate. (I swear, some MDs play the ICD codes like a blackjack table and know the payout stats for every scenario.)

_Do I blame the MDs?_ **ABSOLUTELY NOT.**

_Behavior that is rewarded is repeated._ (Now we're back to biochemistry!) It is the system which rewards procedure over performance, encourages patient turnover (customer attrition), and selectively burns out some of the best diagnosticians while rewarding those who "manage" illness with willy-nilly prescriptions based on prejudicial "evidence based medicine" published in academic journals. Good MDs are just as victimized as the patients.

So what do I hope comes out of the ACA?

Well, for me it lowered my premiums from $1800/mo to $300, thanks to a pre-existing condition. This has given me the freedom to be a free agent (grad student) for a while and to study how the current system works, or doesn't work. From a personal note, it has been great.

But from a broader perspective, I'm happy my students can now receive affordable health insurance so they aren't bankrupted by car accidents, bar fights, or other crazy things that we all do in our 20s. I've coached too many of my students to negotiate down the atrocious medical costs, payments, (and interest rates) and to understand how these payments and the corresponding low credit scores can damage one's life for decades. It is the risk to this younger generation that the ACA was truly meant to cover, as those faced with predatory pricing in crisis truly have no choice.

What was happening to our youth was truly evil and I'm thankful _something_ was done. It is a start and those who wrote the bill knew, and fully admitted it would need tweaks along the way. It is a work in process and I feel there is nothing wrong with that.

From the broadest perspective, I'm enjoying watching the generational shifts and responses to the changes. We have about 12 more years of economic ketogenics, so hang on. Ever wonder why SEVEN million have already signed up? Look at the voting record by cohort in the last Presidential election... then think about how their brains developed.

It is interesting stuff.

I am also hopeful, but I understand that is part of being a creative.

Marie

### Gracelind — 2014-04-16T02:21:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-obama-care-is-ok.10547/post-121748>

No one denies that the American healthcare system and the means by which it is paid for need to be improved.

The ACA does not/cannot achieve resolution of very many of the problems it was promised to solve. It has no real mechanisms for lowering costs. It doesn't reform the malpractice system that is a terrible drain on the resources of our doctors, hospitals, and other providers of medical care. It perpetuates and even contributes to deeper lack of competition among insurance providers, creating fewer choices for patients. It greatly reduces the range of providers patients can consult under their policies.

It interferes in the business decisions made by companies. It's a major disincentive to small business to hire new employees or to give longer hours to full-time employees. Reports are that 5-6million formerly insured people lost their coverage because it didn't conform to the coverage required by the ACA (coverage that demands premiums for services most insureds will never use such as pediatric vision care). At this time how many of those bought insurance through the exchanges is not clear. In the next round many millions more are expected to lose coverage that will have to be replaced or penalty paid.

In the process of campaigning for passage and then for enrollment in plans, the highest leaders in our government felt compelled to grossly misrepresent what this law can do and how it will affect those who up until then had healthcare coverage and doctors they liked. The damage done by misinformation, outright lies, broken promises, and devastating unintended or unpublicized consequences is deep. The process of passing this law required questionable if not outright illegal procedures that have created deep rifts in both Congress and the American public.

What no one is discussing right now is the rest of the Act which mandated the creation of something like 118 expensive new govt agencies--each with its own hierarchy of staff--most of which are aimed at social engineering and have little to do with the actual providing of health care to patients by physicians and hospitals.

I'm very happy for those who benefit from lower premiums and govt subsidies--to them I say as an American taxpayer, "You're welcome." Though no one has ever actually thanked the taxpayers yet. While enjoying the personal benefits that some are receiving, it's important to also consider the wider damage the ACA is wreaking on healthcare and the economy.

BTW reports are that very few physicians were consulted, outside the AMA, in the process of drafting the Bill. Big pharma, insurance companies, and other organizations who saw opportunity for profit (such as AARP) were at the table. Legislators from one political party were either not allowed into meetings or if allowed were silenced (I watched on C-SPAN). This seems to be a theme of govt, most recently for the current Administration, to be very selective about who is invited to the table and allowed input, to carefully, almost surgically remove opposition until the final step when an actual vote is required.

FWIW I've been politically independent for many years. Neither party speaks for me. In my experience both parties have some good ideas and some destructive ones. I was at least as critical and disgusted with the previous Administration, and the one before that. I have observed some benefits and laudable actions within both political parties. What I most deeply desire is the health, safety, and solvency of my country--for now, for our grandchildren and their grandchildren.

The path the ACA is taking us down is not the way.

### Marie — 2014-04-16T04:57:02+0300

### Gracelind — 2014-04-16T08:32:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-obama-care-is-ok.10547/post-121795>

As part of the campaign to sell their plan proponents from the President on down promised many times to lower healthcare costs and to lower premiums by $2500 for each family.

I watched the process unfold very carefully and heard doctor after doctor say that they had never been consulted and that expressions of concern and offers of input were ignored.

You're right that there was another economic agenda having little to do with medicine and Dr Emmanuel (brother of Rahm) among others had a big hand.

Most of us are here because mainstream conventional medicine has failed us. Even within the constraints of recent insurance policy Dr Kruse has been able to offer his patients innovative and effective pre- and post-surgery protocols including nutrition advice and CT that improve their healing.

Will he be able to provide this kind of care in the face of govt mandates to standardize treatment inline with "evidence-based" treatment? Will he be able to give the time to each patient and comply with govt demands for "efficiency"?

As one with major AI issues I know that many of the meds used in treatment are off-label usage because there are few studies of the kind the med establishment requires. Will this kind of treatment still be available?

How many times have our members from Europe or the Commonwealth bemoaned not being able to get certain lab tests or to have access to doctors for alternative non-mainstream care?

More often than not monolithic bureaucracy squelches innovation and encourages mediocracy. That's not what I want for my own care or for my country.

All of these just some of the reasons I find the ACA not only inadequate but destructive.

# Thread: Let's talk about Poop Baby...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-poop-baby.10549/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaulG — 2014-04-15T08:18:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-poop-baby.10549/post-121580>

Sorry to lower the tone but I wanna talk about poop...

Nutritionally I had a relatively good day yesterday although more food than I normally eat

8am Large bowl of leftover liver, bacon and sweet potato followed by scrambled eggs on gluten free toast plus bacon
2pm steamed mussels followed by greek yoghurt (hmm!) despite not feeling hungry
7pm Salmon, ratatouille, sweet potato with a glass of local red wine
8pm Whole bar of 70% dark chocolate
1am - Finally go to bed after finishing the wine bottle (always go to bed around 9/10pm)

This morning...slightly hung over but my poop is horendous, dark black, foul smelling (which also occurs when I eat cashews)

So, I have a blind spot, I dont get constipation or diarrhea and I go every morning but the foul smell is something I should be paying attention to and in that subconscious part of my mind where I imagine I am training on Dagobah
to be an epi-paleo [jedi master](http://m.youtube.com/watch?v=S5ZsMBLUsqU), the ghost voices of selectively ignored Amy Yasko inspired methylation posts whisper in the style of Yoda "[Address you must first CBS mutation](http://www.mthfrsupport.com/other-gene-mutations-that-must-be-addressed-before-starting-an-mthfr-protocol/)". "Bad is high protein Paleo diet", "sulphur and thiol foods dark side surely lead to" "Join us you must..True is the path for Vegan worrior"

But I don't want to throw out the baby with the bath water. And is that smell really ammonia or something else such as sulphur or hydrogen sulfide (if I were a chemist I could sniff these in the lab) or do I have [Helicobacter pylori](http://www.medfriendly.com/helicobacterpylori.html)

So, rather than bury my head in the sand I resolve to become a poop jedi so that I can talk sh\*t with true poop jedis such as [Sean Croxton](http://m.youtube.com/watch?v=0WQaqeC_wME)

as Jack says when you know better you do better...so [lets talk about poop baby](http://m.youtube.com/watch?v=ydrtF45-y-g) as we all do it even educated fleas do it.

**Poop resources**
[http://www.enzymestuff.com/rtstools.htm](http://www.enzymestuff.com/rtstools.htm)
[http://articles.mercola.com/sites/articles/archive/2013/02/14/normal-stool.aspx](http://articles.mercola.com/sites/articles/archive/2013/02/14/normal-stool.aspx)
[http://www.chirocolonics.com/stoolchart.html](http://www.chirocolonics.com/stoolchart.html)
[http://www.emilysstomach.com/2013/06/poop-what-different-colors-and-smells.html](http://www.emilysstomach.com/2013/06/poop-what-different-colors-and-smells.html)
[http://www.thenaturalrecoveryplan.com/articles/The-Scoop-on-Poop.html](http://www.thenaturalrecoveryplan.com/articles/The-Scoop-on-Poop.html)

**Toilet humour**

### caroline — 2014-04-15T11:12:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-poop-baby.10549/post-121596>

wow Paul .... you eat a lot!!! I know you are a big guy ....but holy smokes ........

Do you normally eat all those things? gluten free bread? yogurt? wine? Ratatouille? 70% chocolate?

### caroline — 2014-04-15T11:13:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-poop-baby.10549/post-121597>

### PaulG — 2014-04-15T12:15:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-poop-baby.10549/post-121608>

Gluten free products I try to avoid but they are in the house for the kids, yoghurt and wine not normally, chocolate a lot.

The raw thing I need to explore more.

### sooperb — 2014-04-15T13:18:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-poop-baby.10549/post-121614>

Chocolate, a whole bar, would be the likely culprit.

I see a lot of dark green spinach coloured stools, I assume it's the greens chugging through. Since I reintroduced the oxbile it's supercharged.

### kovita — 2014-04-15T14:28:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-poop-baby.10549/post-121617>

think of yoghurt as well. The usual store yoghurt is packed with cassein. Some people (my husband) tend to react dramatically to highfat cassein loaded dairy. I would not like to blame the wine, too good to be the truth, but most of the wines (specially the red ones) contain sulfites. To many people this is a garantee fro diarrhea. Last and but not least you have more stress last days, this always shows up in digestion.

### nicld — 2014-04-15T18:21:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-poop-baby.10549/post-121666>

Great thread

I have become way more aware of what is going on and now I might need to dig a little deeper into it. I am fairly regular, between 6:30 am and 8 am everyday, after I eat and get the beeeeeeep out of my way. I have gone from C to D now and as much as I dislike the D I would rather have that than the C.

Everything changed after I was treated for SIBO last year which has made me dig way deeper into gut issues. Now I am on Ox bile and a bunch of other stuff to help digest the fats and proteins since I have no gall bladder. Also being treated for microplasma biofilms and a gut infection so who knows what is making it wonky.

### caroline — 2014-04-16T00:26:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-poop-baby.10549/post-121727>

70% chocolate has a lot of sugar ..... I found some 90% Lindt at woolies the other day - the first time I have found it.

I would like to start here a thread on th1 shift. It would be great to know if anyone of you is dealing with it, the strategies you pursue and the success/experience you made.

### kovita — 2014-04-15T11:09:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/th1-shift-anyone.10550/post-121595>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=121592)
>
> I don't understand?

Caroline, th1 shift in your immune system. Meaning that your body is producing mainly cytotoxic immune response. normally there is a longtemr bLance between the th1 (cytotoxic) and th2 (antibody) response of your immune system. Cytotoxic response is used to fight intracellular parazites (viruses, bugs like chlamydium, mycoplasma, borrelia etc.) and antobodies are used to attack cellular parazites (anything else starting by the prokaryota till visible parazites).

# Thread: The definition of the word quantum ......
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-definition-of-the-word-quantum.10551/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sooperb — 2014-04-15T13:14:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-definition-of-the-word-quantum.10551/post-121613>

**Definition of _quantum_ in English:**
**quantum**

**noun (plural quanta /-tə/)**

-   1 _Physics_ A discrete quantity of energy proportional in magnitude to the frequency of the radiation it represents.
    More example sentences
-   1.1An analogous discrete amount of any other physical quantity, such as momentum or electric charge.
    More example sentences
-   1.2 _Physiology_ The unit quantity of acetylcholine released at a neuromuscular junction by a single synaptic vesicle, contributing a discrete small voltage to the measured end-plate potential.
    More example sentences

-   2A required or allowed amount, especially an amount of money legally payable in damages: _the court must determine the quantum of compensation due_
    More example sentences
-   2.1A share or portion: _each man has only a quantum of compassion_

Are we sure we all understand the correct usage of the word?

# Thread: Internet-addicted infants "lack motor skills"
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/internet-addicted-infants-lack-motor-skills.10552/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 34
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-15T20:25:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/internet-addicted-infants-lack-motor-skills.10552/post-121695>

> **Rising numbers of infants lack the motor skills needed to play with building blocks** because of an “addiction” to tablet computers and smartphones, according to teachers.
>
> Many children aged just three or four can “swipe a screen” but have little or no dexterity in their fingers after spending hours glued to iPads, it was claimed. …

"It was claimed" is suitably cautious. I'd not be surprised if this were true, however. The rest of the article ought to disturb people's complacency, too, but I'm not sure it will.

> Experts have warned that the growth is having a serious effect on children’s social and physical development.
>
> Last year, a doctor claimed that rising numbers of young people – including one aged just four – required therapy for compulsive behaviour after being exposed to the internet and digital devices from birth. …
>
> Mark Montgomery, a teacher from Northern Ireland, said overexposure to technology had been linked to weight gain, aggressive behaviour, tiredness and repetitive strain injury.
>
> He called on parents to turn home wi-fi off overnight to stop children staying awake to play online games on iPads.

Much more here:

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/educatio...oy-building-blocks-due-to-iPad-addiction.html](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/education/educationnews/10767878/Infants-unable-to-use-toy-building-blocks-due-to-iPad-addiction.html)

Changing the subject somewhat ...

"Turn off wi-fi overnight" is telling. People leave it on permanently. I never have and can't really fathom the laziness behind the reluctance to flip a switch.

Interestingly, BT the national telecoms provider in the UK says it has "millions" of wi-fi "hotspots" worldwide. I haven't tried to confirm this by looking online but my guess is that when they send you out a router they have it set to accept connections from any BT user who inputs his or her user credentials. How else could they have _millions_ of wi-fi connections available? It would certainly explain why they are so eager to send you out a router: I had one, but they insisted on sending me their own when I signed up for service with them and made out that they were doing me a favour by "only" charging me a few quid for it. it's a pretty powerful one -- 811 g or n or the latest whatever with a pretty powerful signal that will go through walls and clean across the street for sure.

I have, of course, surfed to the IP address that is assigned to the router and turned wireless off, using hardwired ethernet cable instead. The router doesn't like it, though. Every time I turn it on it take a few minutes going through a sequence of different coloured flashing lights before it finally "decides" it's not going to be able to be open to wireless, gives up, and settles down with a solid blue light, indicating "ready".

If BT are doing what I think they're doing, a national campaign for people to switch their routers off when they're not using them, as the teacher from Ulster who's quoted wants, would sure put a spoke in their wheel.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-16T19:57:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/internet-addicted-infants-lack-motor-skills.10552/post-121912>

Why do infants have this happen........think Jeremy and Temple Grandin. electron steal syndrome from the subcutaneous fat cells of kids is why. the silicon semiconductors cause an electron depletion because the childs' semiconductors are carbon based. Any place two dissimilar semiconductors occur we get avalanche collapse of the lower powered system. Now consider the purpose of subcutaneous fat in the human infant. Please carefully re read EE1.

Implications:

FIAF builds the sub cutaneous fat mass in humans. FIAF grows when their is a scarcity signal. This implies that the gut microbiota has to take a lot of the electrons in food for themselves. This is also why the human gut shortened from primates. When it shortened it helped craft the FIAF signal to develop sub cutaneous fat mass to support post natal brain growth. The second ecologic effect of a shorted gut is it created an island of isolation for the microbiota. This gave the human gut microbiome a severe diversity compared to other primates. Our teeth are very different from other primates as well and do not allow us to chew as well. Babies have no teeth. In fact children also have a primary dentition that really are underdeveloped.........EVER WONDER WHY??

This delivers more unprocessed food to this diverse microbiome which makes things for us that drive organ health in many places to continue to under process food for the growing brain to provide it with a massive influx of electrons and not PROTONS. PROTONS are for the gut bugs when O2 is not present.

. The underpinning here is when you are isolating and shortening your gut for the microbiome it implies you can only do this when food sources with a dense source of electrons are present. this is the marine food chain. This is why human evolution began in the East African rift adjacent to the ocean. The electron is massless and can carry huge amounts of energy and information at a low thermodynamic cost compared to a proton. This is why humans have built their most complex systems, the brain and immune systems, around electrons and not protons. Protons are used to signal in the mitochondria the signals the environment provides. These signals then tell the cytochromes how to process foods.......in cytochrome 1 or two. The other cytochromes are used to signal the change programs in mitochondria, namely autophagy and apoptosis by opening a pore in the mitochondrial membrane to increase mitochondrial size. This is how heteroplasmy occurs in our mitochondria in centenarians.

The better the redox potential the more heteroplasmy we see because it tells us the organism is best able to handle the changing signals in the environment. When the redox potential is not as good we employ too much apoptosis and not enough autophagy.......and we deplete stem cells. Where do the human stem cells come from? The most plastic human stem cell is in subcutaneous fat. To access those stem cells requires a massive stream of electrons in the sympathetic nervous system. Beta 3 agonists increase adiponectin and allow us to tap the stem cells.......alpha 2 agonists in SNS shut it off. Leptin resistance increases the alpha two pathway and shuts down the beta 3. Remember CD36 in the mouth we spoke about long ago? It stimulates the beta 3 agonist pathways. CD36 is a mouth receptor for fats in the MOUTH. What else activates beta 3 pathways? Cold weather. Anyone see why CT makes sense yet? This is the pathway where ox/phos is uncoupled and the potential energy locked within the mass filled particle, the proton is used to alter the function of UCP1 to generate free heat and not make any ATP. Protons are used for heat generation and not electrons.........which are reserved for human complex systems

In fact children also have a primary dentition that really are underdeveloped.........EVER WONDER WHY??
>
> This delivers more unprocessed food to this diverse microbiome which makes things for us that drive organ health in many places to continue to under process food for the growing brain to provide it with a massive influx of electrons and not PROTONS. PROTONS are for the gut bugs when O2 is not present.
>
> . The underpinning here is when you are isolating and shortening your gut for the microbiome it implies you can only do this when food sources with a dense source of electrons are present. this is the marine food chain. This is why human evolution began in the East African rift adjacent to the ocean. The electron is massless and can carry huge amounts of energy and information at a low thermodynamic cost compared to a proton. This is why humans have built their most complex systems, the brain and immune systems, around electrons and not protons. Protons are used to signal in the mitochondria the signals the environment provides. These signals then tell the cytochromes how to process foods.......in cytochrome 1 or two. The other cytochromes are used to signal the change programs in mitochondria, namely autophagy and apoptosis by opening a pore in the mitochondrial membrane to increase mitochondrial size. This is how heteroplasmy occurs in our mitochondria in centenarians.
>
> The better the redox potential the more heteroplasmy we see because it tells us the organism is best able to handle the changing signals in the environment. When the redox potential is not as good we employ too much apoptosis and not enough autophagy.......and we deplete stem cells. Where do the human stem cells come from? The most plastic human stem cell is in subcutaneous fat. To access those stem cells requires a massive stream of electrons in the sympathetic nervous system. Beta 3 agonists increase adiponectin and allow us to tap the stem cells.......alpha 2 agonists in SNS shut it off. Leptin resistance increases the alpha two pathway and shuts down the beta 3. Remember CD36 in the mouth we spoke about long ago? It stimulates the beta 3 agonist pathways. CD36 is a mouth receptor for fats in the MOUTH. What else activates beta 3 pathways? Cold weather. Anyone see why CT makes sense yet? This is the pathway where ox/phos is uncoupled and the potential energy locked within the mass filled particle, the proton is used to alter the function of UCP1 to generate free heat and not make any ATP. Protons are used for heat generation and not electrons.........which are reserved for human complex systems

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=121917)
>
> So if you're following........when your leptin sensitive why would a guy on the internet tell you to IF?
>
> IFing also provides the "scarcity signal" needed to make a diverse microbiome that take the protons and not the electrons from our food.........but it requires a good redox potential in the gut with exclusion of O2 within the lumen........to reserve electrons for us and give protons to the microbiome.
>
> What did I say in the April webinar Q & A about oxygen? Electrons carry energy and information
>
> Electrons build complexity.
>
> What do humans have that primates do not?
>
>
> Where do we develop that complexity?
>
> Still think macronutrients and calories matter?
>
> Only a simple mind does.............
>
> BOOM

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=121919)
>
> And Gretchen.........knowing about your kids.......this should really hammer home everything I have told you bout them.

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=121961)
>
> The more you do not know the more you learn because you focus. This is why the most successful people are dyslexics. This is why you are doing well here. You are not allowing your lack of learning stop you like it has stopped others.......when you emerge from your conclave you will be one very special starfish that can infect other starfish more than I can.........I am an innovator looking to create starfish with focus to do what I can not. In that sense I function a lot like our gut microbiota.

Yes Jack this resonates. for both me and the kids. What's interesting is when we're outside working in the yard I no longer have to tell them to take their shoes off. They do it automatically when we go outside.

Even more amazing - I strained/a ligament in my elbow back in January when I was having issues w/my shoulder dislocating (didn't know the shoulder dislocation impacted my elbow it at the time) but it finally caught up with me. My chiropractor has been working on helping me heal it with electrical stimulation and manipulation. She told me to take it easy. Instead I went out and gardened for two whole days - ripping up english ivy, planting new plants and digging in the dirt in general - out side with a tank top on. I've been drinking good water etc. She fully expected me to come in worse than I came in last friday. But my range of motion increased and she could tell I've already started healing. She's blown away by how quickly my body repairs itself. She actually told me I'm healing faster than I did back in November when I tore my calf muscle. Its all about free electrons. Its a little stiff today b/c yesterday I didn't use it at all and we spent to day at the Smithsonian Natural History Museum - so not as much free electrons - unless you count the big boulders we found to sit on after spending the day inside

I also thing each individuals Redox potential is completely driven by their own N=1, their connection with the natural environment and how their body leverages the advantages they have (asthma, sleep apnea, pcos) I full thing I need more carbs along the Leptin Rx right now - but that' doesn't mean I'm eating more every day. I'm interested to see how this all plays out.

Keep laying out the oyster shells. I'll use them to guide my journey. What I find interesting is your comment about dyslexia - its totally true. I do apply myself differently b/c of my dyslexia and its become a great advantage to me.

# Thread: A message to help parents understand JK
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-message-to-help-parents-understand-jk.10560/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Nick — 2014-04-16T12:59:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-message-to-help-parents-understand-jk.10560/post-121807>

Caroline thought I should publish this letter I sent to my father in the forum. I wasn't going to but maybe it will help someone else.

Dad is well'ish, but there are storm clouds brewing if he doesn't start to really nail down some important details. It is quite a challenge to try and communicate what JK is teaching us in a way that is easy to understand for a lay person that hasn't bashed through millions of words of blogs and hours of webinars. He is epi paleo but still has work to do in the other areas, water, sleep, eating times and blue light.

I finally seemed to have cracked an easy to understand explanation that starts with watching Pollacks water video.

Once an exclusion zone has been seen and the penny drops that this is like a car battery that powers life the rest of JK's informations fits in a lot more easily. At least it did for me!

Dad watched the video, was really amazed and came back asking if Dr Kruse was talking about water and EZ as well. My reply is below, I bashed it out in about 30 minutes, it is probably full of mistakes but regardless it did the trick, mum and dad are now really engaged and asking all the right questions.

Hope this might help someone else,

Cheers

Nick

### Nick — 2014-04-16T13:00:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-message-to-help-parents-understand-jk.10560/post-121808>

Hi Dad,

Really glad you finally got to watch the video, it is massive and is foundational to everything Kruse is saying. I’ll give you the summary of how it fits in in as it is hard to explain over the phone.

**The EZ Link**

The Exclusion Zone (EZ) appears anytime water is next to to a hydrophilic substance and heat or UVB is applied. Collagen and proteins in cells are hydrophilic. Collagen connects every part of the body and is always surrounded by water.

The sun is a virtual perpetual energy machine for creating an EZ in animals as there is always heat, even in winter. This is how it skirts around the second law of thermodynamics, creating order without expending energy (because you can’t flatten the sun). Water is amazing and unique in this respect. Decent UVB triples the size of the EZ, which is also a way of saying it triples the potential energy of the EZ.

Now you know why it feels good to sit in the sun.

The charge separation (size of the EZ zone) determines the ability of your body to repair itself. This is the findings of Robert Becker who discovered that the DC current in animals allows healing and limb regeneration.

Big EZ = great health. Small EZ = sickness, shortened lifespan.

We are always meant to be hydrated with water.

In summer we are designed to eat carbs (i.e. bananas) and get heaps of sunlight to create a big EZ. Significance of carbs is that it utilises the first cytochrome in the mitochondria and produces just 36 ATP for every mole of carb ingested.

In winter there is not enough UVB to increase the size of the EZ. We are designed to eat fatty meats (fish) in winter. Fat bypasses the first cytochrome and enters using the second cytochrome.

The second cytochrome produce 147 ATP for every mole of fat. Fat is the same as Ketones. Babies burn ketones exclusively for the first few years of their life from breast milk unless they are fed junk.

The function of the ATP is to unfold proteins in the mitochondria to bond with lots of water molecules. This bond creates a big EZ around the proteins to take from where the sun left off. Remember the proteins are hydrophilic and all it needs is water to create an EZ.

The EZ is used to generate energy for cells, the bigger the EZ the more energy there is to live optimally.

We use petrol in sumer and switch to diesel in winter. The coldness of winter also gives us a boost by increasing the size of the semi-conduction of the electrons in the EZ to create even more energy. Fat stores are also meant to be used in winter to provide extra fuel to cytochrome 2.

Think about a bear, eats berries all summer and gets fat, switches to salmon in autumn to go ketogenic, then hibernates in the cold in winter, burns fat, the cold makes muscle and then the bear appears in Spring ripped and ready to go.

This is all in a perfect world which used to exist until relatively recently.

**This is what happens now.**

People are dehydrated through EMF. This does a lot of bad things including lowering the size of the EZ dramatically.

People eat carbs all year round and never access the the second cytochrome pathway to produce a bigger EZ in winter. A carb in winter is a really bad idea as it shits down this pathway for a long time.

People never get cold any more meaning there is never a boost in semi conduction.

People drink fluoride that reduces the size of the EZ dramatically. It is a dielectric blocker, along with soy sauce and vegetable oil.

People are hit with UVB and are dehydrated which means there is no water to absorb the UVB and convert it to the EZ which results in skin cancer.

**Here are some more things that happens at a cytochrome.**

When the cell is hit with EMF two things happen. It is dehydrated of water and the water that remains has its PH lowered. This acidic water drops the voltage (mv) in the cell that allows an efflux of once tightly regulated calcium. One of the effects is that protons that are being produced by the mitochondria that are meant to be utilised by water now build up. Protons are a positive charge, a positive charge is inflammation. Oxidisation starts to build up know as Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) in the cell.

When this happens the mitochondria has trouble with respiration that changes the NAD+ and NADPH which causes a chain reaction.

This chain reaction spills out of the mitochondria and into the nuclear DNA using the mTor and other pathways which effects the P53 oncogene that protects the cell. This causes bad gene expression from the nuclear DNA which is the start of cancer.

This is brand new science from last year where 3 papers were published all coming to the same conclusion form different angles. Science has spent the last 40 years and $1Trillion dollars thinking that cancers starts at the nuclear DNA with genetic mutations where it looks like it starts way higher at the cytochromes in the mitochondria. Cancer is a water chemistry problem.

**Here are other links.**

Cysteine is converted to Glutathione which is critically used in the mitochondria to reduce the oxidisation. It is a massive antioxidant. This is especially important for the effect that EMF has on the transition metals in the mitochondria. This is a critical safety valve for managing the effect that EMF has on these metals and all other oxidisation issues in the cell.

Cysteine needs melatonin to convert to Glutathione. Melatonin is a function of circadian biology and is blocked/stopped in its tracks by late meals and any artificial light after dark.

EMF smashes the Brain Blood Barrier and the gut barrier. Think leaky gut and viral problems in brains. Putting a mobile phone to your head de-mylenates your brain stem in hours.

DHA and Iodine are critical to brain function. Fluoride binds to the same point that Iodine does.

Brain function in populations is dropping rapidly, there are a lot more mad people around let alone people with Autism, Parkinson's, Depression, Alzheimer's etc.

A magnetico helps tell the body what is day and what is night and aligns every molecule of water in your body for best possible semi conduction/lowest possible entropy.

When a body has high inflammation it has a large positive net charge, Earthing by standing on wet grass or in the ocean allows a flood of electrons back into the body to reduce this charge.

There is some serious quantum physics and complex biochemistry that I may never get my head around that explains this properly.

**To get well and live a long and healthy life**

To get well you just need to put fresh water into faster than the EMF can drain it (get a BUN/Creatine ratio of 7:1), wear blue blocker glasses at night, get plenty of sunlight in summer and watch your diet in winter to be ketogenic.

**All disease is reversible** using these principles. The sicker you are the stricter you need to be and more fine tuning you will need but it is all fixable.

**Some interesting facts.**

Einstein won his nobel prize for the photo electric effect. Edward Ling used this to come up with his original theory about an EZ. Lings papers are horrendously complicated but a guy worked with him in the 60’s to use this theory to invent the MRI. If Ling is wrong an MRI can’t work which it clearly does. Pollack picked up where Ling left off and luckily he is a great communicator.

Saint Georgy won the Nobel prize in the 30’s for his discovery of Vit C. The chemistry of Vitamin C dictated that there must be semi-conduction occurring and stated this is a talk in Budapest in 1942. A young surgeon named Robert O’Becker heard this talk and decided to investigate and discovered the DC current connection to wellness in all animals. That is how a Salamander regenerates its limb. He was nominated for two Nobel prizes. Just before Becker died in the late nineties he said that mankind biggest mistake ever was to let loose EMF. I have bought his book called ‘The body Electric’.

Beckers assistant for 20 years was fellow called Andy Marino who went on to become both a scientist, lawyer and expert on EMF. His story is detailed in a book called ‘Going Somewhere in Science’ which I also have. It is a remarkable story of how governments have hidden these effects and lawyers have killed off most law suits. Science and law both suck some days!!!

And then along came Kruse who has connected all of these dots, adding his own neurological knowledge and come up with a theory of the ascent of animal life post the CT event. He also uses these theories on his patients with great success, remember that Aussie gymnast with the broken back that went to the Beijing Olympics????

This all means that biology has very probably made a huge mistake about how a cell is powered, previously thought to be by the Sodium pump. Three Nobel prizes have been given out for this so there will be egg on lots of faces sometime in the future. Fundamental Cell biology has now began to be redrafted but it will be like changing the course of a super tanker with an out board engine. Think of the massive profits of the drug campaniles and even the Dr’s!

So there you go, rough dump of what I have been learning but you can see why there is such a mismatch between modern man and ancient biology and why disease types are going through the roof. I have probably made a few typos and skipped huge parts but the basics are all there.

I am off to drink lots more water, cook some prawns, put on the blue blockers and get an early nights sleep.

# Thread: Fat Bombs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-bombs.10561/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2014-04-16T16:01:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-bombs.10561/post-121851>

I've been craving fat for breakfast and am giving my body what it wants. Although my weight is up a couple of pounds, my clothes are fitting looser.

First I tried dali dula's drink that is found in the follwoing thread (very good):

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/first-experiment-ketosis.10432/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/first-experiment-ketosis.10432/)

Sometimes I am craving savory rather than sweet. Although this mixture seems bit odd it tastes good - I mix solid bacon fat, chopped boiled egg, and a little grated onion together.

This morning my fat bomb was 2 squares melted Lindt 90% chocolate, 1 TBL coconut oil, 2 TBL Kerrygold butter, and 2 TBL natural peanut butter. It's like a reeses peanut butter cup .

Anyone else have other "recipes" for fat bombs? Variety is the spice of life...

### 400lbbenchpress — 2014-06-11T05:37:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fatigued-but-determined.10563/post-130487>

You're a smart guy Dr. Kruse but I simply don't believe that sunlight, water, and DHA will cure a biotin transporter deficiency or make one able to function with nearly the energy everyone else has while operating with that gene. I don't believe it because I ate fish and swallowed fish oil pills daily, drank lots of water, exercised regularly, did not smoke, did not abuse my body, and still had CFS. I feel like you are smart enough to welcome a little difference of opinion. I agree that lifestyle can perhaps ameliorate some genetic difficulties but saying that all of those genes such as the one I mentioned can be reversed with lifestyle and enable the individual to function as efficiently as those not affected is quite a blanket statement. Considering that we do not understand the function of even a fraction of genes I can't see how we can effectively understand every epigenetic potential presently. And simply saying that epigenetics cures everything given the proper environment I think is a highly oversimplified statement. If I misinterpreted your statement please let me know.

I've met a lot of doctors who told me what I was suffering with was all in my head until I found a reason. I sort of feel like I am hearing the same bit, except this time I was just not living properly and so I developed problems. Sure, EMF surrounds us - but realistically we cannot get away from it all the time. Epigenetics is a new science. It's exciting to think that we can cure everything by living in accordance to nature but I think that is wishful thinking at best. I don't doubt it can potentially help - but when you literally are exhibiting every symptom of biotin deficiency and cannot summon any energy to get out of bed because of it I'm pretty sure natural measures are quite worthless at that point.

It always seems as if doctors think that real diseases don't exist or are quick to call out hypochondria because they themselves never had a serious health problem they did not create in their life. And of course it is this way because obviously you have to have a strong and healthy body to make it through med school. It weeds out the people not endowed with great genetics. So it makes sense that I can be expect to not be understood and can be blamed for my problems from a doctor who never had to struggle with anything other than self inflicted illness their entire life. I am not saying this is you - but this is more than several doctors I have had the displeasure of meeting. I think in medicine it's great to try and explain everything by a unified theory but we run the risk of basically mind \*\*\*\*ing people when we tell them, oh you just are not using your epigenetic potential and that is the reason your cerebral palsy is acting up. No one here is questioning the potential for these theories but I simply don't think they can be applied to the same extent in the case of everyone.

I do not see the contradiction unless one is looking for the idea that one's N=1=everyone can achieve the "same" N=1=Optimal.....N=1, implies just that, within the limits of one's body's physiology and the COE's. Yes everything operates under the same laws and every system tends to have unique limits. A "genetic" issue predisposes one to deficiencies in protein substrates and or misfolded proteins at the first and second folds. Depending upon the pathways involved, there may or may not be a way to bypass it and condition the system to adequately compensate. If one reads most of the literature on methylation and genetic issues, at best they take it to the molecular level with the assumption that all COE's are the same. There is little or no atomic or subatomic mechanistic analysis, and little or no COE's considered. I have never heard Dr. Kruse say "one size fits all" and in fact he stresses the need for a practitioner, extensive testing and consideration of genetic factors. Most people jump in in the middle without reading all of the blogs, the book and do not listen to all of the webinars where there are additional jewels about his own health and path. They assume he is recommending a one size fits all approach. This is haute cuisine as far as medical theory is concerned and without a trained palate, it is difficult to appreciate. Only through Dr. Kruse's in depth conceptual approach to explanation, have I personally been able to frame where "genetic" and "epigenetic" mechanisms might fit into my own health issues and those of my friends, family and clients. Furthermore, this is the only set of tools that begins to answer the questions regarding the relative value of a given intervention at a given time in a given case/person/N=1....Be aware, as far as I can tell, Dr. Kruse is aggressively refraining from practicing medicine on the internet and recommends that everyone seek professional help to work out their health issues. At the same time, he is recommending that they pay attention to the axiomatic truths that govern the physical universe we all live in and apply these to the recommendations they are given. It is not easy and is not a contradiction of a targeted therapy that remediates a genetic predispositon that has expressed since birth and or later due to COE's. Consider how can you evaluate the limit of the effectiveness of the intervention and the likelihood that it will continue to work indefinitely....?

### yewwei.tan — 2014-06-11T07:12:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fatigued-but-determined.10563/post-130500>

> [400lbbenchpress said:](/goto/post?id=130494)
>
> Does that mean genetic disease doesn't exist in tribal societies that live off fish and spend all day in the sunlight? I mean if you are going to make a statement saying that epigenetic potential can relieve any genetic defect I would like to know if it is an absolute one that correlates with reality or if simply epigenetics provides potential partial relief to genetic defects.

OSF3 to OSF5 go into this into great detail. (unfortunately, they are very long reads)

Basically, DNA (genetics) only affect the "repertoire" of proteins that you are able to express. But ultimately, **protein function (which is the most important thing) is tied to the protein's size, shape, and energy levels.** What are those dictated by? The Electromagnetic force and water chemistry.

Hence why Dr Kruse has always said that you can have the functional equivalent of Bad SNPs if your redox is poor, and why a SNP defect is not a death sentence. Of course, having bad SNPs makes things harder, in that your margin of safety is lower, but that doesn't mean you can't alter your existence through improved conditions of existence (at a cellular level).

It's almost as if "a qubit, is a qubit, is a qubit", and that so long as you have enough qubit carrying capacity in your brain, the entire thing can function optimally. The various different structures of the brain may just be there as either "legacy components" of evolution.

Or put differently, is is possible to build a brain that is made of nothing but the material found in the prefrontal cortex?

If almost all diseases are tied to brain energy levels, wouldn't that imply A LOT more adaptability and recover capacity than we currently think is possible?

CT is an example of something where Dr. Kruse says in many places to first make sure that several biomarkers are in a given range before trying it. Yet, many people seem to apply an absolute "this is good for me and more is better" approach that is not optimal in many situations from what I can tell by their posts. Your mildly inflammatory post implied that you felt he had said if you do A, then B will be the result. I have never read it that way. This may be my own error, but in my experience all healthcare whether on myself or with a "client" is a "practice" where I apply techniques and concepts in an ongoing experiment to try to improve or optimize function. So it ends up being more like "if this, practice, then maybe that. Travel at the speed of light is theoretically possible, but no one has achieved it yet. Also an E=mc2 concept....

It is where I get the questions that take me back to blogs, forum posts, PubMed and more to satisfy my curiosity and find functional models. Likewise with the hookworm inoculation that has, with the methylation support, profoundly changed my life over the last 15 months. I do not know whether they are more or less first aid or whether they are what I might consider "sustainable" over the rest of my life. # Thread: Vampires
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vampires.10564/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-16T19:48:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vampires.10564/post-121909>

Not the Transylvanians with the teeth.

[http://i.telegraph.co.uk/multimedia/archive/01773/Leslie25\_1773332i.jpg](http://i.telegraph.co.uk/multimedia/archive/01773/Leslie25_1773332i.jpg)

… but people who "steal energy".

Now @[Albert83BCN](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5984/) broached this subject indirectly recently in a thread where he says:

> "Could hate be a survival reaction when you sense that someone else is stealing electrons from you at a very quick rate?"

I stumbled on an article recently that talks about this. It was on a site that's sometimes mentioned by people in the paleosphere, although it isn't a paleo site. (Evan Brand, among others, does some of this guy's stuff.) It's a site belonging to a Dr. Wilson, and there's an enormous number of articles on it on everything from seaweed to water to toxic metals to energy. Some of it seems fairly eccentric and some pretty interesting. Anyway, he has an article entitled "Energetic vampirism_".
_
I have to say although I'd read and remembered that post, and indirectly sentence, of Albert's I'd perhaps missed the literalism in it. (I also haven't returned to Albert's thread to see what's new in it since his original post.) But this is essentially what this article is about.

I don't know how seriously to take it all. As often happens in the U.S. both religion and politics get dragged in. Thus Obamacare -- whatever that is: I only know very vaguely -- appears as a form of vampirism. We also get some fairly esoteric and unverifiable claims, such as this one:

> Several types of vampirism are common with sex. … One of the partners causes the chakras of the other person to spin backwards.When this occurs, the person always gives up some etheric energy to the other partner.Some people who are very sensitive can feel this ...

Notwithstanding, it is interesting to suspend disbelief for a while and see some of the situations he talks about in _energetic_ terms, something I wouldn't really have thought of doing.

Clearly, some people do set out to "upset" others. It wouldn't have occurred to me that they do it in order to cause energy to flow from the victim to themselves -- I wouldn't really have thought in terms of energy doing that. Maybe he's right, though.

"Upset" has the sense here of putting someone, as it were, off balance. So it could be done by threats or violence or shouting but also by flattery or seductive behaviour. Sometimes, he says, it's sexual (or quasi-sexual?). So a woman wearing provocative clothing might be trying to put men off balance in order to steal their energy; a man wolf-whistling in the street might be trying to put women off balance in order to steal their energy.

The "religious" angle comes in, because Wilson says that energy that is "stolen" is impure and actually not good for the "vampire". The energy that is pure is that which comes from the ground of being, i.e. God.

( A side note that occurred to me in the religious context:

See the story in Luke 8:43-47 (it's in all the synoptics). There there seems to be some kind of movement of energy, although there's nothing sinister about it:

[http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+8:43-47&version=KJV](http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+8%3A43-47&version=KJV) )
​

Anyway, the article may need to be taken with a pinch of salt -- at any rate by those who are sceptical of energetic explanations or religion or chakras or who like Obamacare or agree or disagree with any number of other things in it -- but, FWIW, here it is:

[http://drlwilson.com/articles/vampirism.htm](http://drlwilson.com/articles/vampirism.htm)

### fitness@home — 2014-04-16T20:10:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vampires.10564/post-121927>

You come up with the most interesting finds! Reading artilce now...

> Women often better vampires than men. According to Roy Masters and some other authorities on this subject, women vampirize others more than men.
>
> This is a rather complex subject, however, because this perspective may be due to a quality of women that is discussed in other articles on this website. It is that many women tend to sort of latch on to men, even their fathers and brothers if they are unmarried, and to take a certain amount of energy from them. Most women then process this energy and then give it back to the men in their lives. This is a type of give and take, and is not what is meant by the term ‘vampirism’ in this article. Instead, this is a type of energy exchange that is usually very beneficial.

> The energy field and energy vampirism. Some people can see or feel the electrical energy field around each person. Everyone has an energy field around their body because we are electrical and frequency beings. This is sometimes called the aura, the chakras, the tubes and other names.
>
> If one can see the energy field, energy vampires generally have little tentacles that extend from their bodies to the bodies of others, particularly those closest to them such as family members and “friends”. They also may have little suction cups on the ends of the tentacles that literally suck energy from others. This topic is illustrated in a book called Hands of Light and other books by Barbara Brennan and Joseph A. Smith.
>
> Meanwhile vampire victims often have an energy field that is full of holes and often a number of people have their “tentacles” in the holes, sucking energy from the person. These people are usually quite unhealthy.

I admit to being an energy vampire with my DH. I love it when he puts a warm hand on my back or shoulder when it is aching... I just absorb the energy.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-04-17T09:27:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vampires.10564/post-122085>

Hey, I'm glad I inspired you with my thoughts hehe

Quite interesting article, if I were to read it several years ago I guess I'd have said that it's bullshit... but as of today... being aware of how much I don't know... I consider almost anything is possible (and on the contrary, I also question everything that is presented as true). I think the article is spot on in the description of what happens, but not so much on the mechanisms that cause it. Specially as of recently and influenced by the blogposts from this site, I think that this has totally something to do with the laws of thermodynamics. It's funny that this trimester I got to study biochemistry and biology in my degree and it's helping me to understand a lot of the topics presented on this site. It has not been so long since I've been presented with the term Entropy but since I have been, I've had a profound feeling that everything has to do something with it. Marrying it with these new organization series and what we know from the quantum perspective since 2013, I think that most things surrounding this vampirism behaviour has to do with the fact that when an exergonic or endergonic reaction takes place, that energy must be taken into account within the system and outside it. But here's the fun. Every single small thing in this universe is a system in itself, that inflicts a change to the external system. That makes almost impossible to able to guess what events will take place for sure, but from the physics laws we know or at least think that whataver happens it has to be a reaction that tends to bring stabilization to this world.

The idea of entropy is that everything will try to become stable, so that the entropy (organization) becomes lower. This is tied with some weird ideas that I can barely understand saying that time must always flow forwards but not backwards. Once a jar breaks It will never spontaneously flow to a state where it reconstructs, because that reaction would require a high level of reorganization that is very unlikely to happen. That means that being in a high state of organization (for us also) is a state of high entropy that is maintained by some kind of energy (being chemical, mechanical or in this case... spiritual?). So when the entropy on us is higher (and I guess that certain situations in life like the ones the article present make it happen) we need to balance ourselves so we flow to a more stable state (peace of mind?) and that will require to take energy from other place, most probably from the people that surround us. That would be according to the laws of thermodynamics if I understood them correctly. I'm pretty sure I've just got a glance of how they really work. BTW, this brings some other weird questions... if one person is what we would say an empowering one... where does he get all the positive energy? At home, I observed an interesting thing, one that I don't like very much though. As I try to improve myself, be more positive, and explore how the world works, I can feel it has had a profound impact on me, a positive one. I tried to inspire both my parents. My dad, after being on quite a few troublesome things, has adopted some of the principles of the paleo diet, I tried to rewrite his mind in a more positive manner, and I think it kind of worked. He's more thoughtful now than he once was. In fact, he tended to be the kind of man that would start shouting out loud when something irritates him, which happened quite often. Not so much as of recently. But, on the other side, I do have the feeling that my mom is getting to worse position than ever before. A typical doc would say that the menopause has settled a different scenario for her, and well, it's almost certain that it might have it's influence, probably she has a lower redox potential than before, but considering the laws of thermodynamics and the fact that energy must be conserved... I'm worried that maybe both I and my dad getting more positive is making her loss energy to compensate for the equilibrium at home?

That makes me wonder: is it even possible to get electrons without stealing them between us? Well, I guess we have an answer in the latest blogposts. If we need electrons and we don't want to steal them from others, were might we look for them? Optimal food, the photoelectric effect from the sun and being in contact with nature, that would be the greatest source of electrons "for free". Well, for free? Nothing is for free, I guess that's why we are wasting the planet resources so fast in our era. Might it be that the more insane we become, the more resources from nature we need?

### caroline — 2014-04-17T11:00:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vampires.10564/post-122092>

Jeremy told us in the Q&A with Dr. K. a bunch of stuff about this very topic. When there is 2 CF kids in a family - one will live and one will die......the healthier one will survive because they will steal electrons from the weaker one.

Jeremy said when he is in a group of people with CF - like recently they did a fund raiser and wanted to get a group pic........ Jeremy searched the group for the strongest to stand beside. He does this on intuition and it makes him feel bad - but he is protecting himself.

He also told us that they used to send CF kids to a camp in the summer ..... and they stopped doing it - you can guess why.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-04-17T12:42:59+0300

Now that you talk about vampires, I guess you all know about [Elizabeth Báthory](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elizabeth_B%C3%A1thory), who killed young girls ir order to bath in their blood to preserve her youth? Massive electron stealth syndrome?

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-18T10:35:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vampires.10564/post-122332>

> [Albert83BCN said:](/goto/post?id=122085)
>
> I think the article is spot on in the description of what happens, but not so much on the mechanisms that cause it.

This is an important point in general, isn't it? To turn it round the other way, sometimes the explanations people give you for phenomena are unconvincing, but it doesn't follow that the phenomena don't occur.

I like your conclusion where you suggest it doesn't have to be a zero-sum game. I have to think we don't live in the kind of universe where it is. To put this discussion in a larger context, one of the contemporary problems with our world might be that people do see life as a zero-sum game.

It seems to me that [fitness@home](mailto:fitness@home) (post #2 in this thread) is _not_ a vampire, although she jokingly says she is, because her husband _gives_ her the energy. It's not obtained through force or fraud. Of course, that kind of distinction seems to belong to the _Lebenswelt_ not the scientific frame of reference.
