I've just got round to reading Clint Ober's book. I had some idea of what it was about, but actually reading him leisurely setting it out in order I found I had a much stronger sense of what he was talking about and how critical it was.

He mentions a Frenchman near the beginning of the book who was saying some of this stuff, albeit without the technical understanding Ober _et al_ have now, but in a broad ecological context quite some while ago.

I also found it interesting how fragments from Ober's past began to make sense to him taken retrospectively. For example, he says at school he had Indian (Native American) friends, and he recalled a boy's mother saying to him, "Now you're home take your shoes off, or you'll get ill."

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-16T19:58:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-trials-of-the-cherokee-were-reflected-in-their-skulls.10565/post-121914>

> Researchers from North Carolina State University and the University of Tennessee have found that environmental stressors – from the Trail of Tears to the Civil War – led to significant changes in the shape of skulls in the eastern and western bands of the Cherokee people. The findings highlight the role of environmental factors in shaping our physical characteristics.
>
> “We wanted to look at these historically important events and further our understanding of the tangible human impacts they had on the Cherokee people,” says Dr. Ann Ross, a professor of anthropology at NC State and co-author of a paper describing the work. “This work also adds to the body of literature on environmental effects on skull growth.” ...

[http://news.ncsu.edu/releases/wms-ross-cherokee2014/](http://news.ncsu.edu/releases/wms-ross-cherokee2014/)

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sooperb — 2014-04-16T22:41:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-121969>

I was interested to find out more about these and came across this website. I've no idea about the doctor whose site it is or how correct she is but it made a good read

[http://drmyhill.co.uk/wiki/Detoxing\_-\_Far\_Infrared\_Sauna\_(FIRS)](http://drmyhill.co.uk/wiki/Detoxing_-_Far_Infrared_Sauna_\(FIRS\))

I'd be happy for opinions, does an IR sauna seem like a positive option?

### Sue-UK — 2014-04-17T13:52:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-122118>

I've used one before Soo, last time a couple of years ago. I was going to do a few sessions in March, but didn't because my FIL was ill (still is). This time I was going to do it to improve redox potential rather than detox, so wasn't going to go very hot with it. I felt the time was right because I'd been drinking lots of well water, and the weather was so horrible using the sun wasn't an option. When I found myself under a lot of stress I thought if I was dehydrated the FIR was a bad idea.... Now the sun's shining the water, grounding and sunshine is the better option for me, I'll probably rethink the FIR from October onwards.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-17T14:01:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-122119>

Jack says in one podcast -- I think one he did with Evan Brand -- that he has infra-red bulbs built into his bathtub. I'm not sure how he uses them, though. One use seemed to be to get a heat gradient across his body -- heat it in one place and cool it with ice in another. But perhaps that's not the only thing he does with them.

What I hear is that the _far_ infra-red saunas, the ones they often have in spas, may be bad news. Far infra-red is getting nearer the wrong place on the electromagnetic spectrum. Apparently, you can get a pretty hefty dose of harmful EMF radiation in one of those. They have large panels and if you're leaning on a panel, it's going right in your head.

The bulbs are _near_ infra-red -- in fact, they go over into the visible spectrum and you can see red light from them. They use those bulbs for poultry houses and reptile tanks, which is why the bulbs are commercially available.

### cantweight — 2014-04-17T17:28:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-122162>

I just started with FIR sauna use, my BUN/Creatinine is decent around 13. Initially I was feeling great using it, but have stopped recently due to really bad edema. After reading that article (thank Sue!) I realize I was staying in too long.

And the whole rinsing immediately after did not always happen. I let myself get a good sweat on a few times which I knew I shouldnt do from a previous thread...but it felt good...I succumbed...

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/mitochondria-produce-water-hydration-by-exercise.9543/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/mitochondria-produce-water-hydration-by-exercise.9543/)

### sooperb — 2014-04-17T17:52:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-122175>

Shame, they look quite attractive.

### Justfourme — 2014-04-18T04:30:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-122295>

I have a FAR infrared sauna and use daily. Jack seems to say they are ok for most things. Brand does matter. My integrative dr researched and narrowed down to 2 brands. She has one at her office you can purchase packages/minutes of use. I tried the beginner package and decided to buy my own unit. As far as effectiveness, prob need testing to determine heavy metal and toxins, then re-test to see changes. I have noticed a few changes. Toxins are excreted in sweat, urine and poop. Let's just say the restroom smelled like a landfill for several days! I had a bug bite on my arm that got infected and has been scaly/not healing , turned to some type of fungal infection for months. In 2 weeks time it was 90% healed. All skin seems to be feeling softer and looking better. With all the toxins in everything these days, if it helps to prevent disease it will be well worth it.
I had also been experiencing chronic shoulder pain for over a year - too much typing for extended periods. That too is 90% gone.
If nothing else, it's a quiet 30 min of meditation!
I bought from high tech health as it was the lowest in emf. Sunlighten is a close second. And no, I have no affiliation w either company. Beware of any made of cedar wood - unless you like adding another toxin to the mix.
and no higher than 130 degrees F, and no longer than 30 min. High temps and longer exposure should not be done. And you need to gradually work up to these over several sessions.

### Justfourme — 2014-04-18T04:42:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-122297>

You also need to up mineral supplements while using or you will not feel well. Mag, vit c, sea salt, potassium, etc. I also drink prob 18 to 20 oz of water while in the sauna.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-20T12:41:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-122660>

There's a podcast specifically on these from Wendy Myers. Wendy seems to have connections with Evan Brand, who I'm sure you know. They're using these in accordance with Dr. Lawrence Wilson's protocol (crops here in the "Vampire" thread).

[http://www.liveto110.com/infrared-saunas-in-depth-with-nikki-moses/](http://www.liveto110.com/infrared-saunas-in-depth-with-nikki-moses/)

I have heard -- where I can't remember: possibly on this podcast -- that one study looked at the sweat of people leaving saunas (whether Finnish or Infra-red I can't remember) and found mercury in the sweat. This certainly suggests that saunas can help shift some substances out of the body.

The more hard-nosed people in Paleo would probably scoff at the notion of "toxins" and "detoxing". I've heard Rob Wolf do so on a podcast. To be sure people who talk vaguely of "detoxing" probably don't really know what they mean and the methods they propound don't necessarily work anyway. But these are real physiological processes. And maybe some of the methods Wendy uses do help our bodies' elimination systems.

I haven't tried infra-red saunas, but they do sound interesting. So many cultures use sweat-baths that I have to think there has to be _something_ in it for some purposes at any rate. (See Native American sweat lodges, for example.) They seem to have been almost the original form of bath. What seems to have driven them out in the modern world is, curiously enough, the spread of Christianity. The early Christians could be highly ascetic, and some even looked upon keeping onself clean as a form of luxury. There's no Biblical basis for this attitude at all, of course -- no more than for the New England Puritan view that was supposed to hold that "cleanliness is next to godliness" and had missionaries persuading Eskimos to use towels much to Stefansson's annoyance, since the towels spread eye-diseases from person to person -- but that's what they thought. Notice that the sauna comes from Finland, which was, as it were, on the fringes of the world and evangelised very late.

Anyway, if sweat lodges were good enough for the Apache, they're probably good enough for us -- though I'd be just as interested in any data that showed they were not.

### RobH — 2014-04-26T03:22:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-123472>

Bought a SunLighten (got a great deal, got it up and running a few days ago.) Supposedly very low EMF per them (and I will test). Jack said in one podcast of the OR series (with Dr. Patel) that he thought they were great as long as one didn't suffer from chronic dehydration (and the sauna was low EMF). I am thinking anything I can do to add energy to my system will be helpful since energy loss is a huge issue. Naturally the folks who sell them cite host of benefits (detoxification, skin health, muscle tone, decreased BP, increased healing, weight loss, cardio tone, improve sleepetc.) Ours also has "chromotherapy" with changeable light (set to red) for additional anti-inflammatory effects.

My wife tried it the other night before bed. Said it stopped her late night carb cravings and she slept great. Turned off breakers too that night though.

So far have done 3 sessions in it < 125F. About 45 min each. Very nice. Really enjoyed it. Very relaxing. Have a few minor wounds / scrapes that seem to be healing faster than expected. Perhaps it's my imagination but my skin feels softer. Have been drinking lots of cold vortexed and magnetized RO water before and during.... goal is to store a whole bunch of extra energy in EZ of water!

I worry a bit about the FIR as postulated above by gagnrad but actually there are far worse sources of EMF I deal with every day. Ours is a "full-spectrum" model so theoretically I can customize the output and specify how much near / mid / far EMF to emit.

Very very happy so far.

Ask me in a month or so and I'll let you know...

RobH

Perhaps it's my imagination but my skin feels softer. Have been drinking lots of cold vortexed and magnetized RO water before and during.... goal is to store a whole bunch of extra energy in EZ of water!
>
> I worry a bit about the FIR as postulated above by gagnrad but actually there are far worse sources of EMF I deal with every day. Ours is a "full-spectrum" model so theoretically I can customize the output and specify how much near / mid / far EMF to emit.
>
> Very very happy so far.
>
> Ask me in a month or so and I'll let you know...
>
> RobH

Hopefully you got one of the larger models with the removable benches. I find being able to stretch while inside to be almost as beneficial as the IR energy. I got mine a few months before you and use it daily.

### RobH — 2014-04-26T05:28:00+0300

Prices on the saunas aren't cheap and if you want something for just the IR then one of the solo dome type models would be an excellent choice especially if space is an issue. Just make sure you find one with ultra low emf otherwise you might as well crawl in a microwave oven.

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=123508)
>
> How close are you to the actual lights in the sauna? I was told by my doctor that the light doesn't penetrate the body unless you are within six inches or so of the actual bulb….he's not always a fountain of truth, but I thought I would ask how the saunas are set up. It would seem that the bigger one's may be less effective if this is true.

They aren't lights but rather a carbon based thin panel that emits radiation in the IR spectrum. To get full IR impact you should be very close to them, but mine has a total of 14 heaters including the floor so not too hard to get close. For stretching the heat is all that matters. I got mine after reading Pollack's book and listening to Jack and was amazed at the energy (electrons) it gave me. If you live in a northern climate, I'm in Canada, it really shows you how much we miss the sun in the winter.

# Thread: The Obesity Paradox?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-obesity-paradox.10570/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TribalSpice — 2014-04-17T02:35:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-obesity-paradox.10570/post-122025>

[http://www.abc.net.au/health/featur...hAndWellbeing|WhatIsTheObesityParadox\_FBP|abc](http://www.abc.net.au/health/features/stories/2014/04/14/3984799.htm?WT.mc_id=Innovation_Innovation-HealthAndWellbeing|WhatIsTheObesityParadox_FBP|abc)

> Being overweight or obese is known to increase your risk of a range of serious diseases. Yet researchers are finding you're more likely to survive a chronic illness if you carry extra weight.
> { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }
>
>
> \[Image source: iStockPhoto | jsheets19\]
> It's no secret Australians are getting heavier. More than [60 per cent of us are overweight or obese](http://www.aihw.gov.au/diabetes-indicators/overweight-and-obesity), and OECD data suggests when it comes to our weight we're the [fourth biggest nation in the world](http://www.oecd.org/els/health-systems/Health-at-a-Glance-2013.pdf)
> Being overweight or obese is known to increase your chances of developing serious illnesses such as [type 2 diabetes](http://www.abc.net.au/health/library/stories/2002/12/19/1829403.htm#d), [heart disease](http://www.abc.net.au/health/library/stories/2003/02/27/1831271.htm) and cancer, and maintaining a healthy BMI (Body Mass Index) is one of the best ways to prevent these health issues later in life.
> But a growing body of research suggests some people who are classed as "obese" have a better chance of surviving the chronic diseases that obesity causes in the first place. This phenomenon has been labelled the "obesity paradox" and it has researchers puzzled.
> **Breaking down the BMI**
> The BMI scale is used worldwide and is considered the best way to measure a person's body fat in relation to their height.
> It's easy to determine by plugging your [height and weight into an online calculator](http://www.abc.net.au/health/quizzestools/tools/2008/01/17/2114338.htm#bmi) and it will quickly let you know if you're carrying too much fat.
> CategoryBMI Range
> Very severely underweightLess than 15
> Severely underweight15-16
> Underweight16-18.5
> Normal (healthy weight)18.5-25
> Overweight25-30
> Obese class one (moderately)30-35
> Obese class two (severely)35-40
> Obese class three (very severely)Over 40
> But many experts, including Professor John Dixon, head of clinical obesity research at Baker IDI Heart and Diabetes Institute, warn BMI isn't the most accurate way of telling if someone is a healthy weight, because it will change depending on the person, their age and health issues.
> "Optimal weight for health, longevity and mortality isn't constant – and why should it be for all adults of any age and with any disease?" he says.
> **When bigger is better**
> The evidence for the obesity paradox has found those who are overweight or obese are more likely to survive renal failure, heart failure, diabetes and coronary heart disease, regardless of their age.
> Furthermore, one recent US study found being overweight or class-one obese can actually [protect people from _all causes_ of death](http://jama.jamanetwork.com/article.aspx?articleid=1555137).
> But despite all the recent research into the obesity paradox, experts still don't really know why it occurs.
> However, Dixon says, "it certainly is a very real thing". He argues it's easier to understand how older people in particular are protected by carrying extra weight, especially those who suffer serious wasting diseases.
> "It's been found that in people with all forms of cancer, the bigger they are, the better they do in treatment, because they have greater reserves of muscle and fat at a time when they are under stress," he explains.
> For example, a new review by Deakin University, which looked at research carried out on 20,000 people over the age of 65, found [those with a BMI between 23 and 33 actually live longer](http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/early/2014/01/22/ajcn.113.068122.abstract) than people with a BMI in the lower end of the normal weight category (between 18.5 and 23).
> But what is harder to understand is why young people with lower BMIs have a smaller chance of surviving a sudden serious illness than those of a similar age who are obese, explains Joseph Proietto, professor of medicine at the University of Melbourne.
> "Let's say there's someone with a BMI of 19 who's fit and healthy and someone with a BMI of 34," he says.
> "Each has a heart attack because they've inherited high cholesterol. Who'll survive better? The evidence suggests the person with the BMI of 34 has a better chance."
> Proietto says it could be because people who are obese are often already being treated for conditions such as high blood pressure and cholesterol.
> People whose weight is not an issue on the other hand, probably aren't even aware that they have some of these health problems, giving them a disadvantage if they suddenly fall ill.
> **Cleaning up the confusion**
> The obesity paradox seems a huge contradiction to everything we've been told about maintaining a healthy weight. So it begs the question: "why bother?" when it comes to diet and exercise.
> It also doesn't help that researchers can't explain why obese people seem to fare better in illness. Until more research is done, they're really working on theories.
> But Dixon stresses the obesity paradox is definitely not an invitation to throw healthy eating and exercise habits out the window. Preventing these diseases should always be your priority and the best way to do that is by staying lean when you're young.
> "People under the age of 40 who have no illness should really be trying to keep their weight down, because there's no doubt that overweight and obesity is associated with disease: diabetes, heart disease, heart failure," he says.
> Up until middle age you should attempt to stay within a BMI of 20-25 and lead a healthy, active lifestyle, Proietto says.
> "But as you get older, you shouldn't fuss too much if you gain a little weight, because it's when you're older that you're going to have a stroke or heart attack or surgery," he says.
> "However, this doesn't apply if you're massively obese. Morbid obesity shortens your life, without a doubt."
> Once you reach middle age, he recommends focusing on keeping fit and eating highly nutritious foods, rather than losing weight.
> "If you stay fit, you're at least minimising your risk of having cardiovascular problems," he explains.
> But if you suffer from, say, diabetes and you're over 50, you're much better off asking yourself, "What's the ideal weight for me?" and seeing your doctor for advice.
> "We're not suggesting severely obese people shouldn't try to improve their diet and lifestyle, and try to lose weight," Dixon says.
> "If they can take off just three per cent of body weight, that has measurable benefits on their health.
> "But we can't ever expect people over the age of 60, for instance, to try to achieve a 'normal weight' range, which may not be good for healthy ageing.
> "I think the real concept here is that the normal weight range isn't necessarily the healthy weight range for a given individual."

# Thread: Tim Noakes Interview
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tim-noakes-interview.10575/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-17T11:58:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tim-noakes-interview.10575/post-122108>

Boy is Dr. Noakes in good form in this one:

[http://vinnietortorich.com/2014/04/angriest-trainer-256-dr-timothy-noakes/](http://vinnietortorich.com/2014/04/angriest-trainer-256-dr-timothy-noakes/)

I haven't heard it all yet. I'm still listening to it, but what I've heard so far is very good.

Among other things I like his very well-informed explanation of the history behind where we are now. He agrees that Ancel Keys is a key figure. What is interesting is that Dr. Noakes says that an Irish reseracher now living in South Africa found out that Keys had wanted to at least partially retract what he'd said later in his life but found he couldn't. Keys said he was blocked by industry, who'd found that the high-carb/low-fat paradigm was just too profitable.

This, of course, isn't a "conspiracy theory". They never devised the paradigm. People aren't as devilishly clever and far-seeing as that. What humans are smart enough and organised enough to do is to take advantage of situations when they see them. Unfortunately, people's self-interest -- or here group-interest -- is not always in the general interest (the fable of the bees nothwithstanding), and probably not even good for them in the long run. People think short-term, though, don't they?

Dr. Noakes is also good on the corruption of the medical profession by the pharmaceutical industry. He says that back when he trained doctors wanted to know what _caused_ problems; they didn't just throw a drug at a problem and push you out of the surgery (or "office" as they say in the U.S.). I think there's a kind of attitude in the "alternative" health world, and even in the paleosphere, where people will sneer on the grounds of not looking for causes at "Western medicine" -- as if anything "Eastern" is automatically superior! I think anyone who even knows a smidgin of the history of medicine will find this rather silly. If you're Chinese and your kid's face gets half burnt off, you don't put your trust in "traditional medicine"; you try to get her to the West (an actual case I know of). Modern medicine has its weaknesses, but it has also been corrupted by the pharmaceutical companies.

Anyway, Noakes is very good here on lots of things. It would be a good podcast to put in the way of anyone who still believes in the high-carb paradigm, since he explains things very well.

Oh, nearly forgot. Also on Keys. Dr. Noakes says that when Keys first trotted his theory out in a committee meeting of scientists, they were derisive and he was "humiliated". There's another moral to the story. Ancel Keys's pride was obviously hurt, and as a consequence he set himself to make his views dominant. Sometimes it's "interest" that is the problem; but often it's pride. Keys may have wished to retract later, but by then he'd done the damage.

> The evil that men do lives after them; the good is oft interred with their bones.

### sooperb — 2014-04-17T18:49:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tim-noakes-interview.10575/post-122185>

You could almost add "and the rest, as they say, is history". A useful illustration of the benefits/profits of commerce outweighing the good of the populace, there's nothing altruistic about big business is there!

I remember reading somewhere that the opponents of margarine endured some pretty awful heavy handed and threatening pressure when they started delivering lectures against it. In the end they gave up because they felt it wasn't worth the risk to either themselves or their futures.

### Gracelind — 2014-04-18T18:27:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tim-noakes-interview.10575/post-122388>

Re: margarine My dad used to talk about how the original margarine was an unappetizing gray. The butter industry prevented the margarine from being artificially colored at the plant. But it came with a packet of coloring that could be stirred in to make it yellow.

_By 1902, 32 states had passed margarine coloring bans. Butter is yellow; margarine, which at the time was produced with animal fat, was gray. Dairy producers opposed coloring margarine yellow. So margarine took on lots of colors, and, in some instances, margarine was sold with a home coloring kit._ [http://blogs.ancestry.com/cm/2013/03/11/a-margarine-of-error/](http://blogs.ancestry.com/cm/2013/03/11/a-margarine-of-error/)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: GM Crop to Produce Fish Oil
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gm-crop-to-produce-fish-oil.10576/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### HoneyChild — 2014-04-17T14:40:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gm-crop-to-produce-fish-oil.10576/post-122126>

When I first read this I was a bit confused as to how plants could be modified to produce "fish oil" but then I realised that they are actually producing "oil for fish." They want to grow these plants so that they can be fed to farmed fish as currently "80% of the fish oil harvested from the oceans is fed to fish grown in farms."

[http://www.theguardian.com/environm...ified-plants-heart-disease-fish-oils-go-ahead](http://www.theguardian.com/environment/2014/apr/17/trial-gm-genetically-modified-plants-heart-disease-fish-oils-go-ahead)

### nonchalant — 2014-04-17T16:05:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gm-crop-to-produce-fish-oil.10576/post-122146>

> The particular oils that benefit the health of fish and humans, called EPA and DHA, are not in fact produced by fish themselves but instead accumulated by eating marine microbes. Napier's team took up to seven genes from algae that produce the fish oils and [transplanted them into oil seed plants called camelina](http://www.rothamsted.ac.uk/news/crop-plants-green-factories-fish-oils). It naturally produces short-chain oils and has been grown as a food crop for centuries in southern and eastern Europe and is used a biofuel crop in North America.

### nicld — 2014-04-17T15:58:28+0300

# Thread: Do calories matter when you eat epi-paleo and you're trying to lose weight?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 46
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Curves — 2014-04-17T20:27:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122196>

Hey Everyone! I'm happy to be checking back in to my favorite online community.

Here's the question I'm working on now . . . after putting on weight last fall (I'm convinced my body _knows_ it's Fall and packs on the weight no matter what I do), how do I take if off? I'm feeling great mentally, but chunky physically.
**
Do calories matter when you're eating epi-paleo and trying to lose weight?**

### Curves — 2014-04-17T20:47:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122200>

> [spinergie said:](/goto/post?id=122198)
>
> Hi Curves how are you good to see you back, how about carrying on or starting a journal?
> Also on the front page of the website is a guide "to get you started" on reset and the other protocols.
> From a lay person point of view my experience is to listen to your body when planning foods.
> Eating a largish breakfast can I found make a difference to what you eat for the rest of the day.
> When I have done that I have personally found hunger subsides and then com evening, I actually realise I'm not hungry and that sometimes eating is just a habit we had.
> Light dinners. Meat and fish broths. Leaving 4 hours before last dinner and bedtime to be able to let hormonal function access parasympathetic NS. Staying well away from non native EMF.
> Giving yourself time and believing in your goal.
> I don't personally count calories but I watch carbs, every source of carbs, including vegetables. Tables can be found online. Sometimes you think your doing a keto diet but your eating more carbs than you need and insulin levels don't subside consistently.
> I hope this helps and I hope I haven't made any major errors in detailing this

Thanks Spinergie! I've been eating epi-paleo for about two years now and have noticed a dramatic change in all areas of my life. We've also recently moved 650 miles to get out of the super high emf zone where we were living. Now, two years later and feeling super healthy, I'm turning to the weight. I haven't counted calories for two years and have focused on number of carbs, but now I'm wondering -- should calories be completely cut from the equation? It's an open-ended question that I haven't answered for myself yet. I have about 10 pounds of weight concentrated in the middle that I'm tired of and just need to go!

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-17T21:15:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122205>

Calories do not matter......electrons do......so if your gaining weight it is because you gut flora has simplified and is less diverse.......why? They use protons and leave electrons for you. Anything that has more protons in compared to electrons gets larger and more inefficient with respect to energy.

Since that time, I've worked on diversifying my gut flora and no longer have GERD. I haven't gained more weight; just haven't lost what I gained last fall. Suggestions? Time to add HIIT? Continue to diversify my gut flora, ground and sit in the sun to gain electrons? Be patient?

### sjoshua — 2014-04-17T22:17:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122221>

> [Curves said:](/goto/post?id=122214)
>
> Good advice. I'm going to try it.
> Just to be clear, calorie restriction differs from IFing in that you are not restricting your calories during non-fasting times when IFing?

True statement. The goal with IF is to reduce your eating window (i.e. time in a fed state), not your consumption.

But, stop talking about calories as they are not relevant, only the energy they carry with them is.

Regarding cocoa, I don't know if there is a specific place Jack dove into the details, but MDA just posted about dark chocolate (cocoa being the most beneficial component) here: [http://www.marksdailyapple.com/4-foods-that-have-surprising-medicinal-benefits/](http://www.marksdailyapple.com/4-foods-that-have-surprising-medicinal-benefits/)

Regarding resveratrol, its function is raising [adiponectin](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adiponectin) which is used by your body to regulate glucose/fatty acid utilization.

EDIT: K2 blog: [http://jackkruse.com/osteoporosis-two-the-vitamin-k2-story/](http://jackkruse.com/osteoporosis-two-the-vitamin-k2-story/) (quick and dirty is, K2 works in concert with D3 to allow better utilization)

### sjoshua — 2014-04-17T22:49:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122233>

Yes, I IF daily and have for almost 3 years (1 evening meal/day, no other calories). But my life is not yours, so my strategy isn't yours either

The best approach I'd recommend would be to phase it in gently, maybe 30-60 minutes/week until you have found what your body handles best.
i.e. assuming you decide to adjust by 30 minutes/week and want to condense your eating window towards the evening:
if you currently eat Bfast/Lunch/Din at 8a/12p/6p, for the first week push the breakfast to 8:30a instead and keep your other meals the same. The next week, Bfast at 9a, the third week 9:30a, and so on until the Bfast and lunch meals essentially overlap, at which point you could phase one of them out and re-distribute total daily consumption across the 2 meals instead of the 3.

You could also do this the opposite way, by moving your eating window towards the morning by having dinner 30 mins earlier/week if that is your preference. Some people are morning eaters and some are not, so listen to your body for this.

FYI most people tend to lean towards a longer eating window than I, with 2 meals spread across 6-10 hours of feeding time. You may achieve that range, less or more, but the most important aspect is to track your progress and assure you are not doing things that your body doesn't agree with.

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-18T16:18:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122365>

adding my 2 cents here...

I haven't been able to do BAB for over a year now. Every time I try - I either don't finish breakfast or I "Bonk" with a huge postpirandial blood glucose spike. When I looked back at my journal (I write by hand) I found that I do much better on the Post Leptin Rx. w/in a week of adding seasonal carbs back in suddenly my nails are back to growing 1/4-1/2 " /week!

I also will do a coconut milk/hwc shake with raw nuts, berries and a touch of honey. I have less postpirandial blood glucose spikes when I do this. This morning I had a crab cake and shredded sweet potatoes & BPC (I was going for 2 crab cakes but couldn't even consider the 2nd one!) lunch will be a salad with oysters and goat cheese and maybe some fruit on the side.

I've discovered I stall when I skip meals (intentionally or un-intentionally) If I'm going to IF - I push dinner to before 6pm (or make it a very light meal) and wait to have my BPC until 9-10 am the next morning with my first meal around 11am. I usually do this sort of IF on Saturday or Sunday. once a week. other than that.

>
> I wish I could do raw! Maybe in my next life time! However I have come to love cerviche thanks to this site....
> @[cantweight](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1283/), I'm curious, what blood type are you, do you know?

Im AB+ Ive wondered how that is playing into my situation. I have The blood type diet book....found it fascinating. What is your take on on that?

### cmegan — 2014-04-18T17:12:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122373>

I'm a type A also...I am well aware that people on this site do not subscribe to D'Adamo's theories and I can totally understand why. I mean, if I ate all of the grains, beans, and soy that he prescribes for Type A I'd be dead right now! I've dabbled in his work since the 90's when his first book came out and I had an office visit with him several years ago after his book on Genotypes was written. The results from our visit was really fascinating. I do a combination now of epi-paleo and Blood Type A diet, meaning that I eat the proteins and veggies that are blood type friendly for me...low and behold they are the proteins that I have been naturally gravitating toward since childhood (like lamb, codfish, scallops, chicken, and turkey). I don't do well with beef or pork and of course dairy but can have ghee. I have worked over the years on the field of behavioral cardiology (I am a health psychologist with a speciality in cardiology) so most of our patients have metabolic syndrome. Nutrition and dietary behaviors are a large part of my practice and it never ceases to amaze me, but the patients who tell me they "don't like red meat" and eat mostly "poultry and white fish" (or who gravitate more towards vegetarianism) are ALWAYS Type A's! It just blows me away! I've been cognizant of this for about 10 years now. If I had my druthers I'd be eating mostly poultry, nuts, berries, and the occasional lamb chop, but I do believe in epi-paleo so I do the a combo for myself and it has worked out really well! I would love nothing more than to wake up every day and have a huge bowl of berries and nuts but I know that it would not be good for me. It has been quite challenging during the leptin reset to down eggs and lamb burgers in the morning but of course it was so beneficial! I agree with Dr. Kruse that poultry is not the best source of protein but god I just naturally crave it! I try to stick with lamb and my blood friendly fish and seafood. I found that as time went on and I lost the weight I had to add back in more veggies, I wasn't feeling so well on low-carb for so long. I wish I was a Type O! Lol! The other interesting component of his work is his prescription for exercise and lifestyle according to blood type...Type A tend to get stressed more easily and have cortisol issues, so gentle activities like hiking, pilates, and yoga are best...these are the activities I've always gravitated towards. Several years ago I tried HIIT training and did not do so well...Anyway, when Cantweight mentioned her dietary issues I was just wondering if she was an A...

Several years ago I tried HIIT training and did not do so well...Anyway, when Cantweight mentioned her dietary issues I was just wondering if she was an A...

Interesting! I've never been a fan of chicken but ate it for years b/c of how good it was supposed to be. If I do eat chicken these days I prefer the dark meat - but there's something about it that's never settled well with me. But Turkey, Duck - I'm all in! As of Lamb - its my favorite Red meat, followed by venison, elk, and wild boar (when I lived in Guam once a month we'd go out and get a wild boar and cook it! total awesomeness.) I do love a good steak and of course bacon.

DH makes really good steak tartar - and we'll use our local grassfeed beef to make it occasionally. There are days when I'm making hamburgers from that meat - that I could just eat it all up raw...

I love to go hiking but finding good places to do it are a bit of a challenge some times around DC. I told DH we're going camping for memorial day weekend out in the Shenandoah - and maybe even go for a day hike with the kids along the Appalachia trail! I do like yoga - but I haven't found a decent yoga studio here in the DC metro area that does yoga the way I prefer! I do really well when I HIIT 2-3x/week w/ body weight movements. As well as Lifting Really heavy. Long Cardio is part of what brought me to where I'm at today.... Spending 6hrs/day in pool swimming isn't the best thing! Swimming needs to take a cue from what Dara Torres did when she came back several years ago in her 40s! There is a part of me that really want's to go out running this year... but instead I'm going to focus on walking...

I find the same thing. If I eat fish for breakfast, I find that it just does keep me satiated as long. Got home late so just had some homemade lemonade for "supper".

### sjoshua — 2014-04-18T19:27:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122400>

I've also heard of people just mixing 100% cocoa powder into hot water as a fo-"hot chocolate"; sans sugar. Haven't tried it myself though, I'm a cold beverage person

So, my mom was all about the blood type diet template back when it first hit so I've researched it a bit in my past and could never really find scientific support for the premise. Unsure if you guys were aware of this study published earlier this year, but it caught my eye due to this: [http://www.plosone.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pone.0084749](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0084749) Summary: no correlation between successful diet practices and individual blood type was detected for the participants.

> [cmegan said:](/goto/post?id=122291)

### Danco3636 — 2014-04-18T20:32:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122411>

When I am in a fasted state I am very entergitic and have this need to do something. Hard for me to sit still.
I love and almost always do my workouts (fun-in) and movement in a fasted state. I even prefer my CT in a fasted state usually in the morning, when I wake up.
Wake up, walk outside barefoot, minimal to no cloths then some CT with post green tea and mobility work afterwards. - Kick starts the day!

When I eat and mostly around my last meal 2-4 hours before bed, I start to get tired and feel real relaxed.

Before food engerized me.
Now fasting, being outside barefoot in the sun or after CT does.

### Curves — 2014-04-18T20:57:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122415>

I had a big sugar indulgence this last week. I had one of those “I’m going on a 'diet' so let me eat the foods I don’t normally eat‘ mindset. Anyone else been there? What a huge mistake. Today, I feel depressed, lack confidence and have a cold. I know it will pass but I realize I can never fall back into my old ways of thinking -- period.

Having said that, the Leptin Reset saved my life 2+ years ago, and now I’m ready for the next phase.

In order to help me figure it out my next steps, I’ve actually copied all your responses in a document for reference – so thank you all so much!

Here’s what I’m pondering right now:

· Something about IFing intrinsically scares me; I’m not quite sure if I don’t have the redox yet to support it or because I’m a woman but it doesn't yet feel right. I do note however, that when I ate a FULL BAB (not just bacon and eggs), I did it naturally. And now that I'm writing this, I can see that I feel comfortable letting it evolve naturally and slowly which is what you said @[sjoshua](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5781/). That was good advice.

· My does body loves some coffee in the am; I love the hyper focus it provides. I’m considering experimenting with BPC ala @Audrey and @[cantweight](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1283/). I do know that coconut oil in my coffee makes me sick to my stomach so I was thinking of trying some MCT or butter. Based on Joshua’s and Audrey’s cocoa comments, I’ll try adding cocoa along with some vanilla and nuts.

· Definitely return to strict spi-paleo plus seasonal carbs. My body definitely likes it’s veggies.

What I'm confused about though is -- does BPC constitute a meal? Are you technically IFing with a BPC in the am?

### Tanya — 2014-04-18T21:06:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122416>

Thank you, Joshua - it was helpful The recipe there calls for a couple of tablespoons of cocoa. I've been adding 1 teaspoon only to my tea. At least, now I know how much to aim for.....

### sjoshua — 2014-04-18T21:31:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122418>

> [Danco3636 said:](/goto/post?id=122411)
>
> When I am in a fasted state I am very entergitic and have this need to do something. Hard for me to sit still.
> I love and almost always do my workouts (fun-in) and movement in a fasted state. I even prefer my CT in a fasted state usually in the morning, when I wake up.
> Wake up, walk outside barefoot, minimal to no cloths then some CT with post green tea and mobility work afterwards. - Kick starts the day!
>
> When I eat and mostly around my last meal 2-4 hours before bed, I start to get tired and feel real relaxed.
>
> Before food engerized me.
> Now fasting, being outside barefoot in the sun or after CT does.

Cool, my experience matches this, especially regarding the fed/fasted "energized" feelings pre/post IFing. Now I like doing all my daily stuffs prior to eating my meal, and to me eating indicates my day is winding down and I no longer strive to be anything other than present/in the moment - i.e. move away from thinking on the day's stresses and wind down for sleep.

> [Curves said:](/goto/post?id=122415)
>
> What I'm confused about though is -- does BPC constitute a meal? Are you technically IFing with a BPC in the am?

My understanding is, since the BPC is only delivering fat without other macros it is less likely to knock your body out of its "fasted" state (in nature, there are no instances of fat not being paired with either protein or carbs, so you are essentially short-cutting your biology's design by eating an unnatural foodstuff). This is excentuated further by using something like [MCT oil](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medium-chain_triglyceride) vs. butter/ghee/coconut oil due to the fact that isolating medium chain fats means you are delivering the substrate that is the closest chemical match to the ketone form in our bodies and is treated as such with minimal processing necessary, whereas long-chain fats require some digestion.

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=122416)
>
> Thank you, Joshua - it was helpful The recipe there calls for a couple of tablespoons of cocoa. I've been adding 1 teaspoon only to my tea. At least, now I know how much to aim for.....

Sure thing! Please bear in mind that the recipe listed was not necessarily meant to be a daily indulgence, so I wouldn't necessarily use that "couple tablespoons" as a day-to-day benchmark goal. You'd be better off sticking with the teaspoons and increasing slowly to where you find the best tolerance/benefit ratio.

### caroline — 2014-04-19T02:12:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122439>

I do 2 BPC. For breakfast spaced out. Often the first at 9am and then one at 11 am.....and then dinner around 3:30 - 4:00 pm. This works great for me. If I am hungrier earlier I start eating slowly over the space of an hour and a half or two hours.

If I am really hungry in the morning I have some raw meat too or whatever is in the fridge.

### Curves — 2014-04-19T02:54:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122464>

Thanks SJoshua! I just picked up some MCT oil and will experiment with it for breakfast. Can you recommend a place to find the best online description of IF?

Audrey, I'm leaning toward following in your footsteps but will let it unfold gradually but adding BPC to my breakfast and then fazing out breakfast. My first intention is to phase out lunch. When I skip lunch now I get hungry about 3:00. Hopefully, the MCT will carry me a bit further until 4 or 5 so I can eat dinner with my honey at night. I may even try your raw meat if I get brave. I think I told Inger I would try it a year ago lol. The extra weight is motivating me.

And p.s. I was inspired by you to wear a dress today. I've been trying to fit in with the locals who love their pants but I'm a short dress kinda girl. It felt fantastic to be me again, even with the extra weight bouncing along with me

Elaine ..... I just got some quark cheese today - organic. I am going to mix in MCT oil. If you go back and look at Ricardo's posts on ketones ... He had some really interesting thoughts.

### Curves — 2014-04-22T02:09:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-calories-matter-when-you-eat-epi-paleo-and-youre-trying-to-lose-weight.10580/post-122880>

Thank Dr. K for the links. I read both leangains and your blog on IFing. Truth be told, I read until my head starting hurting but it was enough to motivate me. All I can say is "Wow!" Today is IFing Day 4 and I'm feeling great. I started each day with BPC, omitted breakfast, didn't limit calories and condensed my window to between 4-6 hours. I wasn't even hungry at noon but ate because I wanted to acclimate slowly like SJosha suggested. I even think my belly is smaller. I'm defintely going to continue this new investigation.

Audrey, I know you mentioned that you had success with this months ago, but I feel the timing is perfect right now.

And ps., I wore sandals with my short dress so I could show off my feet LOL!

### Clayton — 2014-04-17T23:43:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/niacin-and-riboflavin.10581/post-122243>

I haven't been able to sift through all the info on this site so maybe this is a stupid question...if we want to gain electrons could we not just supplement niacin (B3) and riboflavin (B2) which are both electron donors into the ETC? I hafta say I always feel better after a niacin flush.

### fitness@home — 2014-04-17T23:45:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/niacin-and-riboflavin.10581/post-122244>

> [Clayton said:](/goto/post?id=122243)
>
> I hafta say I always feel better after a niacin flush.

Not me! I quickly rash out, get itchy, and turn red for about 30-60 minutes.

Interested int he answer to your question though.

### Clayton — 2014-04-17T23:49:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/niacin-and-riboflavin.10581/post-122245>

I get the same response (redness, itchiness) but i also feel more energetic and alert.

### Clayton — 2014-04-18T02:11:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/niacin-and-riboflavin.10581/post-122264>

Another thought. Could these electrons throw off circadian biology based on the info/spin they carry?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-18T03:12:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/niacin-and-riboflavin.10581/post-122285>

There are at least three blogs on Niacin. Google Niacin and Jack Kruse to find them. Riboflavin is tied to layer 5 in neocortex. These are the motor neurons and motor neurons needs a ton of electrons. Riboflavin is part of cytochrome 2 as well......

# Thread: Gender bias in mitochondria
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gender-bias-in-mitochondria.10583/>
**Subforum:** Mitochondrial Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-18T06:56:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gender-bias-in-mitochondria.10583/post-122314>

The most powerful reason for a man to be healthy for longevity: Half of all women older than 65 are widows, and widows outnumber widowers three to one. At age 65, for every 100 American women, there are only 70 men. At age 85, there are only 38 men for every 100 women. And the longevity gap persists even into very old age. That is why female centenarians outnumber their male counterparts nine to one.

I like these odds and implications.

### Josh — 2014-04-19T06:34:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/current-blog.10586/post-122495>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=122492)
>
> nope......I am in New Orleans writing my book

Have you seen the book joke I posted in a couple places, it was especially for Caroline/Audrey?

A famous Soviet academic, Petya, is dying, and his friend and colleague, Vasya, comes to his
bedside to pay his last respects to this great old man. He finds Petya Lying there with closed eyes
and a sad look on his face.
Slowly he opens his eyes, looks at his friend, and then says, ”Vasya, look at all those books on that
wall over there – just look at them.”
”Yes, Petya,” replies his friend. ”Those are your most famous works. Everyone knows what an
important and...”
”Yes, yes, yes!” interrupts Petya. ”But look over there at that wall – you see all those books?”
”Yes, my dear friend. Those are the translations of your works into all the languages of the...”
”Yes, but that is not the point. Look over there – do you see those books?”
”But of course. Those are the works of your students and followers. We all admire...”
”No, but you don’t understand, Vasya!” exclaims Petya. ”Look, do you remember when we were
students, and we were sent off to work on the collective farm?”
”Of course I remember, my friend. How could I forget?”
”And do you remember, Vasya, walking down that country road one hot summer’s day?”
”Yes, Petya, yes I do.”
”And do you remember, we passed that cornfield, and there, sitting on the top of one of the
haystacks, was that pretty young Masha?”
”Yes, yes, Petya, I do! You liked her, I remember!”
”Yes,” says Petya, sinking back onto his pillow with a blissful smile on his face. ”You know what I did,
Vasya? I climbed up on top of the haystack and we were really starting to have a bloody good time.
But you know, my friend, those bloody haystacks – just when you are really getting somewhere,
whoosh!... they give way under you. I did everything I could, but every time that bloody haystack...
So you see, I have just been thinking, Vasya” – Petya looked round the room – ”how it would have
been if we had all these bloody books underneath us!”

# Thread: NASA IS GETTING THE MESSAGE
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nasa-is-getting-the-message.10590/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-18T18:59:12+0300

He got into the dance of it and it was fun. This helps me to model and visualize the emerging model here with changes in protein structure and physiological function based upon our emergent COE's. Great Blog.

### Josh — 2014-04-19T19:23:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/happy-holiday-2014-easter-and-passover.10596/post-122562>

Easter Zen Story...

One evening, Katz, a black cat who lived at Master Soen-sa’s ashram, died. The seven-year-old
daughter of one of Soen-sa’s students was very troubled by the death. After the burial and chanting
to Amida Buddha, she went to Soen-sa for an interview.
Soen-sa said, ’Do you have any questions?’
She said, ’Yes. What happened to Katzie? Where did he go?’
Soen sa said, ’Where do you come from?’
’From my mother’s belly.’
’Where does your mother come from?’
She was silent.
Soen-sa said, ’Everything in the world comes from the same one thing. It is like in a cookie factory.
Many different kinds of cookies are made – lions, tigers, elephants, houses, people. They all have
different shapes and different names, but they are all made from the same dough and they all taste
the same. So all the different things that you see – a cat, a person, a tree, the sun, this floor – all
these things are really the same.’
’What are they?’
’People give them many different names. When you are thinking, all things have different names
and different shapes. But when you are not thinking, all things are the same. There are no words for
them. People make the words. A cat doesn’t say, ”I am a cat.” People say, ”This is a cat.” The sun
doesn’t say, ”My name is sun.” People say, ”This is the sun.” So when someone asks you, ’ What is
this?”, how should you answer?’
’I shouldn’t use words.’
Soen-sa said, ’Very good! You shouldn’t use words. So if someone asks you, ”What is Buddha?”
what would be a good answer?’
She was silent.
Soen-sa said, ’Now you ask me.’
’What is Buddha?’
Soen-sa hit the floor.
She laughed.
Soen sa said, ’Now I ask you, ”What is Buddha?”’
She hit the floor.
’What is God?’
She hit the floor.
’What is your mother?’
She hit the floor.
’What are you?’
She hit the floor.
’Very good! This is what all things in the world are made of. You and Buddha and God and your
mother and the whole world are the same.’
She smiled.
Soen-sa said, ’Do you have any more questions?’
’You still haven’t told me where Katz went.’
Soen-sa leaned over, looked into her eyes, and said, ’You already understand.’
She said, ’Oh!’ and hit the floor very hard. Then she laughed.
Soen-sa said, ’Very, very good! That is how you should answer any question. That is the truth.’
She bowed and left. As she was opening the door, she turned to Soen-sa and said, ’Master, but I’m
not going to answer that way when I’m in school. I’m going to give regular answers!’
Soen-sa laughed.

Went climbing that sunday, did some hard crimps and all of a sudden, one of my finger really slipped and i got a really bad cut... Bleeding started but i felt that this could be fixed, just letting the air touch my wound, holding and fixing my finger for an hour stopped the bleeding totally. The funny thing, during the night something else happen, in the morning i lost my voice, got a light feeling of bad hearing also. This would tell me a steling syndrome was at work. To heal my wound i had to let go of the things that are sublime and delicate and since i also stressed these systems before they just went out with the garbage. This is the reason why someone that just kicked a rock yesterday can get bad hearing next day, another illness next IF you do not get energy back into the system.

I did cure myself on that monday but if i did not know how i would still be ill and perhaps starting a tsunami of problems.

The cure? Simple, sun, water, rocks, proteins and fat... Mixed with a real spoonful of sleep.

Really funny to start to understand how and why my systems do the things they do.

### nicld — 2014-04-20T04:35:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/revolutionary-new-beauty-patch.10597/post-122633>

Uhmmm, there is nothing in the patch. It was a study on women's phycology and the placebo effect. The women started seeing them self as more beautiful as the weeks went on and were brought to tears when the found it was all in their head which the video showed.

It shows that our mind is more powerful than any "drug".

### Inger — 2014-04-20T14:43:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/revolutionary-new-beauty-patch.10597/post-122668>

loved this Nicole. I need this right now... every day. I cannot tell why but I assure you this is one of my weak spots.
I have to work hard on my thoughts... because of certain things. and i so know, the secret is all in my mind - I need to remind myself every day,and not allow me to be dragged down by circumstances

### fitness@home — 2014-04-20T14:55:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/revolutionary-new-beauty-patch.10597/post-122669>

That was a very powerful short video.

Really brings home how hard we are on ourselves (women especially). For example, ever wonder why there are times you can go shopping for clothes and NOTHING looks right on your body; and another time you find a ton of things you love?

I know that I'm too hard on myself and give that negative inner voice too much power at times. I look on the mirror and see myself aging (when did I start looking so much like Mom?!) but inside I still feel like I'm in my 30s. For me, I need to work on how to accept aging gracefully and embrace it -- not try to ignore it .

Last thing I want to be is one of those women that shops in Forever21...swear I saw a 70 year old woman last night at a restaurant who was trying to appear 40! The make up and outfit were for someone much younger. It didn't work...

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-20T17:01:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/revolutionary-new-beauty-patch.10597/post-122684>

This just highlights how distorted our personal view of beauty is in the western world - as a result of the marketing of barbie, and hollywood's definition of what is beautiful.

The most beautiful person I've ever seen was a 90 year old man pushing his cart of fresh veggies to market in the Okinawa countryside. I was out hiking - when I saw him. what was amazing was seeing his face light up when he got to market - and his wife came out of the stall to help him set up the veggies for sale. radiant. There was more beauty in those two old wrinkled faces than there every has been in hollywood.....

We've be sold an illusion of beauty instead of learning how to recognize what true beauty is.

# Thread: Looking for resources on Brain/Gut/antibiotic connection.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looking-for-resources-on-brain-gut-antibiotic-connection.10599/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Marie — 2014-04-20T04:49:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looking-for-resources-on-brain-gut-antibiotic-connection.10599/post-122636>

A friend's 60 y/o hubby is having +40 seizures a day and the docs can't find anything wrong with his brain. I suggested that they look for a gut connection and asked if he'd had any heavy rounds of antibiotics. She said "Funny, yes. And the seizures dropped to 2 a day."

They've cleaned up his/their diet to the point that the hubby says "Life is better when you are eating close to the ground." His intuition is kicking in.

I'm guessing this is a Vit B issue and that the antibiotics are changing the gut profile for the better for a short while, but I'm looking for resources to send them to give to the docs.

(Oh, and yes... horrific circadian cycles with EMF exposure as he is a programmer.)

Marie

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-20T12:18:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looking-for-resources-on-brain-gut-antibiotic-connection.10599/post-122658>

> [Marie said:](/goto/post?id=122636)
>
> (Oh, and yes... horrific circadian cycles with EMF exposure as he is a programmer.)

Get him grounded. Send him a copy of this, perhaps:

[http://www.amazon.com/Earthing-Most-Important-Health-Discovery/dp/1591202833/](http://www.amazon.com/Earthing-Most-Important-Health-Discovery/dp/1591202833/)

Besides normalizing you electrically, it will, apparently, help your gut bacteria to normalize. Or perhaps I shouldn't say "besides" -- all these things seem to be linked.

I can recall some years ago reading in that marvellous book _[Lights Out](http://www.amazon.com/Lights-Out-Sleep-Sugar-Survival/dp/0671038680/)_ how harmful bacteria tend to get kept in check by benign ones during sleep; so one of the one of the important functions of sleep is allowing that to happen. Well, one of the most consistently reported effects of grounding/earthing is better sleep: some people who've only had a handful of hours a night and broken at that report going through solid for 7, 8, 9 or more the first time they sleep on a grounded sheet. If this man is a programmer, I'll bet his sleep is shot.

[http://www.amazon.com/Earthing-HSK-Half-Sheet-Kit/dp/B0071URSUW/](http://www.amazon.com/Earthing-HSK-Half-Sheet-Kit/dp/B0071URSUW/)

Not what you asked, however ...

I'd suggest trying pubmed:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed)

with some relevant search terms, e.g:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=brain+gut](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=brain+gut)

# Thread: An Easter 2014 lesson:  Where you really come from
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-easter-2014-lesson-where-you-really-come-from.10603/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-20T17:21:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-easter-2014-lesson-where-you-really-come-from.10603/post-122686>

[http://www.messynessychic.com/2013/11/01/the-last-japanese-mermaids/](http://www.messynessychic.com/2013/11/01/the-last-japanese-mermaids/)

I would suggest after you read and look at these amazing photo's you have a Re-read of Brain gut 1-6 in order.

Matter organizes itself according to how you think!!

# Thread: How do you do raw?

**Posts:** 64
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cantweight — 2014-04-20T19:12:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-do-raw.10604/post-122701>

I hate to admit it but I have been brought up without raw foods in my diet even veggies were rarely served raw to me as a child. Our meats were cooked to oblivion and usually covered with some kind of sauce (which I was grateful for since the meat was so damn dry!). Canned hams, canned veggies, and rice-a-roni were staples....makes me shudder now.

My children eat everything! They love all kinds foods having been brought up to try anything at least once and more often then not they like what they try. Our meats are medium rare, seafood is as plentiful as the wallet allows, veggies and fruits are eaten seasonally.

As for raw proteins I am stymied beyond oysters, carpaccio, and sushi.

I'm not sure we are ready for high meats and MHS.....wondering how the rest of you plan to diversify your guts with raw food?

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-20T20:00:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-do-raw.10604/post-122704>

Chrsti

Have you considered making Graved Lachs? Igner had a great recipe that she posted that I use when I make it.

Wild caught salmon (I just buy a filet from Trader Joes) & Defrost it.

then pull out a large piece of parchment paper, and drape it across a plate.
put salmon (or what ever fish you'd like) on the parchment.
Drizzle some wild raw honey (Very small amt)
Salt generously with celtic sea salt
add herbs of your choice and whole pepper corn

Wrap up the salmon with the remaining parchment paper and set another plate on top - go ahead and put something on it to make it heavy and put it in the fridge. let sit for 24-48 hrs - if you can.

eat to you hearts content!

I'm also a huge fan of ceviche. shrimp, scallops and conch are my favorites!

DH makes some killer steak tartar

### Inger — 2014-04-21T09:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-do-raw.10604/post-122752>

Technically dried meat at temps less than 40 degree C are raw.. but I do not think they have the benefit juicy raw seafood or meat has. I still feel better eating dehydrated meat than cooked..... Store bought dehydrated meat/jerky is pretty sure not raw

I love to dehydrate burgers for breakfast at 35 degree C over night, they are just dried on the surface... so yummy with coconut oil or raw butter
but I sure think it is better not to use a dehydrator for health!

To keep meat on a wooden plate in the fridge, uncovered, is a great thing, it dries out a bit on the surface and get a salami like taste after some weeks... SO yummy! Just eaten plain!

looks yummy, no?

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-22T12:25:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-do-raw.10604/post-122961>

> [cantweight said:](/goto/post?id=122701)
>
> I'm not sure we are ready for high meats and MHS.....wondering how the rest of you plan to diversify your guts with raw food?

Much the same as you mention.

I do sometimes buy and eat the fermented cod liver oil. I realise that's controversial for a number of reasons and whole fermented livers would probably be better, but anyway ...

What no one else mentioned was raw dairy. That's again controversial.

You hear a lot about lactose-intolerance in Paleo circles. That, I think, is overblown. All one has to do is to lactic-ferment the food. Practically speaking, people with quite compromised guts on the GAPS Diet seem to get benefit from home-made yoghurt and kefir and well-aged raw cheeses. There's data for you, as Humpty-Dumpty might have said.

What, it seems to me, is more of an issue is the _proteins_, in dairy namely, beta-casein -- at least some forms of it. It's been covered here before, so I'll say no more. Keith Woodford's has some intriguing and suggestive material -- quickly summarized here:

[http://thebovine.wordpress.com/2009...e-enough-of-an-improvement-over-store-bought/](http://thebovine.wordpress.com/2009/03/20/the-devil-in-the-milk-dr-thomas-cowan-on-how-a2-milk-is-the-answer-to-the-mystery-of-why-even-raw-milk-sometimes-does-not-seem-to-be-enough-of-an-improvement-over-store-bought/)

I have wondered whether if wheat had never been in the diet (and everything else were in order) whether dairy products (of a suitable sort) would not be problematic. I'd suggest the apparently very healthy state of herding peoples from the Ancient Scythians to the modern Nuer -- even the mediaeval Irish who made abundant use of dairy but, apparently, little of cereal grains beyond a little oats -- suggests that it wouldn't be.

I once heard Sally Fallon asked on a podcast two or three years ago (probably by Jimmy Moore) why the WAPF recommends raw milk. (This probably on account of the ban on it by Paleo.) "What is it about milk? What is so special about it?" her interlocutor said. Her response was, "Nothing." She said they were sure that it was important to have some raw protein and some raw fat in the diet, although they didn't know why. All traditional societies ate that way. The WAPF recommended raw milk, because most modern Americans would drink raw milk; most would not, for example, eat raw liver or "rotting fish":

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rakfisk](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rakfisk)

True enough.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-22T15:11:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-do-raw.10604/post-123009>

Here's a key piece from the recent blog:

> This is why modern cooking techniques need to be questioned too. It is even why bone broths and seafood exoskeleton broths have downsides. Few people see this. Crock pots and Sous vide’s are great modern tools for convenience, but they degrade our microbiome and create a new environmental mismatchs that modern humans have had to deal with. It’s been in their blind spot too. We need to realize eating the tough sinew and tendons of fresh animals allows for the glycans and GAG’s to go undigested and make it to our distal gut to feed the critters there to keep the colon healthy by changing these things to butyrate and creating subcutaneous fat and not visceral fat.

I like broths and use them, but in the evolutionary perspective I think they're suspiciously hard things to make. They seem to have got into Paleo out of WAPF practice -- although you won't hear Paleo people admitting so, giving credit where it's due not being one of their strong suits.

But here's the point: how do make broth/stock without a cooking pot? Sure, you don't need iron, or even pottery, to boil food. You can use a skin bag, or even a lined hole in the ground, and drop heated stones in. But who's going to sit there dropping stones in every few minutes for as long as it takes to make stock, which takes _hours_? I just don't see it.

And that's man. Hominids certainly weren't making broth. No, the sinews and tendons have to have been going down whole -- and raw or with brief heating.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-22T20:15:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-do-raw.10604/post-123059>

> [BJK77 said:](/goto/post?id=123039)
>
> If you're a dumbass, I am too  I've been trying to do more raw. I've been eating at least a half dozen raw oysters daily for several weeks now and even before that I had them frequently, just not daily. In the past week or so I've started eating beef liver and ground beef very rare - not completely raw, but pretty dang close!
>
> I love pork, but I used to always eat pork roasts and now these damn histamine issues have put a stop to that. Like you, I thought pork had to be cooked as well.

Supposedly, if liver is frozen for 14 days that will kill any parasites, and it can then be eaten raw quite safely.

The ground beef -- I think that's going to depend on who minced it. If the meat came from a good source and you did it at home and your equipment was clean, that's probably fine. But if you bought it ready done, it should probably be thoroughly cooked. The problem in this case is a surface area one: when the meat's cut up small, there's a _huge_ surface area -- plenty of room for harmful bacteria to multiply on. With a large piece of meat only the outside surface is exposed. Further, once it's chopped small, the outside surface is divided up through the food. Lack of care at the slaughterhouse can mean the outside of the meat came into contact with faeces. And the butcher or supermarket only has to be a bit lax with hygiene: there's a heck of a lot of meat going through _their_ grinders and infected fragments could get left on the equipment.

I don't mean to evoke the disgust response by mentioning dung. If one could be sure the animals were healthy maybe it wouldn't matter:

> I may add that Mongols keep milk in the dirtiest way imaginable. It frequently happened that one of them would ride up to our tent with a jugful for sale, the lid and spout of the vessel having been smeared with fresh cow dung to prevent the liquid splashing out on the road. Cows' teats are never washed before milking, nor are the vessels into which the milk is poured."
>
> \-- Przhevalsky (1875)

[http://www.westonaprice.org/in-his-footsteps/diet-of-mongolia](http://www.westonaprice.org/in-his-footsteps/diet-of-mongolia)

But if the animals are not healthy and _their_ gut bacteria possibly disrupted?

My conclusion: nowadays, if you want to eat _steak tartare_, make it yourself.

Pork? I'd make sure that was fairly well-done, and possibly even use a meat-thermometer to check if you're not sure. There's always salami and air-dried ham -- _prosciutto crudo_ and so forth -- but ordinary pork I would cook. The risk of trichinosis is too great to my thinking.

It's often said that the Jews don't avoid pork for no reason. And, indeed, it _may_ be because of this problem. But it becomes a debatable point when you don't know for sure why they avoid it and no _reason_ is given in the Old Testament. I believe at the last count there were 7 (seven) different theories as to why they don't -- including the intriguing, and fairly plausible, one that pork had perhaps once been a specially revered meat. (Pork is, indeed, highly regarded in many cultures -- the heroes eat it in Valhalla, for example.) Accordingly, it may have originally been consumed as a sacrifice on special occasions -- and _consequently_ avoided at other times. But no one really knows. And in the Bible the answer is simply that "God has said not" -- a heuristic terminus in a traditional religious culture if ever there was one. You don't argue with God: "Canst thou draw out leviathan with an hook?" etc . …

Jews do not, of course, "abominate" pigs: they simply don't eat them.

Anyway, historical excursus aside, I'd cook pork well or eat the salted, air-dried stuff.

I'd also cook chicken well. Maybe raw wild jungle-fowl would be OK, but I think modern domesticated fowl tend to be too intensively farmed and there's probably some risk of salmonella if they're undercooked.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-27T16:59:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-do-raw.10604/post-123583>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=123560)
>
> Anyone know where to get proper salami in the UK? Would it be at market places? I assume supermarket ones would be bad in some way. Sooperb?

Interesting question. If you look at the ingredients list on most of the packs of these, they do seem to have some kind of sugar in there usually, but it's usually a negligible amount. See here, for example:

[http://www.waitrose.com/shop/ProductView-10317--5700-Waitrose farm assured antipasto misto#.U10J-1ySFFI](http://www.waitrose.com/shop/ProductView-10317--5700-Waitrose+farm+assured+antipasto+misto#.U10J-1ySFFI)

There's dextrose in that, but it doesn't amount to much.

Is this lacto-fermented? I don't know. I'd have thought it would state "lactic culture" or something of the sort in the ingredients list, which this example doesn't. But maybe I'd be wrong there.

You could try a farmers market, particularly if there are people over from the Continent selling their wares. I've been to a couple of French markets in England, and honestly the quality of the food is in general so much higher than you get in the U.K. it's embarrassing. There was a tent selling biscuits and the biscuits were all-butter. Needless to say, I didn't buy any, but can you imagine that as a matter of course over here? Whatever kind of shortening is cheapest tends to go in, regardless of the health consequences -- or, which is where the French put their foot down, the taste.

It seems you get an Italian market in Sheffield. I'd think that would be worth a look:

[https://www.sheffield.gov.uk/busine....html#continentalandotherinternationalmarkets](https://www.sheffield.gov.uk/business-economy/markets/farmers-and-specialist-markets.html#continentalandotherinternationalmarkets)

Or have a look around online. With a few relevant search terms I found this:

[http://www.therealboar.co.uk/salami.html](http://www.therealboar.co.uk/salami.html)

If you click the "stockists" link you can see they're stocked in outlets all over the country.
