### BJK77 — 2014-04-30T15:36:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-do-raw.10604/post-124041>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=123059)
>
> Supposedly, if liver is frozen for 14 days that will kill any parasites, and it can then be eaten raw quite safely.
>
> The ground beef -- I think that's going to depend on who minced it. If the meat came from a good source and you did it at home and your equipment was clean, that's probably fine. But if you bought it ready done, it should probably be thoroughly cooked. The problem in this case is a surface area one: when the meat's cut up small, there's a _huge_ surface area -- plenty of room for harmful bacteria to multiply on. With a large piece of meat only the outside surface is exposed. Further, once it's chopped small, the outside surface is divided up through the food. Lack of care at the slaughterhouse can mean the outside of the meat came into contact with faeces. And the butcher or supermarket only has to be a bit lax with hygiene: there's a heck of a lot of meat going through _their_ grinders and infected fragments could get left on the equipment.
>
> My conclusion: nowadays, if you want to eat _steak tartare_, make it yourself.
>
> Pork? I'd make sure that was fairly well-done, and possibly even use a meat-thermometer to check if you're not sure. There's always salami and air-dried ham -- _prosciutto crudo_ and so forth -- but ordinary pork I would cook. The risk of trichinosis is too great to my thinking.
>
> It's often said that the Jews don't avoid pork for no reason. And, indeed, it _may_ be because of this problem. But it becomes a debatable point when you don't know for sure why they avoid it and no _reason_ is given in the Old Testament. I believe at the last count there were 7 (seven) different theories as to why they don't -- including the intriguing, and fairly plausible, one that pork had perhaps once been a specially revered meat. (Pork is, indeed, highly regarded in many cultures -- the heroes eat it in Valhalla, for example.) Accordingly, it may have originally been consumed as a sacrifice on special occasions -- and _consequently_ avoided at other times. But no one really knows. And in the Bible the answer is simply that "God has said not" -- a heuristic terminus in a traditional religious culture if ever there was one. You don't argue with God: "Canst thou draw out leviathan with an hook?" etc . …
>
> Jews do not, of course, "abominate" pigs: they simply don't eat them.
>
> Anyway, historical excursus aside, I'd cook pork well or eat the salted, air-dried stuff.
>
> I'd also cook chicken well. Maybe raw wild jungle-fowl would be OK, but I think modern domesticated fowl tend to be too intensively farmed and there's probably some risk of salmonella if they're undercooked.

Good info Gagnard. Thanks! I do know my meats are very high quality. I get them locally from an organic, grass-fed/pastured Amish farm and I visit often to see the animals. I don't, however, know anything about the butcher and I didn't think about the greater surface area of ground meats. Good points to consider.

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-07T20:22:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-do-raw.10604/post-125665>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=125661)
>
> Thanks sooperb. I have found a substitute for it at the moment at the asain market. It's just raw beef strips, high in fat, and fairly cheap.

The word "cheap" rings alarm bells for me. I'd worry about cheap beef. How do you know it's beef and not, for example, horse?

Now there's nothing wrong with horse _in and of itself_.

Actually, there's an interesting historical story behind why people don't eat horsemeat in England. It seems to go back to a letter sent out by the Pope back in Anglo-Saxon times. He was anxious for the English to avoid horsemeat, because they'd only recently been converted, and the horse was sacred to Woden, so that any horsemeat likely came from a horse sacrificed to him. This went right back to the Early Church which decided that Jewish dietary law did not apply to Gentile Christians but which drew the line at "meats offered to idols":

[http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Acts+15:29&version=KJV](http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Acts+15%3A29&version=KJV)

Gaul had been converted far longer, so the Pope had no worries about people in what's now France, and consequently the letter never went to them, and the French still eat horse.

The story's told here:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Anglo-Saxon-Food-Drink-Distribution-Consumption/dp/1898281556/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/Anglo-Saxon-Food-Drink-Distribution-Consumption/dp/1898281556/)​

Anyway, back to _in and of itself --_ that's the point. Horse is OK, but the horse that was finding its way into the British food-distribution system -- and which was in products from major companies and national supermarket chains a year or two back -- was a worry, because it wasn't supposed to be there. Much of it wasn't fit for human consumption; the animals had been sick; there were residues of veterinary drugs in it, and so on …

I'd be wary of cheap "beef" whose provenance you don't know.

You can get high quality dried beef from grass-fed Scottish cattle here:

[http://www.biltong.co.uk](http://www.biltong.co.uk)

Or you can always buy a dehydrator and make it yourself.

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-08T10:45:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-do-raw.10604/post-125792>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=125788)
>
> Haha, can't win can I. Thanks for the info. I think I am going to stick with this stuff for a bit regardless and see if I notice any difference.

Well ... it's just that "cheap" isn't always what you want to look for when it comes to food.

Most of us spend quite a lot on all sorts of things, but when it comes to what we're putting in our mouthes we want to buy cheap. Could you imagine that in France or Italy? Doesn't this tweet from the Caveman Doctor say it all about Anglo-American attitudes?

[https://twitter.com/CavemanDoctor/status/462403363661836288](https://twitter.com/CavemanDoctor/status/462403363661836288)

As for eating things raw ... it seems that all traditional cultures from the Eskimos to the French have _some_ raw foods in their diet. But there's no known isolated healthy group of people that's eaten _all_\-raw -- just as there's none that's eaten all-vegetarian. (Weston Price looked for the latter out of interest and could find none. Price eventually found a location where animal foods were hard-to-come-by: the people there supplemented their vegetable diet with cannibalism.  ...)

And when eating raw foods one needs to exercise some caution. Part of that is knowing all about the quality of your meat and how it's been raised. The WAPF, and now some paleo people, go as far as saying, "Get to know the farmer, look around the farm, and talk to him."

Inger is a country girl and does actually live on a farm. She eats a lot of game -- which is wild anyway. Other meat is either from her grandparents' farm or will come from a farmer she knows and whose animal husbandry she knows about and approves of. (She has talked about such things on the board before.) If it's fish … well, that comes from the sea, and, heck, the fishermen have probably just been flirting with her. Wouldn't you?

Inger knows about food and she knows about sources and supply chains.

People on the board will say try a farmers' market. The operative word there is _**farmers**_ not _**market**_. By the latter Sheffield City Council merely means sales taking place not on permanent premises. The point of farmers' markets is to put housewives in direct touch with food producers. It's all about supply chains.

### Inger — 2014-05-08T11:56:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-do-raw.10604/post-125800>

hehe... Gangrad I have to disappoint you now.. this is not really a farm.. just my parents have chickens and a few sheep.. lol Neighbors are around too, but not close enough to get any wifi signal, thanks God!
and I have eaten all sort of foods raw.. supermarket vacuum packed stuff... farmed fish... old fish... I am totally not paranoid about what I put in my mouth... lol as long as it is living thing
but that is what I do, and that is my own risk. For others, I always say, try to find best quality! And educate yourself about parasites........

And sure, I do all I can to get best quality! No need to get hormones and antibioticts etc. in my body. But I am not too scared to eat such stuff a few times in my lifetime, raw. But for regular consumption, no!
It is not the raw aspect I fear, bacterias or stuff, it is the other poison in it that stays when cooked too. And if I have to eat the same stuff cooked or raw I prefer raw.... because it makes me feel way better

IDK how much people cooked long ago, and I could not care less... lol I just care what is my own N=1 and that is... when I do all raw it feels very very amazing

the more challenges I have the clearer it makes me see - that is my impression.
But - _I could be totally wrong and just make it up in my head._..  I know that. So I lay back, continue to do what I think works for me.. and see where this all goes  and if I get convinced I need to cook part of my food, I sure will change my habits!
I do eat something cooked like pre cooked mussels once in a while tho, so I get the cooked part anyways even if most days not

# Thread: Do Food Electrons Impart a Quantum Effect?

### Melanie Procter — 2014-04-21T10:56:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-food-electrons-impart-a-quantum-effect.10605/post-122761>

**I just read this post.
[http://jackkruse.com/do-food-electrons-impart-a-quantum-effect/](http://jackkruse.com/do-food-electrons-impart-a-quantum-effect/)

Here is a quote from the post.

"I have been an Einstein freak my whole life**. And I have been wondering for 15 years whether an electron from carbs, fat or protein, or somewhere else is somehow categorized differently than one another by metabolism. If so this could have major implications for a new understanding of all the biologic pathways in metabolism and in aging." --Jack Kruse

I know people in the ormus community/monotomic elements state there is a difference. I wonder where these monotomic elements fall into the concept.

Mel

### Melanie Procter — 2014-04-21T11:03:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-food-electrons-impart-a-quantum-effect.10605/post-122762>

I know in a recent blog or webinar, I heard Jack say oriental medicine works with protons as opposed to electrons. I just remember that in passing. So, I could be misquoting him. However, that interested in me. I know he stated that electrons are massless and protons have mass. I couldn't really make sense of how protons move through the meridians.

# Thread: ice vest + yoga or bodyweight exercises

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### freezin4areason — 2014-04-21T16:41:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-vest-yoga-or-bodyweight-exercises.10609/post-122798>

Hello All,

I'm curious to hear people's experience of exercising while using a various cooling vests. I recently bought a coolfatburner vest and I'm wondering if wearing it while doing a fairly vigorous yoga session or body weight exercises would increase the speed of cold adaption. My rationale for this assumption is a theoretical greater release of irisin, the amount produced by shivering and the amount produced from muscle contractions. What are people's thoughts?

Best,
Jake

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-22T13:30:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-vest-yoga-or-bodyweight-exercises.10609/post-122986>

It would but it is not as effective as the ice in water. This is how you control mass, improve electron flow, increase energy efficiency all of which activate Beta 3 SNS to turn on irising to convert WAT to BAT to empty it........you basically turn WAT into proton heat using CT and irisin to do so. While this happen leptin falls and adiponectin rises tightening the tensegrity of the adipose layer......... as this happens you get even more energy efficient

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-22T13:30:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-vest-yoga-or-bodyweight-exercises.10609/post-122987>

you need a human pic so we know who we are talking to.

### sooperb — 2014-04-27T16:25:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-vest-yoga-or-bodyweight-exercises.10609/post-123580>

Yoga in an ice vest, that would be interesting. Do ice vests drip when the ice melts?

### Da-mo — 2014-04-28T05:58:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-vest-yoga-or-bodyweight-exercises.10609/post-123618>

No the ice vests are sealed - they don't drip.

I use the ice-vest in many situations.

1) I am moving through a hot industrial environment for about an hour at a time or working (physically) in a hot environment.
2) With my legs in the hot tub/spa.
3) In the car during my commute to/from work

1) and 2) above I'm going for some Fournier effect. 3) I'm going more for lower temps.

I find that I can get to a nice cherry red skin colour by the time the ice has melted. When I first started the vest would take me an hour to melt but nowadays its more like 40 minutes.

I have seen people running wearing ice-vests and I think exercise while wearing one is somewhat along the lines of the Vasper system. [http://vasper.com/](http://vasper.com/)

While maybe not as effective as ice/water immersion, I have found the ice-vest to be a very convenient way to integrate CT into my daily activity. Its the CT you can take with you and do concurrently with other things.

### nonchalant — 2014-04-29T23:30:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-vest-yoga-or-bodyweight-exercises.10609/post-123942>

>
> I use the ice-vest in many situations.
>
> 1) I am moving through a hot industrial environment for about an hour at a time or working (physically) in a hot environment.
> 2) With my legs in the hot tub/spa.
> 3) In the car during my commute to/from work
>
> 1) and 2) above I'm going for some Fournier effect. 3) I'm going more for lower temps.
>
> I find that I can get to a nice cherry red skin colour by the time the ice has melted. When I first started the vest would take me an hour to melt but nowadays its more like 40 minutes.
>
> I have seen people running wearing ice-vests and I think exercise while wearing one is somewhat along the lines of the Vasper system. [http://vasper.com/](http://vasper.com/)
>
> While maybe not as effective as ice/water immersion, I have found the ice-vest to be a very convenient way to integrate CT into my daily activity. Its the CT you can take with you and do concurrently with other things.

My cat has also benefited from an ice pack, though I haven't fashioned a vest for her yet. ;-)
Our car's air conditioner is out, and yesterday we had a hot drive back from the country. She was upset and holding her mouth open to pant. I couldn't get her to lay on an ice pack (covered with cloth) but she finally submitted to having on put on top of her. She quickly became comfortable. After a bit she moved around so she was on top of the pack, and she stayed there for hours, occasionally moving to get different parts cooled down.

# Thread: Optimism as antientropic. Happy Easter Jack!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimism-as-antientropic-happy-easter-jack.10610/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NeilBB — 2014-04-21T16:41:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimism-as-antientropic-happy-easter-jack.10610/post-122799>

[http://russmatney.com/getitwrite/optimism-and-creativity-vs-entropy.html](http://russmatney.com/getitwrite/optimism-and-creativity-vs-entropy.html)

> That's what creativity is: picking up the pieces entropy leaves behind and recombining them into something that's never existed before.

### NeilBB — 2014-04-21T17:41:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimism-as-antientropic-happy-easter-jack.10610/post-122812>

My latest formulation:

Healthy skepticism/cynicism is an appropriate short-term contextual mental response to a particular negative event or idea. Much in the same way that acute inflammation can be an appropriate physical response to a specific biological insult. These are both appropriate short-term adaptations.

However, the chronic state of _pessimism_ is an ingrained widespread non-contextual negative mental outlook that has been habituated. It is no longer adaptive because it is no longer time-limited, contextualized, and targeted. It is the mental analogy to chronic inflammation of the body. The protective mechanism has surpassed it's function and become a diffuse problem. A source of energy loss...

A consistent pessimistic attitude is the mental equivalent of an elevated CRP for the body. I mean that first of all as an analogy, but am sure its true on a quantum level as well. Pessimism will likely turn out to be a direct source of electron loss and hormonal imbalance as well, I predict Jack will say soon...

So the same way that one must control systemic inflammation in a hostile physical world for health, one must also control pessimism for mental health in a hostile social one. Two sides of the same coin that decrease entropy and maximize the flow of electronic energy in a biological system. I would guess that optimism will somehow make more electrons stick to your DHA, as well! A good thing. It also wouldn't surprise me if this turned out to be the fundamental basis for the widely known _placebo effect_ that has been ignored by western medicine for so long.

Eastern mystics, faith healers, and energy medicine people have long known all this on some vague experiential level, but now we are getting to the point where we are going to be explain exactly _why_ and _how_ from a scientific perspective, for the first time! That is pretty cool, I think.

So Jack, I'd like you to know that now, I'm an optimist! Maybe I have been already, but I know it doesn't always come across that way on the internet. BUT, I still reserve the right to express a brief inflammatory outburst of "healthy negative skepticism" about a particular topic, from time to time!

# Thread: Shifting gut flora
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shifting-gut-flora.10611/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Clayton — 2014-04-21T18:17:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shifting-gut-flora.10611/post-122820>

So I have been adding almonds and other nuts to my diet as well as some raw meat/seafood and swallowing larger sizes of food over the past 10 days or so. I have noticed that my sleep has become horrible and that I have become irritable as a bear waking from hibernation. I'm assuming this could be due to gut flora starting to shift..has anyone else noticed anything similar?

### fitness@home — 2014-04-21T18:31:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shifting-gut-flora.10611/post-122821>

Occasional lower GI issues (enough said).

### nonchalant — 2014-04-21T18:38:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shifting-gut-flora.10611/post-122824>

Every time I change my diet, my tummy gets petulant, and I get irritable. I was already eating raw nuts, but adding larger pieces of raw seafood is making changes. At least no constipation...

### Clayton — 2014-04-21T18:55:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shifting-gut-flora.10611/post-122831>

Ya my bowel movements have been fine..maybe improved but I have also woken up in the night with heartburn and some aching in the lower abdomen. As far as the sleep and irritability just think it could be a disruption in neurotransmitter production and some die-off. Thanks for chiming in guys!

### nicld — 2014-04-22T03:32:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shifting-gut-flora.10611/post-122892>

Interesting, I was wondering the same thing. I am getting more heartburn and I was never one to have other then being pregnant. Also as soon as I eat the rumbling starts. I hope this means things are getting better.

### SueZ — 2014-05-05T20:50:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shifting-gut-flora.10611/post-125326>

Although eating this way had helped the heartburn, I still need to keep a slice of ginger root in the side of my mouth about 20/24. Also the adjustable bed and sleeping in almost a sitting position (more upright than the recliner) has helped me tremendously.

### nonchalant — 2014-05-05T22:24:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shifting-gut-flora.10611/post-125341>

Interesting. For a couple days sometime after starting to eat more raw, I had brief bouts with heartburn. Fine now. Something was happening down there for sure.

### Aerose91 — 2014-05-29T03:02:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shifting-gut-flora.10611/post-128767>

Correct me if I'm wrong but don't nuts and seeds contain high amounts of lectins which are bad for leaky gut. Could you also have hypochlorhydria?

# Thread: Metabolomics? NIH
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metabolomics-nih.10612/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-04-21T19:29:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metabolomics-nih.10612/post-122836>

[http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/early/2014/04/16/ajcn.113.078758.abstract](http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/early/2014/04/16/ajcn.113.078758.abstract)

> Objectives: Our first aim was to identify metabolites that are biomarkers of usual dietary intake. Second, among serum metabolites correlated with diet, we evaluated metabolite reproducibility and required sample sizes to determine the potential for metabolomics in epidemiologic studies.
>
> Design: Baseline serum from 502 participants in the Prostate, Lung, Colorectal, and Ovarian (PLCO) Cancer Screening Trial was analyzed by using ultra-high-performance liquid-phase chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry. Usual intakes of 36 dietary groups were estimated by using a food-frequency questionnaire. Dietary biomarkers were identified by using partial Pearson's correlations with Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons. Intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) between samples collected 1 y apart in a subset of 30 individuals were calculated to evaluate intraindividual metabolite variability.
>
> Results: We detected 412 known metabolites. Citrus, green vegetables, red meat, shellfish, fish, peanuts, rice, butter, coffee, beer, liquor, total alcohol, and multivitamins were each correlated with at least one metabolite (_P_ < 1.093 × 10−6; _r_ = −0.312 to 0.398); in total, 39 dietary biomarkers were identified. Some correlations (citrus intake with stachydrine) replicated previous studies; others, such as peanuts and tryptophan betaine, were novel findings. Other strong associations included coffee (with trigonelline-_N_\-methylnicotinate and quinate) and alcohol (with ethyl glucuronide). Intraindividual variability in metabolite levels (1-y ICCs) ranged from 0.27 to 0.89. Large, but attainable, sample sizes are required to detect associations between metabolites and disease in epidemiologic studies, further emphasizing the usefulness of metabolomics in nutritional epidemiology.
>
> Conclusions: We identified dietary biomarkers by using metabolomics in an epidemiologic data set. Given the strength of the associations observed, we expect that some of these metabolites will be validated in future studies and later used as biomarkers in large cohorts to study diet-disease associations. The PLCO trial was registered at [clinicaltrials.gov](http://clinicaltrials.gov/) as [NCT00002540](http://ajcn.nutrition.org/external-ref?link_type=CLINTRIALGOV&access_num=NCT00002540).

[http://www.nutraingredients-usa.com...tter+Daily&c=T5DwZFxZmFHDKkAUN/jBxBvDZTrQryR6](http://www.nutraingredients-usa.com/Research/Advancing-nutritional-epidemiology-to-a-whole-new-level-NIH-scientists-publish-metabolomics-biomarkers-to-study-diet-disease-associations/?utm_source=newsletter_daily&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=Newsletter%2BDaily&c=T5DwZFxZmFHDKkAUN%2FjBxBvDZTrQryR6)

### nonchalant — 2014-04-21T22:34:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metabolomics-nih.10612/post-122863>

Wonder if those 39 biomarkers include ones for "vegetable" oil, high fructose corn syrup, soy, fluoride, wheat, A1 dairy, and MSG.

### prAna303 — 2014-04-21T22:20:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hmmmm-savant-syndrome.10614/post-122860>

> When he extended his hand out in front of him, it was like “watching a slow-motion film,” as if every slight movement was in stop-motion animation.

This directly hit me... Quite often i feel that i see the world very different, almost in that kind of slow motion... Often when i see a dance performance i can zoom in on details and slowly go into that special moment, the same when listening to music, i build a room that is bigger than the sum of the environment, almost like expanding...

When i run with friends in the mountains, often downhill i see a line, i can bounce on rocks and roots where others crash and fall, it is like time was so much slower for me.

### sjoshua — 2014-04-21T23:42:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hmmmm-savant-syndrome.10614/post-122873>

Excellent story, @[RobH](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1033/) thanks for sharing.

> [prAna303 said:](/goto/post?id=122860)
>
> This directly hit me... Quite often i feel that i see the world very different, almost in that kind of slow motion... Often when i see a dance performance i can zoom in on details and slowly go into that special moment, the same when listening to music, i build a room that is bigger than the sum of the environment, almost like expanding...
>
> When i run with friends in the mountains, often downhill i see a line, i can bounce on rocks and roots where others crash and fall, it is like time was so much slower for me.

I think it is the animal processor in us... much more efficient than the intelligence we've piggybacked on top of it. It allows my dog to complete precise math in his head to catch a frisbee at 100 yards mid air, or catch the "squirrel" travelling at varying rates in a mostly circular pattern while using a [flirt pole](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wSKkQcE7Mcs). He will neither ever be cognizant of nor could he ever reproduce that information outside of the games... but, in his world he would never have to.

This is the data you don't NEED to know that you know. It would be like trying to consciously monitor and regulate the efforts your body puts forth to maintain balance... we have no issue relying on this subconscious system 100% of the time. Like @[prAna303](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1363/) said, this same system can be relied on for other higher level functions too if we let it. I've experienced similar in my life too, usually in running/biking, but even in driving and other areas on occasion.

The slow motion perspective is an interesting phenomenon, and I believe it is due to the subconscious "taking the wheels" per se on the sensory inputs and sorting through all the nonsense to only deliver the key frames to the conscious mind to use. Like in Peter's example, when running down a hill it isn't relevant to process the details of the 1,000's of rocks, sticks, roots, plants, etc. in the immediate frame, rather only the ones that are relevant to your endeavor and will provide your next footholds.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-23T08:19:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hmmmm-savant-syndrome.10614/post-123142>

> Dr. Darold Treffert ... championed use of the word “savant syndrome” instead of “idiot savant” in 1980.

Idiot has unfortunate connotations nowadays, since it's used as an insult, but I suppose the term did catch the paradoxical nature of the unusual skills some autists have. A savant is actually:

> A learned person, especially a distinguished scientist
>
> early 18th century: French, literally 'knowing (person)', present participle (used as a noun) of _savoir_.

[http://www.oxforddictionaries.com/definition/english/savant](http://www.oxforddictionaries.com/definition/english/savant)

Someone who's able to memorise the _Decline and Fall_ and recite it on request but who has no understanding of what it's about is not a "learned person" and can't really be said to _**know**_ it. The man who was given this to do as a task was disturbed while he was carrying it out. When he resumed reading, he began again at the wrong place, a paragraph behind where he'd already got to. Ever afterwards when reciting Gibbon he would speak that paragraph twice in succession.

There appears to be something unnervingly obsessional and rather "automatic" about some of these skills.

It's not really clear from the Post article what the nature of Padgett's acquired abilities are. One expert in the area speaks of them as showing "genius" but we're also told:

> ... Padgett is not the next Stephen Hawking. This ability allows him to see the world in a unique way — but it’s highly unlikely that his ability will land him a Fields Medal.
>
> “He needs the help of a trained mathematician,” says Chartier.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-04-23T16:35:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hmmmm-savant-syndrome.10614/post-123187>

I just came to an interesting thread on a certain site:
[http://science.slashdot.org/story/0...-magnets-to-turn-off-the-brains-speech-center](http://science.slashdot.org/story/08/05/18/1253258/using-magnets-to-turn-off-the-brains-speech-center)

> An editor for the Telegraph, Roger Highfield, recently volunteered to allow a UK researcher to [shut off the speech center of his brain](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/earth/main.jhtml?view=DETAILS&grid=&xml=/earth/2008/05/16/scibrain216.xml) with a high-powered magnetic pulse. Regular speech is controlled by a section of the brain called Broca's area. Once the precise location is determined in the subject, a magnetic pulse can temporarily disrupt speech without impairing other cognitive functions. The link contains a video in which you can watch Highfield stutter and twitch while attempting to recite a nursery rhyme. A later test shows that he's able to sing the rhyme without difficulty, since singing is controlled in a different part of the brain (as you may remember from [Scott Adams' speech disorder](http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/15446515/)). Researchers believe that the ability to stimulate or quell activity in specific areas of the brain may help in treating conditions like epilepsy and migraine headaches.

If you scroll down the comments, they even talk about a researcher called Allan Snyder who uses TMS to try to invoke these savant-like symptoms.

I'm sure all these things are trivial for a neurosurgeon but I find them awesome and unbelievable, to say the least.

### prAna303 — 2014-04-23T18:14:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hmmmm-savant-syndrome.10614/post-123203>

I think a little bit like this.

A living thing is under constant pressure of information, think about all the ways those waves gets thru you... Somehow we slowly started to get smart (in a bad way) wearing tons of clothes, thick shoes and caps in the summer, we began to loose energy. When you are living in a constant stealing syndrome, the system only works with the things that is about survival, who cares about arts when you are dying? But, quite a lot of information would be stored but ignored.

If or when you get energy, your basics heal and then the sublime levels open up... The things you missed is there once again because you have the power to observe them with all your senses. Is this why the deja vu feeling can creep upon you in sleep?

When you loose energy, you only wake up to big explosions, big things, not the small details... You simple can not "see" them.

I feel alien when i wear to much clothes, i can not read the room, my body is not part of the environment... This could be good but also bad if i am naked in WiFi and iPhone hell. Nature and the elements must be there, you simple can not FAKE what you are made of.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-04-23T18:49:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hmmmm-savant-syndrome.10614/post-123213>

Really interesting thoughts guys!

> [prAna303 said:](/goto/post?id=123203)
>
> I think a little bit like this.
> When you are living in a constant stealing syndrome, the system only works with the things that is about survival, who cares about arts when you are dying? _But, quite a lot of information would be stored but ignored_.

It has been a long time that I realized that I have quite a good memory to store information but not that much accessing it. As a computer science guy, I like to look at it from that perspective because I can see some parallelisms with it. In the computer world things are permanently stored in physical supports like hard drive plates, solid state memories, optical disks, or temporary into RAM chips. Whatever. The thing is that in order to access them, there's the need for some kind of pointer system that can get back that information. For instance, when you get back a file from your hard drive is because there's a partition table that holds the addresses of every single file. If the table ever gets messed up (as when something happens with your PC, like a virus, or when you format a disc to reinstall your operating system) then you loose all information. But, not really. If you didn't overwrite it, there's a chance you could get it back if you scan the whole disc to look for it, and then reconstruct the pointers in a new table. On the other side of things: even if the system is working properly but you have a lot of data, not all that much can be easily accessed because the temporary memory, the RAM, which is faster than the physical, big memory, is limited. You have to keep moving data from one to another, prioritizing which is more important or accessed more times (think [locality of reference](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Locality_of_reference) principle). To speed up things, there are special types of memories, called caches, that are blazing fast volatile memories that try to hold the most accessed data using caching algorithms. That's a reason why PCs can still do some tasks pretty quick because this fine tuning helps keeping up performance.

Why I say all this? I think that the human brain is pretty similar. In fact, I think that computers might be a cheap imitation from our own brain because, well, we designed them in the first place, so it's quote reasonable to think that we did some kind of reverse engineering process based on us to develop machines that do stuff quite fast, but not in the exquisite manner our brain does. Why? because our brain is a quantum computer, but a PC is not, at least not yet, because we don't fully understand quantum principles so much that can port it successfully to build a complete quantum computer, although everyday it seems that there's new progress with it. I think it's the reason why computers cannot solve some quantum problems as well as the human brain like image processing or dealing with problems that require complex behaviour like artificial intelligence. And with regard to storage capacity, the brains seems to be like an almost infinite data center, as if it could perform very high level compression of information, where a computer nowadays can have massive space for storing things like text but not so much for very big data like tons of tons of video, etc.

Getting back to me, I can sense as if I could hold quite a lot of information, which is expected for the human brain, but I struggle a lot to get it back into my "caché". That's why I tend to use some strategies to overcome my limitations like going out for a walk or a run when I want to listen to a podcast, etc. That way, I have the feeling that I build up more powerful "pointers" that help me retain the info I want to retain, willingly, as a rational person. For instance, if I read a blogpost from here in front of the PC, a task that I must share with a lot of other things I do in the PC, I have a harder time retaining it. But, if I hear a podcast while walking into a place I've not been into, or just a few times, I can even recall the exact words from the podcast! That's huge proof to me that the brain can hold a lot of data but the key is to be able to retrieve it. Some people are blessed with the ability to do that, while some other like me suck a little bit with it. I think this all has to do with some things that have been outlied here like how the brain is more prone to learn when we move from an evolutionary perspective, the BDNF thing, the development of the nromal human brain thanks to the cofactors like DHA, EPA, iodine, iron, water, etc... and now the electron density that powers it up.

### Boga — 2017-02-19T15:37:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hmmmm-savant-syndrome.10614/post-208892>

Can Hemoencephalography (HEG) be another method to drive the extraordinary growth?

> **Hemoencephalography** (**HEG**) is a relatively new [neurofeedback](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neurofeedback) technique within the field of neurotherapy. Neurofeedback, a specific form of [biofeedback](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biofeedback), is based on the idea that human beings can consciously alter their brain function through training sessions in which they attempt to change the signal generated by their brain and measured via some neurological feedback mechanism. **By so doing, participants increase cerebral blood flow to a specified region of the brain, consequently increasing brain activity and performance on tasks involving that region of the brain**.[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hemoencephalography#cite_note-Tinius-1)

# Thread: I need more help........to help you better.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-22T03:27:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-more-help-to-help-you-better.10616/post-122889>

I have decided to make drastic changes to the site and how content is delivered. I want to be better than what I was. I am personally, ready for a big change. There are things I think need to be radically changed. But I want your input. I need you to tell me first what is good......what is bad.......and then post your top 3 wants, desires, and needs for the new direction of JackKruse.com

I have hired new people to deliver content in a different way.

I want to blow it all up, honestly.

But I do not want to throw the baby out with the bath water.

So since this place is as much yours as it is mine.........I need to hear from members.

Members input is who I am interested in..........I want to crowdsource your ideas.

Not all will I use. But members are who I am interested in. My plan is make webinars and my time with members more valuable.

My time is very limited and I need to consider how to deliver what I must and limit my computer time.

So leave it here........I will be reviewing it constantly over the next 3 weeks.

I want innovation......I want reality. I listen. I do not want a bitch and moan fest.

I know there are problems. Your emails directly to me have struck a nerve.

It is time to change. Help me help you.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-22T03:43:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-more-help-to-help-you-better.10616/post-122893>

The first change will be the May 2014 webinar. It will be a one hour Q & A. Plan appropriately. Get your questions in early and I will be short and to the point. From here on in Webinars will be limited to one hour Q & A.

The Optimal Reset series was fantastic. I would love more content like that, even if it is in a shorter series. There are so many good subjects left to be covered and things are changing so rapidly.

Thanks for asking for input. Will mull it over and post more later.

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-22T04:01:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-more-help-to-help-you-better.10616/post-122895>

I love the forum. I also love the webinars. Things that will help:

Single log-in. I shouldn't need 4 different log ins to access different content on the site

If I've purchased a content EMF Webinar, Heal Your Hormones bootcamp - I should be able to access that in the same way.

For example When I log in to my "member dashboard" I should be able to see all the programs I've purchased and then be able to access them at will.

So I should be able to see all the EMF Bootcamp Webinars/Q&A and Heal Your Hormones Bootcamp/Q&A on the same page as my Membership content - Silver/Gold/Platinum. If I can't see or access what I've purchased and I have to email support every time I want to watch something - total PITA.
I'm still having problems accessing those two bootcamp, yet I'm supposed to have access to content.

Also I shouldn't have to go check back emails to see if I missed the webinar info.

I love the blog interaction, I also like the Facebook interaction as well. I think Ask Jack has great value - though recently the regular threads have more gold in them than Ask Jack has of recent....

My question to you Jack - is how do you want content delivered? How do you want to interact with your community? What is your goal for how you want us to grow?

For content - are you looking for simple and streamlined? blogs, the forum and monthly webinars and quarterly Q&A's instead of monthly Q&A's or are you looking for something robust that keeps the community engaged? regular blogs, video blogs, monthly webinars and Q&A's, participating on other's Podcasts etc?

For interaction - are you looking for 1-on-1, or maintaining the collective education? How would you like interaction to occur? more on ask Jack? the blog itself, in the forum itself or on FB. Or are you looking for a new medium?
Do you plan to keep using Start Meeting for webinars Q&A? Or are you going to move to something like google hangouts? start meeting has its good and bad components - like privacy and only the people with the # can access the Q&A. Where as google Hangouts - are real time and anyone who looking for a hang out can drop in. (I haven't figured out how to make a large group google hangout private and I think there's a limit on the # of people in a google hangout which might be good if you want a small group)

For starfish growth - do you see a new medium providing value? if so what's that medium? Are you thinking several face to face conferences? I'd love to do one of those... but travel funds are limited right now. But knowing I could potentially have the option 4 x/year to meet you and other forum members somewhere, I'm in.

Gretchen

### yewwei.tan — 2014-04-22T05:24:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-more-help-to-help-you-better.10616/post-122916>

If Q&As are going to be limited to an hour, then we're going to need to clarify the exact goal of such Q&As. Is it to allow for member interaction? For expanding on topics that can't easily be conveyed in words? To talk directly to people about their specific problems?

I think making a Q&A agenda is going to be useful -- which means that Jack and the Community knows roughly what topics the Q&A are going to cover before it starts. At the very least, a set of guidelines should be set up as to what we hope to achieve via the Q&As.

An example would be to treat the Q&A almost like a University Tutorial, whereby Jack would flag certain topics and questions from various sources (forum, blog, facebook, etc ...), and then compile that into a document to be distributed to members somehow. This would require the community to actively post questions before the scheduled Q&A, instead of with-holding questions to the Q&A session. This may also prevent certain personal health related questions from being asked in the lead-up to a Q&A.

One way to have a shareable document is just to use a collaboratively edited Google Docs document. It will be "shared publicly", but the link will only be made known to members. Edit permissions will be given to moderators, while the rest of the community can comment on the document to aid in steering the direction of the discussion. This solution is free ($$ wise), and requires that members have a Google Account should they want to comment on the document. The end result should be a nice little summary document of the Q&A.

Of course, Google Docs doesn't have to be the solution, and any other means of distributing a digital document file containing summary information from the Q&A would suffice.

\---

Regarding scheduling, webinars should be released at least 7 days before the scheduled Q&A if possible, so that mortals like me can digest the material. Having some lag time would also allow facilitate the measures I suggested above.

\---

On a selfish note, I'd like the blogs to be more readable. The blogs are dense, and oftentimes its the simple 2-3 line sentences that set the context for the proceeding paragraph. The order of information is also important, and sometimes the reader is left with too many disparate threads that. I also personally like the 1-idea-1-paragraph rule.

I personally think that having an editor would be helpful, whether they be professional or amateur (like asking your son to proofread the blogs for you).

I know this is an unreasonable and vague request, but I'd be willing to help if possible.

### Josh — 2014-04-22T06:07:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-more-help-to-help-you-better.10616/post-122941>

As I have tried to do in some places sometimes, a facilitator or manager of the forum could move stuff around into topical areas so that the development of ideas on a theme can be followed and built upon.

Another option is to simply limit the treads to ones you approve or start

Often there is a lot of good dialogue that crosses over from thread to thread and it is hard to stay with it.

Sometimes it starts again and repeats because someone could not pick up the original start of discussion on a given topic.

One idea would be to archive threads more frequently and close them. Just keep threads relevant to the current blogs/webinars open to focus discussion.

More rules about content and or more vigorous editing by administrators is another possibility.

Perhaps allow each member to continue their personal musings, documentation of their journey, etc. on their optimal journal. Otherwise limit participation to threads topical to what is currently being worked through.

**_Hell, just make the blog the Quilt with a thread for each square and everyone has to start an optimal journal to join. How much they want to post is their business. The focus is on building the conceptual understanding and fluency with the Quilt. That would give us visual thinkers something to hang our ideas and understandings upon that stayed constant......what do they call it in the memory world, a "memory palace" I believe....._**

### Josh — 2014-04-22T06:34:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-more-help-to-help-you-better.10616/post-122947>

For your amusement....

> There lived a sage constantly traveling in that region of the Himalayas where this Valley of Flowers exists. I knew him well. He was very strong and healthy, about eighty years of age, but quite unusual. He used to carry a unique blanket all the time. It was very heavy. The weight of this blanket was approximately eighty to one hundred pounds. You might wonder how he made this blanket so heavy. Any piece of cloth which he found during his wide travels, he would patch onto the blanket. It was a blanket of a thousand patches. He called it gudari, which means “blanket of patches,” and people called him Gudari Baba. On my request he said, “If you would really like to see the Valley of Flowers and want to follow me, you will have to carry this blanket.” I agreed, but when I put the blanket on my shoulders I stumbled under its weight. He asked, “How is it possible for a young man like you to be so weak when you are apparently so healthy?” He picked up the blanket and said, “See how light it is?” Then he put it on my shoulder again. He knew my master and so he allowed me to follow him to the Valley of Flowers.
>
> “No one can retain his memory when he goes through the Valley of Flowers during the blooming season. We should bring all the obstinate kids like you here and set them right. Those who try to be intellectual and argue with us should be brought here so that they understand their worth.” I said, “But I am following you.” He said, “Oh yes. You argue all the time and don’t listen attentively. You are very proud of your intellectual knowledge. I do not know how to read and write. You are more educated than I. You have education, but I have control of mind.”
>
> I told him, “I also have control.” He replied, “We shall see.”
>
> I said, “Sir, first of all, please take away your blanket from my shoulders because it is difficult to carry.” He lamented, “Oh, the children of this modern age!” He took his blanket from me and started conversing with it: “O my beloved blanket, nobody understands anything about you. No one knows that you are a living blanket.” I looked at him and thought, “This man is really crazy!”
>
> The next morning a Japanese monk joined us. He was equally anxious to see the Valley of Flowers. This Japanese monk also thought that Gudari Baba was a crazy man. He asked me, “Rama, can you explain why this man is carrying such a heavy load?” We started talking and I thought it would be nice to share these experiences with each other. This monk was afraid of going to the Valley of Flowers all alone. Someone had told him that if any traveler goes to see this valley, he forgets everything and his senses do not coordinate in perceiving sense objects. The traveler loses his memory and smiles all the time. He said that this baba was the right person to guide us because he traveled in this region and knew all the trails. The next day this Japanese monk started shivering with fever. He had lived in the jungles of Burma and had suffered from malaria. He had a temperature of 103 to 104 degrees and his pulse rate was very high. The baba said to him, “You told this boy that I was crazy. Do you want to see the living power of my blanket? Do you know that this blanket is not a mere blanket, but a living force? Do you want to get well? Then kneel down and be humble!” The baba covered the Japanese monk with the blanket. The monk said, “I will be flattened! It’s too heavy and I am a small man.” The baba said, “Keep quiet!” After a few minutes he took the blanket away from the monk. When he removed the blanket, it was shivering. The baba asked the monk, “What happened to your fever?” He said, “Sir, I don’t have a fever anymore.” The baba said, “This blanket is very generous and kind and has taken away your fever.” The baba looked at me and said, “Do you want his fever to be cured forever?” I said, “Yes, please.” The baba said, “But he calls me crazy. I don’t think he deserves my help.” I said, “The sages are kind and great and they always forgive others.” The baba smiled and said, “Of course I will help him.” We traveled together for fifteen days and the Japanese monk did not suffer from the fever again.
>
> Swami Rama (2009-10-03). Living With the Himalayan Masters (pp. 128-131). Himalayan Institute Press. Kindle Edition.
> .

### yewwei.tan — 2014-04-22T07:57:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-more-help-to-help-you-better.10616/post-122952>

**Via Negativa**

This is one of the best lessons I learnt from Nassim Nicholas Taleb, namely that being wise is first and foremost about knowing what NOT to do, rather than trying to focus on what "solutions" to take.

So much of health today is about avoiding the stressors of modern life that seek to destroy our body's ability to be healthy.

Examples:

-   don't eat wheat because bromide is a dielectric blocker
-   try not to sleep on the magnetico during the day
-   Jude had to figure out the hard way that man-made Iodine isn't good for you (hyperthyroidism and atrial fibrillation) -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/the-iodine-thread.943/page-58#post-122719](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/the-iodine-thread.943/page-58#post-122719)

That last example is illustrative of the kinds of scenarios where a member of this community could have benefitted greatly from knowing what is in the current blogs. This is not asking you to reveal stuff that's coming down the pipeline, but if possible, to issue warnings against actions that you see which are blatantly wrong and harmful to the person doing them.

For example, you've mentioned many times before that iodine should be taken in it's evolutionary package, but for some reason the point never seemed to sink it. An alternative approach could have been to say something like:

"Iodine should be eaten in its natural form, or else there can be serious negative consequences. If <some\_list\_of\_symptoms> happens to you, then you need to back off fake man-made iodine big time. You'll see why in <future\_blog\_name>"​

Perhaps that sort of warning would have fell on deaf ears at the conception of this site, but I think your members know by now to take you seriously when you say that something can be really bad, and trust that the explanation for why is coming down the pipeline.

\----

This shouldn't take that much time on your part, since it's the little nuggets that can make a big impact. Here's an example of what I mean.

I asked a question about Sun exposure and Skin Blushing a couple days ago -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/sun-exposure-and-skin-blushing.10569/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/sun-exposure-and-skin-blushing.10569/) . The response was that the answers would be within the June webinar. I'm totally fine with that answer, since I can afford to wait, but another way answer it would be something like one of the following:

-   "Good question, skin blushing after sun exposure is a good sign, contrary to what CW would lead you to think. The June Webinar will tell you why."
-   "Getting red skin tells me that your redox potential is screwed, I wouldn't stay in the sun for too long if I were you. The June Webinar will give you the answers."

(_Those are answers that I just made up by the way, not actual answers to my question_)

In other words, you don't need to tell us why some course of action is bad, just that it is bad (hints are obviously welcome though ); you can leave breadcrumbs that are actually useful to people's lives in the present, stimulate your more eager members to start pondering the mechanism of action, while also not revealing your cards until you decide to.

\----

Life is lived by heuristics -- simple rules that we hold near to our head and heart, and that resonate (literally) with the situation that we're currently dealing with, and provide us with the intuition needed to avoid getting harmed.

​

Unfortunately, the toxicity of data increases faster than its benefits (Taleb's quote). We're being inundated by all sorts of sensory input today, and Gangrad pointed out certain pitfalls in trying to "listen to the doctor in your head" (to which I replied -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...nar-with-dave-asprey.10486/page-4#post-120887](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/todays-webinar-with-dave-asprey.10486/page-4#post-120887))

Some slight tweaks to the way that you respond to topics on this forum can greatly help us cut through the fog.

### BJK77 — 2014-04-22T16:48:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-more-help-to-help-you-better.10616/post-123027>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=122952)
>
> **Via Negativa**
>
> This is one of the best lessons I learnt from Nassim Nicholas Taleb, namely that being wise is first and foremost about knowing what NOT to do, rather than trying to focus on what "solutions" to take.
>
> So much of health today is about avoiding the stressors of modern life that seek to destroy our body's ability to be healthy.
>
> Examples:
>
> -   don't eat wheat because bromide is a dielectric blocker
> -   try not to sleep on the magnetico during the day
> -   Jude had to figure out the hard way that man-made Iodine isn't good for you (hyperthyroidism and atrial fibrillation) -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/the-iodine-thread.943/page-58#post-122719](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/the-iodine-thread.943/page-58#post-122719)
>
> That last example is illustrative of the kinds of scenarios where a member of this community could have benefitted greatly from knowing what is in the current blogs. This is not asking you to reveal stuff that's coming down the pipeline, but if possible, to issue warnings against actions that you see which are blatantly wrong and harmful to the person doing them.
>
> For example, you've mentioned many times before that iodine should be taken in it's evolutionary package, but for some reason the point never seemed to sink it. An alternative approach could have been to say something like:
>
>
> "Iodine should be eaten in its natural form, or else there can be serious negative consequences. If <some\_list\_of\_symptoms> happens to you, then you need to back off fake man-made iodine big time. You'll see why in <future\_blog\_name>"​
>
>
> Perhaps that sort of warning would have fell on deaf ears at the conception of this site, but I think your members know by now to take you seriously when you say that something can be really bad, and trust that the explanation for why is coming down the pipeline.
>
> \----
>
> This shouldn't take that much time on your part, since it's the little nuggets that can make a big impact. Here's an example of what I mean.
>
> I asked a question about Sun exposure and Skin Blushing a couple days ago -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/sun-exposure-and-skin-blushing.10569/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/sun-exposure-and-skin-blushing.10569/) . The response was that the answers would be within the June webinar. I'm totally fine with that answer, since I can afford to wait, but another way answer it would be something like one of the following:
>
> -   "Good question, skin blushing after sun exposure is a good sign, contrary to what CW would lead you to think. The June Webinar will tell you why."
> -   "Getting red skin tells me that your redox potential is screwed, I wouldn't stay in the sun for too long if I were you. The June Webinar will give you the answers."
>
> (_Those are answers that I just made up by the way, not actual answers to my question_)
>
> In other words, you don't need to tell us why some course of action is bad, just that it is bad (hints are obviously welcome though ); you can leave breadcrumbs that are actually useful to people's lives in the present, stimulate your more eager members to start pondering the mechanism of action, while also not revealing your cards until you decide to.
>
> \----
>
> Life is lived by heuristics -- simple rules that we hold near to our head and heart, and that resonate (literally) with the situation that we're currently dealing with, and provide us with the intuition needed to avoid getting harmed.
>
> ​
>
> Unfortunately, the toxicity of data increases faster than its benefits (Taleb's quote). We're being inundated by all sorts of sensory input today, and Gangrad pointed out certain pitfalls in trying to "listen to the doctor in your head" (to which I replied -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...nar-with-dave-asprey.10486/page-4#post-120887](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/todays-webinar-with-dave-asprey.10486/page-4#post-120887))
>
> Some slight tweaks to the way that you respond to topics on this forum can greatly help us cut through the fog.

I really like your ideas Yew.

I find that in order to practice what I'm learning on here I really need to limit my computer time. Combine that with my emf sensitivity and my inflamed brain right now and I can't always digest the "whys." For many sick people, it would be nice to just have the "whats" for now - maybe with links to longer "whys" that we can go back to as we get well and find our brains being better able to absorb and comprehend the scientific background.

### Martin — 2014-04-22T18:01:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-more-help-to-help-you-better.10616/post-123042>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=122896)
>
> Watch at least one of Neil Tyson's "Inexplicable Universe" the economy and balance of words and images is gentle and effective, but not really simple or dumbed down. I am not saying that you should emulate this, it is just what has impressed me recently.
>
> Consider Powerpoints to contrast the dense text. Perhaps someone could take a text blog and break it into a powerpoint with supporting images as a foundation for each series of blogs. Something of an outline with key concepts broken out. The links are great and they take away from the flow.
>
> More when it comes.....

I just caught that first Neil Tyson show and loved how it did so much with so little. Give newbies (and some oldies) a broad, all-encompassing concept first, say, "How Light Affects Your Health", or, "Cold Thermogenisis, Your Primodial Condition?" and, Epigenetics: How You Change Your Genome Everyday" and do it like Josh above described - "economy and balance of words and images is gentle and effective, but not really simple or dumbed down".

Bring them into a world that they perhaps have not considered because of the conventional wisdom they've been exposed to all of their life. Take that "wisdom" and blow it up, show us why it ain't so by stating briefly what Man has done, and what Nature does. Boom. Then take them on a deeper journey into the science that backs it up.

Your video preface on one blog was very helpful, cuz when you talk, you get pithy and give good, real-world examples with a very helpful accent on important aspects of complicated details. I would like to see you run thru the journey of a banana electron inside our bodies in the middle of winter. I know, you've said it a lot in the blogs and I get it, but I've been here every day for three years. I'm talking about an intro piece, that whets our appetite, then links to your already-written blogs for the "why's" that give us certainty about using this as our straight edge.

I look for your pithy quotes in blogs, and especially podcasts, that I could deliver to someone I'm in touch with whose interested in their health and just asked, "How does that CT stuff work?" or, "How does EMF screw me up?" I need to distill it down to a sentence or two that is so damn pithy that it **grabs their attention and interest**. The first sentence of a paragraph sets the whole thing up, and it should grab you and spur you on.

Dates are important in the blogs, but since you started with the mitochondrial info and had to punt because we were not getting it, another way to broadly introduce your findings is perhaps needed.

### NeilBB — 2014-04-22T18:19:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-more-help-to-help-you-better.10616/post-123045>

Jack,

More than most people here, I think I know the path it has taken for you to get where you are now, and I think that in view of that, I say that you are more than generous with your time as it is. You charge very little money for the quality of what you provide, you essentially give a ton away. Like Josh, I like things the way they are and think the message is so much more important than the format that I don't really care much how you choose to distribute it or mind some occasional technical glitches. There are always going to be complainers and naysayers, and I think its dangerous to give those types too much input. You have to do what you want to do. That said, since you want change, these are my personal suggestions:

1\. I enjoy the forums as much as anyone, but they also disturb me a bit. There is a psychological addiction aspect to the act of checking forums all times of day or night to see what people or saying, that seems to be irresistible. Even though the content here is worlds better, in some ways its no different from using FB or Twitter. The internet has psychological drawbacks over and above the EMF-related ones in my opinion and I am far from a Luddite myself. Perhaps its inevitable today, but we _use the internet to criticize the internet_ a lot here. I don't really have the solution for that, except perhaps a tighter time-restricted structure in some way...

2\. There needs to be some sort of accelerated track for advanced health care professionals and technical scientists. If I am going to ever be in a position to challenge the academics at my institution, even in a small way, I need a lot more ammunition and a deeper more systematic and comprehensive understanding, and I need it fast. I feel like I know a lot but it is very fragmented and missing important details and that makes me somewhat under-armed in challenging my arrogant academic colleagues. I need to be really really sharp in this environment. I can't just point people to websites--that won't work here.

I do like your measured gradual approach that encourages people to anticipate and slowly build knowledge, but we do not all learn at the same rate or in the same way. And people's objectives here are different. Many are here really just to improve their personal health and are less interested in scientific details. While I agree that an understanding of some science is necessary for any patient today to navigate the difficult landscape of health and medicine, I don't think they necessarily need as many technical details as **for-example I do**, to do what I want to do. I agree that formal education is a barrier to outside the box thinking and that that limits the number of people who are both advanced scientifically and able to digest this information. I don't know how many other people like that there are, particularly ones that are willing to participate.

3\. Your tone is often a bit argumentative, which I personally have no problem with, but some might. Your hardcore supporters probably don't need to be constantly reminded that simple paleo is short-sighted for example--they know that very well by now. And the skeptics here may never be convinced. So I think sometimes different audiences require different approaches. That is tough when you don't or can't delineate your audiences. For example my discussions with patients are very different from my teaching with medical students which is different from teaching fellows, for example. What would be insultingly simple to an epilepsy fellow would fly over the head of most patients. The material on your website is very sophisticated and there are going to always be major gaps in the way different people receive it. Perhaps consider dividing members into 2 or 3 different groups based on level of scientfic sophistication and/or interest level and objectives.

4\. I think I know why your internet posts became fragmented. On jackkruse.com you are in control of things, building knowledge and to some extent "preaching to a mostly supportive choir." On FB, twitter, and other blogs you are participating in more contentious scientific debates with "peers" and probably attempting to convince and maybe even recruit intelligent scientifically-minded converts from a larger pool. That makes sense but it also creates a lot of internet time and a lot of fragmentation of message.

5\. Frankly, no matter how optimized you are, I have no clue how you are able to do all the things you do, and still practice neurosurgery and live your life. I suspect its too much and can't help but adversely affect you in some way. I am glad you are seeking to limit and streamline your time on internet for that reason alone...

### Dali Dula — 2014-04-22T18:19:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-more-help-to-help-you-better.10616/post-123046>

I often refer people to the site and they tell me they could not get anywhere with it. I came to this site two and a half years ago and it quite possibly saved my life. The site was exactly what I needed at the time. I crave actionable measures and the actionable was there and continued to be rolled out. I have put the protocols to work and reaped the benefits.
Newbies coming to this site are driving 80 miles an hour into a concrete wall.
This site is deep. The science is mind boggling and time consuming. I love it, but I grew with it. It's like throwing someone who never played baseball into a World Series Game.
Present the protocols and Rxs in a more accessible form then link to science for the curious.
I know Jack would like us to realize much for ourselves, and this is good, but it takes a certain kind of motivated individual to dig deep.
I love the science, but people looking to regain wellness are not interested in the sub atomic activity in their nanotubes. They want to feel better. The home page should have unique button links to presentations of the Rxs

The forum is fine. It's a forum and works well.

I would like to have access permanently to any content issued under my membership. I feel I have paid for it and should be able to view it again at any time. The Q/As should also be available upon demand. My schedule rarely allows me to participate.

There is so much content on this site. I am a motivated member and I do not have time to utilize all this info.

I am a whore for actionable information. Looking for the next n-1.

To be a Kruser one must develop fundamentals before diving in.

Make the fundamentals accessible.
In all the disciplines in life fundamentals are emphasized. Deep practice does not happen without solid fundamentals.

There is no one else putting out this interpretation of life and it is a precious resource, thanks Jack

### caroline — 2014-04-22T18:27:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-more-help-to-help-you-better.10616/post-123047>

When Nick pm'd me his summation ....I thought - WOW ...that is so great - we really need this. And it turned out tha everyone really appreciated it, thanks Nick!

As others have said ....there is so much right here! It is hard to keep up with the forum. - but I think that is what has made this a great community. Take for instance - the dress thread ...it started as such a simple thing but look where it took us....

I think newbies are going to feel a little intimidated - I did .... But that is kind of part of the growing and learning....and if we want the information we have to suck it up and get involved. I think it is part of our job as old timers to help the new people. It helps me too - I have to go back and check things to make sure I tell them the right stuff - and often there are things I have forgotten.

Dr. K. - you often follow a thread and see where we are stumbling ....like the donut hole thread ....you gave us huge amounts of stuff there - in tems we could somewhat understand!

I think I often learn more from the threads on the forum - time consuming -,yes .....but this is hugely important to me.

This is such a terrific community now - it has evolved massively - and - so have you Dr K. Not long ago I listened to your first webinar....you have gotten so much better - or I think now you are being you?

### Albert83BCN — 2014-04-22T20:19:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-more-help-to-help-you-better.10616/post-123060>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=123006)
>
> I think a date index of the blog is critical too.......I ve always thought that. I absolutely hate that the old index went away. My fault I did not put up a bigger stink about it.

I also loved the date index. In fact, I thought that you decided to get rid of it for some reason but now that I see your frustration, I wonder... why did it need to be removed? A technical issue? I can see that all posts still maintain their original date so... why not to rebuild a new index? Archives are sometimes a feature given with certain themes and not on others, on Wordpress. But, if your theme doesn't have it, there are plugins that allow to have an archive section, so it should be easy to get a new one. Here's some random posts I found on a quick search on the net with some info for you to digg in, in case you want to. I think your technical team shouldn't have much of a problem implementing something like this:

[https://www.iacquire.com/blog/13-wordpress-plugins-to-organize-and-sort-your-content](https://www.iacquire.com/blog/13-wordpress-plugins-to-organize-and-sort-your-content)
[http://www.wpuniversity.com/wordpress-tips/the-top-5-plugins-manage-your-pages-and-post-order/](http://www.wpuniversity.com/wordpress-tips/the-top-5-plugins-manage-your-pages-and-post-order/)
[http://wordpress.org/plugins/smart-archives-reloaded/screenshots/](http://wordpress.org/plugins/smart-archives-reloaded/screenshots/)

I'm still at 2012 regarding with the blogposts and although I've heard almost all free podcasts existing to date and I think I can somehow handle the latests series, I still love to read it in a chronological order so I can get the feeling of how the blog evolved from 2011 till now. I hope you can get it back!

Some more things. I personally think that the posts are a little bit hard to deal with from a visual perspective. I know it's a pain in the ass first hand but, to see some related images in each paragraph would make it a lot more easy to digest. I know that the great information laid down is the big deal here and the biggest value but, still, a few images would help making things easier, it could help people understand the ideas more easily. I say this because I myself I'm quite dense and need a lot of visual stimulation to understand things. That's a reason why when I see long blogpost with a lot of text but no images tend to avoid it. Yours are amongst the only ones I push through even if they don't fell appealign to get inmersed with as I prioritize the critical info I know I'll get away with. But some new reader won't have that background to decide to stay. Although it might be counter-intuitive, if your goal is to reach people I think it's a good thing to consider. I've seem some of the videos that have been recently posted on the site and I love the idea. That's where I'd like to see the future of the site, but I also realize that doing videos is very resource consuming and can only be done occasionally. I think the blogposts could still do the job with a few hacks like these.

I've also wondered many times if every blog you did could have been a podcast, that would certainly make my life easier as I cannot afford to invest all the time I would reading them. Hearing to the podcasts it's easier to some people as you can squeeze some time when you're travelling to listen to them. Well, turns out that there are some plugins that allow to turn a blogpost into a podcast or at least do real time text-to-speech ([http://www.wpjedi.com/convert-wordpress-posts-to-audio-3-text-to-speech-plugins-tools/](http://www.wpjedi.com/convert-wordpress-posts-to-audio-3-text-to-speech-plugins-tools/)). I've never played with them and I'm not sure they'd be a good idea once implemented to tell the truth, but maybe it's something worth giving a try. I get that putting a synthesized voice over your ideas would suck big time and I know you're very into the idea of getting the 3rd dimension from people that is lacking on the net but... again, as a user I think it could be really useful to me so probably to some others as well.

Well, just some random ideas I got right now. If I can think about something more I'll let you know!

### Marie — 2014-04-23T01:10:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-more-help-to-help-you-better.10616/post-123094>

You need to contextualize the process of disease and allow people to easily see where they are in the process. Picture a 5x5 grid with specific disease stations NAMED in them, much the same way the MBTI grid works to define personality. This gives the readers a visual anchor and a direction for their healing process. You should be able to classify 80% of the population's location using this grid, and using 5-7 data points/inputs. Don't use more than 7 data points, as that's the point where learning to distinguish between differences disintegrates. (Picture the difference between a 5x5 grid with 25 types and a 7x7 grid with 49 types. It becomes too difficult to navigate as the classifications increase.)

Each box in the grid would have the following:
A Name/Archetype
5-7 Expected lab results that identify this "type"
Likely specific Symptoms
Rx priorities
Links to more info (This leads to a supporting page for this type)

The vast majority of the population cannot deal with ambiguity, so allowing people to quickly see which bucket they fall into, and how to move into a "better" bucket through specific actions. This is very motivating. Plus there needs to be more visual anchors, in general, on the site. If each box on the grid feeds into specific information, courses, or even forums, it will personalize the experience while building contextual knowledge that naturally links to other boxes. New people may be at first focused on their own "type", but will naturally progress through learning the types as they learn more about the entire system.

There may be specific points on the Quilt that apply more for one archetype than the other, so building resonance early on leads to community, which leads to retention (of both knowledge and customers).

For the long run, you need to figure out how to build something that monetizes the system you've developed and doesn't rely, or minimally relies, on your input/time. The way the website currently works is a recipe for burnout. NO ONE should work more than 20 hours a week, IMHO. We should all have more time for our families, for the arts, and for pursuing challenges.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-04-23T02:31:52+0300

Caroline: I know what you mean about the meanings of words or acynonmys. Like for quite a while I thought QED might stand for "Quad Erat Demonstandum" which is Latin and means "Thus it is Proved" It was what we used to put at the end of one of Euclid's geometry proofs. I was pretty sure it was NOT that but only recently I realize it stands for "Quantum Electro Dynamics" which now make all the sense in the world.

I don't really have a suggestion for Dr K but wonder how he does as much as he does. Personally I value the blogs and webinars very much and would be happy with just that, I wonder if responding here is the best use of his time.
